TdJd (jack-ten suited) is a very playable, high-equity hand in a 4-handed game — good straight and flush potential and strong top-pair possibilities. I want to take the initiative UTG instead of limping, both to build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop and to apply fold equity against the blinds. The table’s standard open sizing has been ~3xBB, so I’ll raise to 300 total to balance my range, isolate the short-stacked button (Claude, ~17BB) who may shove (TJs is call-worthy vs a shove), and keep the pot manageable against the deeper SB/BB callers. Action: raise to 300.

Folding 8s7c (weak suited connector) from the button. While I have positional advantage, several factors make this a clear fold: (1) 8s7c is a marginal hand that needs significant improvement to win, (2) Minni's UTG raise signals strength, (3) I'm the shortest stack at the table with only 1700 chips - I need to preserve my stack and wait for premium hands, (4) Even if I'm on the button with good position, calling 300 with weak holdings erodes my chip lead unnecessarily. With my stack size, I should be tightening my range and playing high-value hands. Better spots will come.

AJo offsuit is a strong hand (top ~8-10% range), with good equity against an UTG opening range (~42-45% vs typical 10-12% range). Minni has shown aggression in previous hands with a variety of holdings, suggesting a somewhat wide UTG range. In the SB, facing a single raise and fold, this is a standard 3-bet spot for value and to isolate/put pressure, especially as the BB (Flash) folds frequently to raises in history. Post-flop OOP is a concern, but the hand plays well multiway or heads-up, and ace blocker helps vs AA/AK. 3-bet sizing to 900 (3x the open) is standard, building the pot while maintaining fold equity. Folding is too tight, calling risks multiway pots OOP with a marginal ace that can be dominated.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 4c. Minni raised from UTG and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind. 9-4 suited is a very weak hand and does not have the equity or playability to justify calling a 3-bet, especially when facing an UTG open and a Small Blind raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

T♥J♦ suited is a very playable hand versus a single 3‑bettor from the small blind. Elon’s 900 is a standard 3‑bet size; his range will be value‑heavy but will include enough broadway and suited hands that TJs has reasonable equity and good playability postflop. Pot odds: I must call 600 into a 1300 pot (600/(1300+600) ≈ 31.6% break‑even), and TJs vs a typical SB 3‑betting range is around or above that, especially with backdoor diamond/straight potential and strong two‑pair/straight outs if I connect. I’m deep enough to realize implied odds when I hit, and a min 4‑bet to 1500 here would be too small to credibly fold out many hands and too large to turn my hand into a pure fold equity bluff — it’d also commit us to difficult postflop spots OOP. Plan: call 600 (900 total) and play postflop — continue on favorable boards (pair, picks, good draws), fold to heavy resistance on dry boards where I have no equity.

Heads-up pot of 1900 on a Qc5d2d flop, first to act OOP as the preflop 3-bettor with AsJh. This board misses my hand directly (ace-high, no pair/draw), but As blocks strong Ax hands like AA/AQ/AK, and J is somewhat live. My 3-bet range (QQ+, AKs/AQo+, AJs) hits this board decently often with top pair (AQ/AK often have diamond blocker but still), sets, etc., while giving me fold equity against Minni's calling range (which includes some speculative suited connectors, broadway, pockets that missed). Minni's history shows aggression but also folding to postflop bets (e.g., Hand 10 she bet big but opponent folded river; Hand 12 called but lost). Board has a diamond flush draw, but c-betting 2/3 pot (~1200) charges it, denies equity to her floats/dominating draws (KQ/KJ/QJ less likely in her 3-bet call range), and builds pot OOP with showdown value or as semi-bluff. Checking risks giving free cards to her draws/OESD potential and cedes initiative; betting maintains range advantage. Sizing to 1200 (63% pot) balances value/protection/fold equity without overcommitting my 7k stack. With 150-hand goal, exploiting 3-bet range perception vs Minni's wide tendencies is +EV.

Elon 3‑bet preflop so his range is strong, but on Qc‑5d‑2d his small flop stab to 1,200 into a 3,100 pot is fairly small — he’s trying to price draws in and will have a mix of Qx, broadway hands and some bluffs. I hold TdJd: two diamonds in hand plus two diamonds on the board gives me the nut diamond draw (9 outs) and two overcards. Calling 1,200 into 3,100 requires ≈28% equity. My combined equity (flush draw + pair‑outs on runout) versus his likely range is comfortably around or above that, so a call is +EV. Raising here to the min (2,400) is a thin semi‑bluff against a 3‑bettor and bloats the pot with a drawing hand; I prefer to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call the 1,200; if I hit a diamond or pair I’ll extract value, if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I’ll fold, and if the turn checks I’ll consider barreling as a semi‑bluff or betting for value depending on the card.

Heads-up on turn OOP with AsJh (ace-high, no pair/draw) on 5d Qc 2d 8c board vs Minni's deep stack. Preflop as 3-bettor and flop c-bet (semi-bluff/value), she called both, representing strength: likely top pair (AQ/AK/KQ), sets (55/22/QQ), flush draws (diamond/club now possible), or two-pair combos. Board is dry-ish but flush draws active; turn 8c adds club draw without helping me. My range blocks AA/AQ/AK but continues with strong hands (sets, Qx, overpairs QQ+), giving bluff equity, but with 5800 stack into 4300 pot (SPR ~1.35), barreling commits ~40% stack for thin value/no equity, high risk if she shoves (folds crushed). Checking preserves stack, realizes equity with ace-high showdown (beats bluffs/draws/A-high), induces bluffs from her missed draws, and avoids bloating pot OOP. History shows Minni calls big but folds to aggression sometimes (e.g., Hand 3,11); check exploits her potential weakness while maximizing fold equity later if needed. Pot control key with marginal hand this deep in.

I hold TdJd — nut diamond draw plus a runout straight to any 9. There are 9 diamond outs + 3 non-diamond 9s = 12 unique outs (~26% to hit the river). Elon 3‑bet pre and led flop but checked the turn, which smells like pot control/weak Qx or a missed hand. This is a classic semi‑bluff spot: betting can take the pot now and still leaves me plenty of equity if called. The pot is 4,300; I’ll bet ~1/3–35% pot to apply pressure but keep the price reasonable if called. Bet 1,500 is a size that folds out many worse broadways and small pairs, yet is <~2,346 which keeps the line +EV even if he always calls. If Elon check‑raises large I’ll treat it as strong and likely release the draw; if he calls I’m happy to see a free river with 26% equity and will re-evaluate on the river.

Facing a turn donk bet of 1500 into a 4300 pot (pot now 5800, odds ~3.9:1) OOP with AsJh (ace-high, no pair/draw) on a 5d Qc 2d 8c board. Preflop 3-bet and flop c-bet polarized my range toward value (QQ+, AK/AQ, sets) or strong semi-bluffs; Minni's call-call now followed by aggressive donk (after my check) strongly reps Qx (AQ/KQ), two-pair, sets, or completed flush draws (clubs/diamonds possible in her calling range). Board texture favors her range heavily: flush draws opened up, straight draws unlikely, overcards blocked by my As but she likely has showdown value. Calling leaves 4300 into ~7300 pot (SPR ~1.7), pot-committed but with zero equity/draw/showdown vs her value-heavy betting range; thin value from beating pure bluffs unlikely given her history of big bets with showdown (e.g., Hands 3,10,12 where she bets rivers/turns with made hands). Raising as a bluff suicidal OOP with low fold equity vs her strength and deep stacks. Pot control check exploited her potential weakness but she fired, confirming strength; folding preserves ~5800 stack for better spots in 150-hand game. Minni's aggression exploitable long-term but not here with marginal ace-high crushed.

I'm UTG with KJ of diamonds, a premium hand. This is a strong raising hand from early position. My stack is 1700 (17 big blinds), which is moderate. KJ of diamonds has both high card strength and flush potential. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here—it shows strength, builds the pot with a quality hand, and is not overcommitting given my stack size. This should win the pot preflop a reasonable percentage of the time or get me good value when called.

Qh8h is a strong speculative suited hand on the button with excellent position. Calling the UTG raise from short-stacked Claude (14BB) offers good pot odds: 300 to call into 450 pot (pot will be 750). The hand plays well postflop with flush and straight potential, good implied odds against deeper stacks behind, and position to control the pot. SB and BB may call, creating multiway pot where suited connectors excel. Folding is too tight; raising risks commitment vs short stack jam without domination. Plan: realize equity on favorable flops, fold to aggression on bad boards.

I am in the Small Blind with K6o. Claude, who is the short stack with only 1400 total, has raised to 300 from UTG. Elon called on the button. K6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a UTG raiser, especially since Claude's stack size suggests he is committed to this hand and might be looking to get his remaining chips in soon. While the price to call is 250 into a pot of 750 (3:1 odds), the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a clear fold. I want to preserve my stack and wait for better spots.

I'm in the big blind with Js3s facing an UTG raise to 300 and a button call (multiway). This hand is marginal — suited but dominated by many of the opponents' likely ranges. 3-betting is unattractive: I'm out of position to the button, I have poor blockers, and Claude is short-stacked (1400) and could shove, which I don't want to gamble with on a bluff. Calling is cheap: I need to put in 200 more (total 300) into a ~750 pot, roughly 21% pot-odds to continue and see a flop with backdoor spade and pairing potential. Plan postflop: proceed only with clear equity (pair, good spade draw, strong draws); on dry, high-card boards I will check-fold to significant aggression to protect the stack. Therefore I defend the blind with a call.

Check. I flopped a marginal made hand — bottom pair (3s) with a weak kicker and only a runner-runner spade possibility. This is a three-way pot against an UTG raiser (short-stacked Claude) and a loose button caller; leading here gains very little fold equity and only invites raises that I don’t want to face with such a vulnerable hand. I’ll control the pot, see a free turn, and re-evaluate: call small to medium bets to realize equity or fold to significant aggression unless I improve to two pair/trips or pick up a strong draw. If the street is checked through I’ll consider a small turn stab only with a favorable card or if I see clear fold equity; otherwise I’ll protect the stack and proceed cautiously.

Flop: 3c 9s 2h. I hold KJ diamonds with strong equity on this dry flop. Minni checked to me after I c-bet preflop. My hand benefits significantly from this board texture—I have high card strength (K-J high), a flush draw (4 diamonds), and straight potential (any Q or T helps). With 1400 chips (14 big blinds), I'm in a critical stack zone and need to maximize value with strong hands while managing my chip commitments carefully. A bet of 600 (roughly 2/3 of the current 950-chip pot) is appropriate here: it builds the pot for a strong hand, applies pressure to weaker holdings, and protects against draws. This leaves 800 chips behind for future streets, maintaining good flexibility. The flop is unlikely to have hit Elon or Minni hard given their checked/called preflop ranges, making this a natural spot to establish aggression and build the pot with equity.

Preflop call with Qh8h from the button was standard speculative play for position, multiway potential, and suited connector playability. However, the flop of 3c9s2h completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot would require 10 for QJT but no), only a weak backdoor flush draw with one heart on board. Claude's large bet of 600 into 1550 pot (~39% pot, but he has only ~200 behind with 800 stack) from the preflop raiser position screams strength: overpair, 9x, set, or strong draw. Pot odds are ~3.58:1 (600 to win 2150), requiring ~22% equity, but my queen-high has minimal showdown value and little equity against his polarized short-stack range. Minni still to act behind and could raise. Best play is to fold cleanly, preserve my 5500 stack, and exploit position in future hands rather than chase in a multiway pot with air.

I call 600. I have Js3s on 3c-9s-2h — bottom pair with a very weak kicker and a backdoor spade. Claude is short-stacked and put in a relatively small c-bet (600 into a 1550 pot), so calling is cheap: required equity ≈ 600/(1550+600) ≈ 28%. Versus an UTG c-bet range that will contain a lot of missed overcards and some bluffs, my pair has reasonable showdown value and enough equity to justify calling. Raising is unattractive (out of position, poor blockers, polarizes my range and commits too many chips against a short stack). Folding surrenders too much equity for a small price. Plan: call to see a turn — continue only if I improve (two pair/trips) or get a very favorable card/cheaper action; fold to large, clear strength on the turn. If Claude shoves his small remaining stack after my call, I will call the extra because the additional commitment is small relative to the pot and my stack and I’m often ahead of his jam range.

I have J♠3♠ on 3♣-9♠-2♥-7♦ — bottom pair with a very weak kicker and no realistic draw (the flush would have needed runner-runner). Pot is 2,150 and Claude is short (≈800) and was the preflop raiser/c-bettor. Betting or raising here is unattractive: I’m out of position, my hand fares poorly versus Claude’s continuing range, and leading only bloats the pot or forces a shove. I’ll check to control the pot and see a cheap river; that keeps my options open. If Claude jams his short stack I’ll call the extra because the additional cost is small relative to the pot and his jam range will include many hands I beat; I won’t bluff-raise or overcommit with this marginal hand.

Turn card (7d) has arrived. Minni checked after calling the flop bet of 600. Her check suggests a weak or medium holding given the action so far. With KJ diamonds, I have K-high plus two strong draws (flush draw with 4 outs + gutshot straight draw with 4 outs = ~8 total outs). This is a semi-value bet situation where my hand has solid equity and her check strongly suggests weakness. Betting 500 applies pressure, maintains momentum, extracts value from her medium holdings, and has favorable pot odds with my draws. With only 800 chips remaining, I need to play actively with equity-rich hands rather than give free cards.

I have J♠3♠ on 3♣-9♠-2♥-7♦ — bottom pair with a very weak kicker and no realistic draws now (turn killed the runner-runner spade). Claude is short and has shown a straightforward line (UTG raise, flop c-bet, small turn bet). Folding here would be too conservative given the price; raising would be a bad play — it polarizes my range, commits chips out of position and is unlikely to fold out better hands. Calling 500 into a 2,650 pot gives me ~15.9% required equity. I have about 5 clean outs to improve to two pair/trips (3 threes + 3 jacks ≈ 10.6%) but I also have meaningful showdown value versus Claude’s bluffs/overcards and some weaker pairs. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot controlled; if he shoves his short remainder on the river I’ll give that shove close consideration (likely calling given the small extra cost relative to the pot and his polarized jam range).

I check. I have only bottom pair (3s) with a weak kicker on a fairly dry 3–9–2–7–T rainbow board. Claude was the preflop raiser and took a straightforward line (flop c-bet, small turn bet) that looks like a fairly strong or at least engaged range (pairs and some overcards) — betting river for thin value is unlikely to get called by worse hands very often (only 22 or occasional random pair/float), and it will simply get called or raised by better hands. There's no real bluffing upside either: Claude is short and willing to call/shove, so a river stab polarizes me into very difficult spots out of position. Checking realizes showdown equity against his bluffs/weaker pairs while avoiding bloating the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to see a showdown rather than bet and risk being raised or called by many hands that beat me.

River decision with K♦ J♦ on a board of 3♣ 9♠ 2♥ 7♦ T♣. I'm all-in for my last 300 chips. Minni has checked the river after calling two aggressive streets (flop 600, turn 500), which suggests either weakness or a medium-strength hand she's looking to get to showdown with cheaply. My K-high (K-J-T-9-3) is a thin hand at showdown, likely behind most holdings she'd call with (pairs like 9s, 7s, medium holdings like 8x, Qx). However, going all-in here makes sense for several reasons: (1) Pot odds are 11:1 (3150-to-300), and I only need 8.3% equity to break even - my K-high has roughly 15-20% equity against her likely calling range; (2) Her river check after calling twice suggests potential weakness or uncertainty; (3) With a 300-chip stack (essentially a dead stack in 50/100 blinds), I need to maximize fold equity - she may fold hands like Q-high, weak 8s, or hands she's uncertain about; (4) Checking gives up fold equity and almost certainly loses to the showdown; (5) My aggressive line (bet-bet) continues naturally with a river bet. If called, I showdown and likely lose, but the fold equity combined with positive pot odds makes this the higher EV play.

Claude (UTG) showed a straightforward, short‑stacked line (UTG raise, flop c‑bet, small turn bet) and now posts a modest 300 on the river. I have bottom pair (3♠) on a largely dry 3–9–2–7–T rainbow board. Folding 300 into a 3450 pot would be overly tight — calling costs ~8% of the final pot (300/(3450+300) ≈ 8%), excellent odds to see showdown. Raising is a bad idea: it polarizes my range, commits chips out of position, and there’s little fold equity vs a short, value‑oriented line. Claude can be bluffing or value‑betting thin; my pair is often good enough. I’ll take the cheap showdown and call.
I am on the button with A7o. This is a standard opening range hand in a 3-handed or 4-handed game. I'll raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds.

Fold. 3♥9♦ is a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand with almost no playability out of position. Flash opened to 300; calling 250 into a 450 pot requires roughly ~36% equity just to break even, and that equity collapses if the big blind defends — turning this into a multiway pot where my hand performs badly. A raise to isolate would be reckless (no fold equity, poor blockers, and I’d be committing more with junk). With a deep stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve stack for profitable opportunities.

68o is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, poor high card strength, low playability postflop. Button raise from Flash suggests a wide range, but this hand has poor equity against it (dominated by better 6x, 8x, suited connectors). Pot odds are ~3:1 (200 to call into 650), but implied odds are minimal with this hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots. History shows Flash aggressive on button, but no need to defend wide from BB here.

I’m on the button with 7♣5♦ in a limp pot against the small and big blinds. This is a classic steal spot: the hand is weak but benefits a lot from position and fold equity. I’ll open to 300 (≈3× the big blind) — big enough to generate folds and price out speculative defenders, but small relative to my 24.95k stack. Plan: if both fold I pick up the blinds; if called I play postflop with initiative and can c-bet good turns; if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold this marginal holding. This aggressive, position-based play maximizes expected value over many hands.

Preflop from the small blind, facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). My hand is 6h3h, a weak suited connector with poor playability out of position. Minni has been aggressive in prior hands, suggesting a wide raising range, but 63s has minimal equity against that range and is difficult to play post-flop OOP with reverse implied odds on low cards. Effective stack is ~5k, but folding marginal hands preflop preserves stack for better spots. No reason to 3-bet bluff here without fold equity or position. Standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind with QJo. Minni raised to 3x from the button. This is a standard opening range for a button. With QJo, I have a solid hand that plays well post-flop, and I am getting 3.25-to-1 odds to call. I'll call and see a flop.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a very dry board (8-4-2 rainbow). My hand, QJ offsuit, provides two overcards but has not connected with the board. Standard play in this situation is to check to the aggressor to see their continuation bet sizing and then decide whether to peel a card or fold. Lead-betting here doesn't accomplish much against a standard button opening range.

I was the preflop aggressor and the flop (8♣-4♥-2♠) is very dry and rainbow. With 7♣5♦ I have only a 4‑out gutshot and little showdown value, but I have position and fold equity. A small continuation bet should fold many overcards and unpaired hands while keeping the pot manageable if called. I’ll bet ~1/3–1/2 pot to apply pressure without risking too much — if Flash raises strongly I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate the turn (look for a 6 or a safe blank).

I have QJ offsuit on a dry 8-4-2 rainbow board. Minni, who has been active and aggressive from the button, has fired a continuation bet of 250 into a 650 pot (roughly 38% pot). While I haven't hit the board, I have two overcards and the pot odds (offering nearly 4-to-1) are attractive enough to see a turn card. Against a wide button opening range, my Q-high might even be the best hand occasionally, or I have 6 clean outs to top pair. I will call this small bet and re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card and Minni's subsequent action.

I am first to act on the turn. The board is 4h-2s-8c-Kc. I currently have Queen-high with no draw. The King of Clubs is a card that significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Minni) range, as she has all the strong Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) and potentially sets. My call on the flop likely looked like a middle pair or an 8. I cannot profitably lead into this card, and I have no reason to bluff here given Minni's aggressive history. I will check and intend to fold to any significant bet.

Check. I was the aggressor on the flop and got called, but the K♣ on the turn is a big, scary card for my 7♣5♦ — it improves a lot of hands that called my flop bet (Kx, two-pair, stronger pairs) and it does not meaningfully improve my holding. I currently have no pair and only four outs (the four 6s) to make a straight (~8.7% to hit the river), so my equity is small. Flash’s check is ambiguous (weakness or slowplay), and a turn barrel here has limited fold equity and will often be called or raised by hands that beat me. With a relatively small pot versus deep stacks, the lowest-variance + highest-EV line is to check and control the pot, realize my equity, and re-evaluate on the river: bet for value if I hit a 6, consider a small bluff on safe blank rivers when checked to, and fold to significant aggression if I miss.

The board is 4h-2s-8c-Kc-6c and I have Queen-high. Minni checked back the turn after betting the flop. This turn check suggests she might have a medium-strength hand like a pair of eights, a small pocket pair, or is giving up with air. Since I have no showdown value with Queen-high, my only way to win is to bluff, but Minni has shown a tendency to call down in previous hands (e.g., Hand #12, Hand #25). Betting into her is risky because she might call with any pair or even Ace-high. I will check and hope for a check-back, though I expect to lose the pot most of the time.

The 6♣ on the river completed a 4-5-6-7-8 straight using my 7♣5♦. This is a very strong made hand, but it is not absolutely invulnerable — the only hands that comfortably beat me here are club flushes (two clubs in the opponent’s hand). Flash’s line (call preflop, call my flop bet, check-check on turn and river) looks like a medium-strength / showdown-type range (Kx, middle pairs, Jx, some floats) rather than a confident value-bet with a big flush. Against that range I should be betting for value. I want a size that gets called by Kx and medium pairs but doesn’t bloat the pot unnecessarily against the club-flush combos or commit the effective stack. Pot = 1,150, so a ~60% pot value bet (~700) is appropriate — it extracts from worse hands and keeps the decision manageable if I face aggression. If Flash raises large I’ll fold (he’s representing a flush); if he calls I’m likely ahead and collect. Action: value bet ~700.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high. The board is 4h-2s-8c-Kc-6c. Minni bet the flop, checked the turn when the King appeared, and has now fired a 700-chip bet on the river into a 1150-chip pot. My Queen-high has zero showdown value; I lose to every pair and every Ace-high in her range. While her check on the turn suggests she might not have a King, her river bet is likely for value with a hand like a pair of eights or a pocket pair, or a bluff. However, even as a bluff, many of her likely 'air' hands (like AJ, AT, A5) still beat me. Without a pair or a strong reason to believe a bluff-raise would work against a player who has shown a willingness to call down, I must fold.

3-handed on the button (acting first pre-flop), 92s is a playable hand with suited connector potential for straights and flushes, good post-flop playability. Standard opening range here includes 92s. Opponents from history are aggressive, but raising first-in builds the pot and takes initiative. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is standard sizing with deep stacks relative to pot. Folding too tight, limping weak. Long-term EV positive.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten of Diamonds and Eight of Hearts (T8o). The Button (Elon) has opened with a 3x raise to 300. T8o is a weak hand to defend out of position against a button open, especially with the Big Blind (Minni) still left to act. Minni has a massive stack and has shown a willingness to be aggressive. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the initiative or a strong hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play in this situation to preserve my stack.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; I'm in the big blind with T7 hearts suited. Heads-up the button's opening range is wide, and T7s has decent playability and flush/straight potential. Calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity — the pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more (300 total committed), so my break-even equity is ~31%, which T7s has vs a typical open range. I don't want to inflate a pot out of position with a marginal hand by 3-betting, and Elon only has ~50BB so he could shove or play back. Plan: see the flop and proceed aggressively only with a heart, pair/top pair, or strong draw; fold to heavy, polarized pressure if I miss. Action: call 300 (total).

Flop: 5h Jd 8s. I hold Th7h — no pair, only a gutshot to a 9 (4 outs, ~8.5% on the turn, ~17% by river) and a backdoor heart flush that would need both turn+river hearts (very unlikely). Out of position vs the button's wide open range, I want pot control with this marginal hand. A c-bet OOP risks bloating the pot when I have little fold equity vs hands that connect (Jx, Tx) and poor equity vs sets/overpairs. Plan: check and see how Elon reacts. If he fires a small bet (~<=1/3 pot) I’ll call to realize equity and reevaluate on the turn; I’ll fold to a large raise or heavy polarized pressure.

Heads-up on the flop after my preflop raise from the button. Board is Jd8s5h, rainbow and somewhat drawy but not hitting my 92c suited directly—no pair, no strong draw, just backdoor straight/flush potential. Minni checked OOP after defending BB wide vs button open. Her check suggests weakness or trapping with a hand like Jx, set, or floats. As the preflop aggressor in position, this is a standard spot to c-bet light for pot control, initiative, and fold equity. BB check-fold frequency is high here vs continuation bet. Betting ~half pot pressures her range without committing too much. If called, reassess turn; fold to raise. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws/improvers and loses value when she folds.

I have T♥7♥ on 5♥ J♦ 8♠. I checked the flop and Elon fired a small c‑bet (~1/3 pot, 325 into 975). This is a wide, low‑risk sizing from the button — he can have many missed hands. My hand has a gutshot to a 9 (4 clean outs), plus pairing chances (tens or sevens) and a remote backdoor heart possibility. The bet gives me roughly 25% break‑even equity; my combined multi‑street equity vs a wide c‑betting range is at or above that, so calling to realize equity and keep the pot controlled is the best play. Raising OOP with a marginal hand is unnecessary and will bloat the pot or get me in tough spots. Plan: call the 325, evaluate the turn — play aggressively if I improve (9/heart/pair), fold to large polarized pressure if I completely miss.

The turn (7♠) pairs my 7 and turns my T♥7♥ into a modest made hand — I now have a live pair with a T kicker and ~9 clean outs (3 tens + 2 sevens + 4 nines) to improve further. Elon opened wide preflop and used a small flop c-bet, so his range contains a lot of missed overcards and thin holdings; his checking the turn shows weakness. Betting a small-to-medium amount (~1/3 pot) achieves three things: takes the initiative and can take the pot down vs his weak checking range, extracts thin value from worse hands (A/K/Qx and some Jx/8x that will call), and denies a free river to overcards/draws. I won’t inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand — if he raises big I’ll fold or re-evaluate based on sizing; if he calls I’ll re-assess river action. Execute a modest value bet.

Facing a turn donk bet of 450 into a 1750 pot from Minni OOP after calling my flop c-bet. My 92cc has no pair, no straight draw (board 5h-7s-8s-Jd blocks most relevant draws for me), and only a weak backdoor club flush draw. No showdown value against her likely value range (Jx, 8x, sets, two-pair) or strong draws (spades flush draws, 6x/9T straights). Her donk bet sizing is small (~1/3 pot), but as preflop caller OOP, her range strengthened on this turn card bringing the spade flush draw and filling gutshots. My button c-bet flop range is polarized, but folding trash here maintains discipline, preserves stack (450 is ~10% of my 4725), and avoids spew. Pot odds ~3.8:1 insufficient for <20% equity I have. Standard fold to avoid difficult river spots.

I am on the button with 8s 4c, which is a very weak hand. Given the aggressive play style of Minni in the small blind and the short stack of Elon in the big blind, there is no strategic reason to open this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I'm heads-up from the small blind with Qh Js — a strong, playable hand that should be raising rather than checking/calling out of position. Elon (BB) has ~46BB, so a standard 3x open (to 300 total) accomplishes three things: (1) builds a pot with a hand that plays well HU, (2) denies correct pot odds to many marginal hands and narrows his calling range, and (3) preserves fold equity so I can take the pot down preflop or on many flops. If he calls I can proceed with a sensible c-bet strategy on favorable textures; if he 3-bet-shoves I will re-evaluate based on his sizing but QJ offsuit has reasonable equity versus a wide shove range from a shorter-stacked opponent. Folding here is too tight and simply calling surrenders too much initiative and fold equity. I therefore raise to 300.

A7s is a strong defending hand from the BB vs a SB raise after BTN fold. Minni's raise is likely wide from SB. Pot odds are favorable: 200 to call into 400 pot (~3:1). Equity is solid vs her probable 40-50% range. While OOP postflop, Ac blocker helps, and suited connectivity allows multiway potential or draws. Preflop call maximizes value without overcommitting. Raise would be fine but risks larger pots OOP vs deep aggressive opponent. Check-fold postflop if miss.

I raised preflop and the flop (9c Th 4h) is favorable to my raising range. Holding Qh Js I have an open‑ended straight draw (any K or 8 — 8 outs), two overcards to the 9/4 (and J > T), plus a small backdoor heart possibility. Against Elon's calling range (broadways, pairs, connectors) I have sizable equity and should use my initiative. A half‑pot continuation bet (300 into the 600 pot) builds the pot when I have good drawing equity, applies fold equity vs weak pairs and air, and keeps the price reasonable if called. If he check‑raises big or shoves I will re‑evaluate (large check‑raise often indicates a made hand like two‑pair/set and I can proceed cautiously), but as played I c‑bet 300 to realize equity and pressure his marginal holdings.

A7o (Ac7s) is a decent BB defending hand preflop vs SB minraise after BTN fold. Flop 9cTh4h misses me completely: ace-high, no pair, no straight/flush draw, but Ac blocker reduces her Ax strong hands. Her 300 cbet into 600 pot (half-pot) is standard continuation from wide SB range, which likely includes overcards, pocket pairs, draws (hearts, gutshots like 85/J8). Pot odds excellent: 300 to call into 900 (~3:1), and my equity is ~35-45% vs her probable 50bb+ effective range (overpairs, Ax suited, broadways, suited connectors). Implied odds good with 44BB stack and her deep stack. OOP is a concern, but small call size minimizes risk; plan to check-fold turn if blank or check-raise if improves. Raising bluff too thin without great fold equity (board draws might call her) and commits ~20% stack. Folding too tight given odds and range advantage. Call to realize equity and control pot.

Turn is a blank (2c) after I c-bet the flop. I hold QhJs: an open-ended straight draw to K or 8 (8 outs, ~17%) plus two overcards versus the 9-T-4 board. Elon called my flop c-bet, so his range is wide (top pairs, Tx, 9x, some broadways and pockets). Checking surrenders fold equity and gives him a free river; double-barreling applies pressure and can win the pot right away vs many of his holdings while giving me equity to improve if called. Pot is 1200 and Elon has ~4125 behind — a half-pot barrel (600) is a sensible size: enough to fold out weaker pairs and get value/realize equity if called, but not commit unnecessarily. Plan: bet 600; if he calls we go to the river with our 8 outs and showdown potential; if he check-raises/shoves large I will generally fold this semi-bluff (only call very small raises), since the raise usually indicates a made two-pair/sets/top pair with strong kicker and my draw's equity isn't enough to call big pressure.

Facing a turn bet of 600 into 1200 on a 9c Th 4h 2c board with Ac7s (ace-high, no pair, no draw, Ac blocker). Preflop and flop calls were standard defense vs SB wide range with good pot odds. Turn 2c is a blank for my hand but completes low club draws and pairs low cards, hitting some villain draws or floats. Minni double-barrels half-pot both streets, representing strength or semi-bluff from aggressive SB range (sets, two-pair combos like T9/J9, overpairs, improved draws). My equity is marginal (~20-30% vs her polarized continuing range), below the ~25% pot odds (600 into 2400), especially OOP where she'll bet river wide or check strong hands. Ac blocker helps slightly but not enough for implied odds with my 4k stack vs her 25k. Raising bluff thin without fold equity or blockers to her value; pot commitment too high (~25% stack). Check-fold realizes fold equity pre-turn and avoids tough river spots. Consistent with flop plan to fold turn blanks.

On the button with 4♥8♠ — a weak but playable, “live” hand in 3‑way because I have position on both blinds. Pot is 150, call is 100; my stack (26,825) is much deeper than Elon's (4,075) and Flash's (8,950), so I can pressure them. I want to take the initiative and try to steal the blinds while keeping the pot manageable, so I open to a standard ~3x BB (300 total). This sizing gives decent fold equity but is small enough that I can comfortably fold to a sizable 3‑bet. Postflop plan: c‑bet on favorable textures and release against significant resistance — I’m not committed with 4‑8o.

Qs3d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential, and dominated by most of Minni's button raising range. Facing a standard 3x raise from the button, where Minni has shown aggression throughout the session, the hand has insufficient equity to defend profitably from the small blind. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities in this long 150-hand game, avoiding speculative calls with trash.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 3s, which is a very weak hand. Minni, who is currently the chip leader and playing quite aggressively, has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. While I have decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), playing such a poor hand out of position against a strong opponent is a recipe for losing more chips. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop on the button with A4o, a speculative ace-high hand with decent playability due to position. Blinds are posted (pot 150), to call 100. Standard button raise to 3x BB (300 total) to steal blinds or isolate weaker hands. Both opponents are aggressive but I have position postflop. Folding is too tight from BTN; limping is weak. My stack is 40BB, fine for a raise. History shows opponents fold to button raises often enough for +EV.

I am in the Small Blind with Seven-Eight offsuit facing a Button open from Elon. 7-8o is a weak hand to play out of position, and with Minni still to act in the Big Blind with a massive stack, calling is unattractive. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Call (300 total). Facing a 3BB button open, folding every weak hand here is too nitty given the pot odds — I only need ~31% equity to make the 200 call profitable. 5♠3♦ is marginal but has enough showdown/backdoor potential against a likely wide button opening range. Plan postflop: play cautiously out of position — check/control on dry boards, continue with pairs or strong draws, and fold to heavy aggression if I miss and face strong pressure.

I called preflop as a defend against a 3BB open, but on K♥ Q♠ 2♥ my 5♠3♦ has essentially no showdown value — just a very remote runner-runner spade backdoor and small pair-outs. Elon checked the flop, which can indicate weakness or pot control, but betting here as a bluff has poor expected value: I have little fold equity versus his likely Kx/Qx/overcards and I’m out of position. With a large effective stack behind relative to the pot this isn’t the spot to invent aggression with no equity. I’ll check, keep the pot small, and plan to fold to any sizable turn pressure; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and only continue with real improvement (a pair or a very favorable runout).

Flop completely misses my A4o: ace-high with no pair, no draws, poor kicker. Board Kh Qs 2h hits Minni's BB calling range hard (Kx, Qx, suited connectors, pairs like JJ/TT/99). She checks, which could be weakness or trap, but c-betting air here risks getting called by better hands or those with equity (hearts, JT straight draw). Position allows checking back to realize equity cheaply or fold to turn aggression without losing more. Pot control is key with 38BB stack; bluffing this wet board vs deep BB defender not +EV. History shows Minni calls aggressively but checks here suggest possible marginal strength we beat sometimes. Free turn card maximizes thin equity (backdoor ace outs).

I have nothing meaningful here (5♠3♦): no pair, no real draw — only about 6 river outs (~13%) to make a pair. Elon checked flop and checked the turn, showing either weakness or pot control. Betting out of position with almost no equity and limited fold equity is a losing play; there's no reason to inflate the pot. I’ll check and keep the pot small, planning to fold to any meaningful river bet unless I actually pair.

Turn board brings a 9d, which doesn't improve my Ad4c (still ace-high no pair/draw). Pot control remains key with marginal hand and 38BB stack. Minni checks again, suggesting possible weakness, medium strength, or trap, but her BB calling range includes many Kx/Qx we lose to. Board has straight draws (e.g., JT) and heart flush draw. Checking behind realizes thin equity (3 outs to ace, runner-runner possibilities) for free while keeping pot small. Bluffing here risks getting called by better ace-high, pairs, or draws given her history of calling stations when passive. Position allows free river card; if she bets river, easy fold. History shows frequent check-checks leading to cheap showdowns (e.g., Hand 1,16). No value bet, low EV bluff spot vs deep BB defender. Check maximizes EV long-term.

I have 5♠3♦ on 2♥ K♥ Q♠ 9♦ 9♠ — I missed completely. There are no further cards to improve me and I have essentially zero showdown value. Elon checked flop and turn, which looks like pot control or weakness; betting river as a bluff here has very little fold equity against his likely Kx/Qx/overcard or any made hand, and I have no meaningful blockers to nines. Stack sizes (Elon ~3.8k, me ~26.7k) also argue against risking chips in a marginal bluff spot. Objective is to maximize long-term stack growth, so I check to avoid unnecessary risk and will fold to any meaningful river bet.

River pairs the 9s, board now 2h Kh Qs 9d 9s. My Ad4c is still pure ace-high, no pair, no completed draw. Minni has checked three streets from BB after calling a button raise preflop, indicating possible medium strength (Qx, Jx, missed draws like hearts or JT), weakness, or a trap, but her passive line suggests we have showdown value vs her range. Top pairs like Kx or Qx likely bet turn/river for value given the pot (650) and board texture. Trips (99, K9, Q9) or straights (JT, AT) probably bet earlier aggression. Bluffing here with ace-high is risky vs a calling station BB defender who has shown check-calls (e.g., Hand 12,25); thin value doesn't justify sizing (pot 650, min raise 100 but effective bet ~650-1300 risks stack 3775/38BB). Checking realizes equity for free in position, pot control with marginal hand, consistent with preflop c-bet denial and turn/river control plan. History of check-check showdowns (Hand 1,16,31 so far) favors free card over forcing action. EV+ to check behind long-term.

I am on the Button with a strong starting hand, King-Ten of Spades. This is a premier hand to open-raise with, especially in a three-handed game, as it allows me to take control of the pot and utilize my positional advantage. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300.

5♦6♥ offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the small blind facing a button open. Pot is 450 and I must put in 250 to call (≈35.7% break‑even), with the big blind still to act — a multiway or a 3‑bet from BB will put me in tough out‑of‑position spots with little equity or fold equity. 3‑betting to isolate would be a bluff with poor blockers and is exploitable; calling keeps me OOP postflop with a ragged hand. With a deep stack and many future opportunities, folding and preserving chips for better spots is the +EV play here.

Facing a button raise to 300 after posting BB 100, so need 200 more to call into a 450 pot. My J♥3♠ is a complete trash hand with poor playability, low equity against Flash's likely wide but strong button raising range (which includes many hands that dominate mine). No flush draw potential, gapped connectors at best but offsuit. Deep stack but no reason to defend this wide out of position postflop where I'll be crushed. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. History shows Flash aggressive but profitable to fold weak hands here.

I'm on the button with QcTc — a strong suited Broadway with excellent postflop playability and position against both blinds. Pot is 150 and it's folded to me, so opening is standard. I size to 300 (3x BB) to apply pressure, deny cheap flops, and get value/isolate weaker blind ranges (SB will defend wide). This sizing gives solid fold equity while keeping options postflop: I can c-bet favorable boards or control the pot and outplay opponents in position. If I face a sizable 3‑bet I'll re-evaluate based on sizing and villain tendencies, but my default plan is to raise here to take the initiative.

2c9h is a trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing a button raise to 300 (to call 250 into 450 pot), pot odds are ~1.8:1, but postflop playability is awful—no flush draw potential, weak straight outs, dominated by any calling range. Opponents aggressive per history, but folding weak hands preflop preserves stack for better spots. No value in calling, shoving, or raising.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-3 offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. 8-3 is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Even though I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, I will be out of position against a very large stack (Minni). Given the poor quality of my hand and the disadvantageous position, the most prudent move is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop on the button with As2s in an unopened pot (likely SB Flash folded, BB Minni checked). A2s suited is a strong stealing hand from the button heads-up vs BB: ace blocker, suited for flush/straight potential, great postflop playability in position. Minni's stack is deep but history shows aggression that we can isolate/exploit. Standard raise size to 3x BB for value/steal. Pot 150, committing 300 builds it nicely with fold equity.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh5c, which is a weak hand to play out of position. Elon (Button) has raised to 300, a sizing he has used frequently. While a button's range is wide, Q5o does not perform well enough to justify a call, and 3-betting as a bluff is risky given the stack sizes and the presence of an aggressive big stack in the Big Blind. The most disciplined and profitable long-term play here is to fold.

Elon opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the big blind with Ad8s. This is a marginal offsuit Ace but it has decent blocker value and postflop playability against a button open. Calling the extra 200 (300 total committed) is cheap relative to the pot and keeps the pot controllable — a small 3-bet here is unattractive because the hand is often dominated by other Aces and a larger 3-bet risks getting shoved on by his ~39bb stack. I’ll defend the big blind, see a flop, and use position and my deeper stack to apply pressure or fold to strong action as appropriate.

Flop K♠ 6♦ 3♠ with Ad8♠. I have no pair — just Ace-high with a backdoor spade and a useful Ace blocker. Elon was the preflop raiser but checked the flop, so his range includes both made Ks and many missed holdings. Out of position, betting here with a marginal hand is unnecessary risk: I can control the pot, realize equity, and keep bluffs in my range by checking. I’ll check and see the turn — I plan to call small bets (because of backdoor equity and blocker value) but fold to strong aggression unless I improve (pair or additional spade) where I can apply pressure or value-bet.

Flop of 6d Ks 3s hits my As2s perfectly with the nut flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity heads-up), ace-high equity, and position on button. As preflop raiser vs BB caller who checked, this is a prime c-bet spot for thin value (beats bluffs/air/draws she defends pre), protection (charges her flush/straight draws), and fold equity. Board is drawy (spade flush draw, small straight draws like 45), so betting denies equity and builds pot when ahead. Sizing ~60% pot (400 into 650) balances value from weaker hands/draws while leaving room for turns/rivers with my 3975 stack vs her deep 26475. History shows Minni calls multi streets but folds to aggression when missed (e.g., Hands 1,21,31); expect her to fold worse or pay with draws.

Elon c-bet 400 into a 1,050 pot on K♠-6♦-3♠. I have Ad-8s: no pair but an Ace-blocker and a tiny backdoor spade (runner-runner). To call 400 into the 1,450 total (after the bet) I’m getting ~27.6% pot odds. Versus a typical button c-bet range (Kx, broadways, some Axs, and some bluffs), my hand has borderline but reasonable equity and good blocker value to some strong A combos. Raising out of position with a naked A-high is unattractive — I’d be bloating the pot with very little real fold equity and can be crushed by Kx. Folding is too weak here given the price and my plan to fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve. Plan: call the 400 to see a turn, fold to significant aggression if I don’t improve, and proceed aggressively only if I pair the Ace or pick up a meaningful draw.

Board: K♠ 6♦ 3♠ 3♥. I hold A♦8♠ — no pair, my backdoor spade draw is dead. I called the flop to see a cheap turn and explicitly planned to fold to significant turn aggression without improving. Elon checked to me, so this is a free decision to control the pot. Betting here would be a pure bluff that only folds out the weakest part of his range and is vulnerable to a check‑raise or a call from Kx/strong broadways. With very little showdown value and no improvement, I prefer pot control. I’ll check and allow him to show aggression — I will fold to a large bet but can call a small probe depending on sizing and re-evaluate the river if we see it.

Heads-up on turn vs Minni (BB caller) with As2s on 6d-Ks-3s-3h board. I have the nut spade flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity to hit by river), ace-high showdown value, and position. Preflop raise/c-bet flop hit well; her flop call after check indicates a range including Kx (but board Ks), pocket pairs, draws (spade/straight like 45/A4s), or sets. Turn pairs the 3 (helps some of her range with 3x trips now possible, but unlikely given pre/call), doesn't complete many draws cleanly. Her turn check suggests weakness or trap/setup, but history (calls flop but folds turns/rivers to aggression, e.g., Hands 3,10,17,24,28) favors continuation. Pot 1450, SPR ~2.5: betting builds max value when ahead/draw hits, extracts from worse (weaker Kx/draws), fold equity vs air (~40% she folds given check-check tendencies). Check risks cheap showdown but gives her free card/draws equity; bet ~2/3 pot (1000) sizes well for value/protection, leaves 2575 behind for river flexibility vs her 26075 deep stack. Consistent aggression exploits her as defender/passive postflop.

Fold. The pot is 2,450 and Elon’s turn bet is 1,000, so calling would commit 1,000 into a 3,450 pot (you need ~29% equity). My hand A♦8♠ on K♠-6♦-3♠-3♥ has not improved: the backdoor spade draw is dead and my only clean outs are three Aces (~6.5% on the river). Elon opened from the button, c-bet the flop and now barrels a large turn — that line is heavily weighted to value (Kx, sets, strong broadways) and is unlikely to fold to a call or a light raise. Raising would bloat the pot with almost no fold equity and leave me dominated; calling is a poor price for my actual equity. Consistent with my pre-turn plan to fold to significant aggression if I didn’t improve, I’ll release the hand and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 8h-3c, which is a very weak hand. I have the option to fold, call 100, or raise. Given that this hand has almost no playability or potential to improve significantly post-flop, and I am against two players in the blinds who have shown aggression in previous hands, folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play.

Heads-up as the small blind (button) I have T♠2♠ — a speculative, low but suited holding with good postflop playability. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to see the flop (100 total committed including my SB). With position on all postflop streets and deep effective stacks, I prefer to defend a wide range rather than folding or unnecessarily inflating the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll call to realize equity, see the flop in position, and then either c-bet on favorable boards or fold to significant aggression if I miss and face strong action.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind limp from Minni. My hand is Qc4s offsuit, which is a weak, uncoordinated hand with poor playability. Folding the BB here is -100 EV dead money, so checking to realize equity on the flop is standard. Raising would be overly speculative with such a marginal holding against an opponent with a massive stack who has shown aggression in prior hands. I'll check and evaluate the flop, planning to fold to aggression unless I improve significantly.

Flop: A♠ Q♦ 9♥ — I hold T♠2♠. I’ve missed: no pair and only a two-card backdoor spade draw that needs both turn+river to hit. Pot is small (200) and I have position on Elon (BB), who checked preflop — his check range can include weak hands but also Ax/Qx/pairs. Betting here would be a pure, thin bluff with limited fold equity and meaningful risk of a shove or a call that’s bad for my equity. With deeper stacks and many hands I dominate by checking and using position to realise equity or turn floats/bluffs on a favourable card. Plan: check and evaluate turn — check back to control pot and preserve my stack; if Elon bets small I can occasionally float, but I’ll fold to large pressure with no pair.

Preflop, checked the weak offsuit Q4o in BB vs SB limp to realize equity. Flop hits middle pair Q with trash 4 kicker on A-Q-9 rainbow board, giving decent showdown value heads-up vs a wide SB limp-check range that includes Ax, worse Qx (KJ, JT, etc.), lower pairs, and some draws. Ace-high board caps her strong hands somewhat, and she shows weakness by checking. Half-pot bet takes initiative, protects against turns like K/T/J/8 that improve her range, extracts value from weaker holdings/draws she'll call, and has fold equity vs air. My 5k stack allows flexibility; pot grows to ~300, keeping me pot-committed lightly (~2%). Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and losing initiative, so betting thin value is +EV.

Pot is 300 and Elon bet 100, so I’m getting about 25% pot odds to call (100 to win 400). I hold T♠2♠ — no pair but two spades in hand and an A♠ on the board gives me a very small runner‑runner flush chance and 6 clean outs to make a pair by the river (≈24% to hit by river, plus a small extra from backdoor spade combos). Elon checked preflop, so his bet can include a lot of A‑x, Q‑x, medium pairs and some bluffs; my hand has reasonable equity against that mixed range and I have position to see the turn and act. Raising here with essentially air would be a thin bluff that can get me raised or committed by better hands, so I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the turn — folding to large turn aggression if I don’t improve, or floating/turning a bluff on a favourable card.

The turn (T♥) paired my T♠, so I now have second pair (Tens) with position. Elon checked preflop, barreled the flop and made a small turn continuation — his continuing range includes Ax, Qx, 9x, some weaker Kx and occasional bluffs. Calling the 100 is correct: it’s a cheap price to realize showdown equity (≈100 to win ~400 ≈ 25% pot odds), avoids bloating the pot with a vulnerable hand, and keeps my positional advantage to evaluate the river. I’ll call and plan to fold to strong river aggression if I don’t improve, call small/medium river bets for value, or extract value if he shows weakness.

Turn board brings the Th, adding a second heart for flush draws and potential straight draws like KJ (broadway gutshot) or J8 (gutshot). I have second pair with a terrible 4 kicker on an Ace-high board, which is highly vulnerable to any Ace, better Queen (QJ, QT, QK), or draws improving. Preflop SB limp-check-call flop suggests a wide range including Ax (weak kickers or offsuit), worse Qx, 9x, lower pairs, and bluffs/draws. My flop c-bet was thin value/protection against her weakness, but now pot control is key with a marginal hand heads-up vs her massive stack. Betting risks folding out weaker hands/draws while getting called by betters; checking induces bluffs on river, realizes showdown value cheaply (SPR ~12, pot small), and avoids bloating pot OOP. Plan to call small river bets if unimproved or fold to big ones.

Board: A♠ Q♦ 9♥ T♥ 4♥. I hold T♠2♠ — second pair (Tens) with a very weak kicker. Elon c-bet the flop small and then checked the turn; that line is consistent with Ax/Qx/9x, some medium pairs, and the occasional bluff/draw. The river brings a third heart, so several missed heart draws now become a made flush and many of Elon's continuing hands (A/Q/9) still beat me. There are very few worse hands that will call a river bet (maybe a weird bluff or very weak pair), while most of the hands that will call are ahead of me. Betting here is thin and likely to get value from better hands or fold out a lot of hands that were already behind — not ideal. I’ll check and go to showdown: I realize my equity cheaply, avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand, and only call a reasonable river bet if he bluffs or bets small.

River pairs my 4s, giving two pair (queens and fours) on A-Q-9-T-4 rainbow-ish board (three hearts). Strong holding that beats all non-ace pairs, missed draws, and bluffs in her SB limp-check-call flop-check turn-check range. Vulnerable to Ax (beats us) and heart flushes (range has some suited connectors/gappers), but her passive line caps strong Ax (weak kickers likely from limp) and suggests flushes would bet for value. Small value bet extracts from weaker Ax (A9+), Qx, 9x, Tx she'll call while protecting against rivered draws and having fold equity vs air. Pot 400 with my 4825 stack; 200 (half-pot) is low risk/reward, builds if called ahead. Checking lets worse realize free showdown or risks bet-raise from betters; thin value bet maximizes EV vs her weak range.

I hold T♠2♠ — second pair (Tens) with a very weak kicker on A♠ Q♦ 9♥ T♥ 4♥. Elon bet 200 into a 600 pot; calling 200 to win 800 means I need ~25% equity to break even. His line (checked preflop, small c‑bet on the flop, checked the turn, small bet on the river) is consistent with a range that contains a lot of Ax/Qx/9x value hands and some thin value, but also some bluffs/missed hands. Raising as a bluff is unattractive — my kicker is poor, better hands will call or reraise, and I have little fold equity. Folding surrenders >25% equity for one street when the price is cheap. Given the pot odds, my positional advantage and the reasonable chance that enough of his river bets are bluffs or weaker pairs, a call is the +EV play. I’ll call 200 and see showdown.

I’m on the button with 5♦6♥ facing a single open; pot is 150 and it costs me 100 to call (current bet to 200). This is a speculative, positionally-advantaged hand — calling is cheap and lets me see a flop in position against one or two opponents. I can realize equity with straights/paired boards and use position to fold to heavy resistance if I miss. I won’t 3-bet with 5-6 offsuit here because I don’t want to build a bloated pot out of position against likely stronger opens. Plan: call to see flop; if I hit a pair or strong draw I’ll continue, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to large turn/river aggression.

Preflop in SB with A♡3♠ offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability OOP multiway. Facing a button limp (Minni likely wide range) and BB yet to act fully, but pot odds 5:1 don't justify calling with dominated ace and reverse position. Raising light risks OOP multiway vs deep button stack. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in long game.
I am in the Big Blind with 7d 2d. Minni limped from the button and Elon folded his small blind. Since there is no raise to call, I will check to see a flop for free with a very weak hand.

I have 7d 2d on a flop of 8h 6d 3h. I have no made hand, just a backdoor flush draw and a gutter-straight draw (needs a 4 and a 5). In a limped pot, it's best to check and see if I can improve for free or evaluate if Minni bets.

Flop: 8♥ 6♦ 3♥. I have 5♦6♥ — middle pair (safest pair on board) and I’m in position against one opponent (Flash, checked the flop from the BB). His checking range is wide and contains a lot of missed overcards and small pairs. This is a good spot to take the initiative: betting extracts value from worse hands (smaller pairs, single pairs with weaker kickers, A-highs that will call) and charges the few draw combinations (two-heart combos or backdoors). I don’t want to bloat the pot with a vulnerable one-pair hand, so I’ll bet a modestly-sized amount to protect and get value — roughly half the pot. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises big I’ll fold most of the time since my pair is easily dominated.

I have 7d 2d on a flop of 8h 6d 3h. I have virtually no equity here, just a backdoor flush draw and a very weak backdoor straight draw. Minni, who limped on the button, has bet 150 into a 250 pot. Even in a limped pot, there is no reason to continue with 7-high and no real draws. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

2c6h is a complete trash hand: low, offsuit, unconnected, no blockers or playability. Pot odds require ~28.6% equity (100 to win 250 total pot), but 26o has far less than that against any limping/checking/raising range from blinds. Even on the button with position, this is an easy fold to preserve stack and wait for playable hands. Opponent histories show loose play but no value in speculating here. Discipline over 150 hands to maximize EV.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. The action has folded to me. Minni is in the Big Blind and has a massive stack (over 25k). While I could raise to steal, Q7o is a relatively weak hand to play out of position against a player who has shown aggression and a willingness to call in previous hands (like Hands #12, #25, and #27). However, for only 50 more chips into a 150 pot, I am getting 3:1 odds to see a flop. Given the pot odds, calling is acceptable to see if I hit a Queen or a draw, though folding is also a viable conservative strategy. I will call the 50 to see a flop.

I have Qh3c in the big blind and Flash just limped from the small blind. This is a marginal, often-dominated hand and I'll be out of position postflop. A minimum raise to 200 only costs +100 and is unlikely to fold many better hands, while building a pot I don't want OOP without a stronger holding. Better to keep the pot small and see a flop cheaply — check and evaluate on the flop (fold to heavy aggression if I miss, continue only if I improve or get a favorable runout).

I am in the Small Blind and checked the flop in a limped pot with Queen-high. The board is Ace-high (As-3h-6c), which is generally better for the Big Blind's range or simply a board where it's hard for me to have much. I have no pair and no draw. Since it's a limped pot and the pot is small (200), there's no reason to lead out and bluff into the Big Blind who could easily have an Ace or a 6. I will check and see what Minni does.

I have Q♣?Qh3c — middle pair (threes) with a decent queen kicker on A♠ 3♥ 6♣. Pot is 200 and Flash limped preflop then checked the flop, so his range is wide (Ax, broadways, small pairs, some air). Checking and letting a free card go through is undesirable: many overcards and Ace-holdings will improve or continue, and I’ll lose fold equity. A small, constructive bet (~half pot) maximizes fold equity and gets value from worse pairs / Qx while keeping the pot controllable OOP. I’ll bet 100. If I get called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if faced with significant raise I’ll assume Ax or a stronger made hand and fold.

I have Queen-high on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. Minni, in the Big Blind, has bet 100 into a 200 pot after I checked. I have no pair and no draw. While the price to call is small (100), there is very little chance my hand is currently best, and I have very limited potential to improve. Continuing out of position against an aggressive stack with nothing but Queen-high is a losing strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q2s. This is a marginal hand, but it is suited. In a three-handed game, I have the positional advantage for the rest of the hand. Minni (SB) and Elon (BB) are the remaining players. Given the current pot odds (100 to call for a 150 pot) and my position, calling or raising are both viable. However, since the stacks are somewhat deep (especially Minni's), and I want to see a flop cheaply with this speculative hand rather than bloating the pot out of position against potentially aggressive blinds, I will just call the small blind to see the flop.

I’m in the small blind with J7♣. Pot is 250 and I only need to call 50 more (100 total contribution). J7s is a playable, speculative hand but out of position versus both the button and the BB; raising here would inflate a pot I don’t want to play OOP and could draw a 3-bet from Elon. Calling is the lowest-cost way to realize equity and see a flop with reasonable pot odds. Plan: continue aggressively on favourable flops (pair of J/7, club/straight draws), fold or proceed cautiously to heavy action on dry, overcard boards. Call 50 to make my total 100.

Pocket threes in the big blind facing a button limp and small blind complete. Pot is already 300 with only 100 invested, offering excellent implied odds for set mining multiway. Opponents are deep-stacked, providing good potential payoff if I hit a set. Raising risks isolating with a marginal hand or bloating the pot OOP preflop. Best to check and see a cheap flop, playing cautiously post-flop.

Flop: K♥ 9♣ 7♥ — I hold J♣7♣: bottom pair (7s) with a weak backdoor club possibility. I'm out of position to two opponents and the pot is still small (300). C-betting OOP into two players with a marginal made hand will often get called by better hands (Kx/9x/pairs) or priced into big pots against aggression. My objective is to control the pot and realize equity cheaply, while allowing villains to bluff into me. I’ll check to keep the pot small, call reasonable single-barrel bets to extract value from worse pairs and draws, and fold to large, polarized raises that represent Ks/9s/sets. If checked through I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and look for a cheap showdown or a safe river bluff/value spot if I improve.

Pocket 3s on a K-9-7 flop with two hearts is a weak underpair in a multiway limped pot. The board is draw-heavy (hearts flush draw, open-enders like T8, J10), favoring opponents' likely limping ranges which include overpairs, top pairs, suited connectors, and Ax. OOP as BB, pot control is key with marginal strength. No set, so implied odds not realized. Betting risks isolation or raises from better hands; fold equity low multiway with Flash to act behind. Checking realizes equity cheaply, keeps pot small, and allows seeing turn action from Flash. If turn bricks and safe, can reassess for thin value or bluff later.

On a board of Kh-9c-7h, I have Queen-high with no direct draw. There were no raises pre-flop, and both the Small Blind (Minni) and Big Blind (Elon) checked. Since the pot is small (300 chips) and I have position, I can try to take it down now with a bet. However, given that my hand has almost no equity if called and the board is somewhat coordinated (straight and flush draws possible), checking behind to see a free turn card is a lower-variance play. I will check to see if I improve on the turn.

I have J♣7♣ on K♥9♣7♥6♣ — a pair of 7s with a four-card club flush draw. Flop and turn were checked by both opponents, so their ranges are likely weak or passive. This is a good spot to take an aggressive, value/protection line: I want to charge worse pairs and single-card draws, deny free rivers, and build a pot when I have a decent one-card equity to improve. I’m OOP to two players so I won’t bloating the pot, but a half-pot bet gives the right balance of fold equity and price for calling. If I get a big check-raise I can fold (my hand is vulnerable); if called I’ll re-evaluate the river and proceed cautiously if I don’t improve. Size chosen: ~1/2 pot (150) to extract value while controlling risk OOP.

Pocket 3s on a Kh-9c-7h-6c board is a weak underpair in a multiway limped pot. The turn 6c adds clubs flush draw potential and completes some straight draws (e.g., 8T, 5-8). Minni's 150 bet into 300 from SB shows strength or a semi-bluff/draw, as passive players check weak hands. OOP in BB, calling risks bloating pot multiway with Flash acting behind on button (who limped wide). Equity is poor against value ranges (Kx, 9x, 7x, 6x, sets, two-pair, draws); implied odds limited with only ~5k stack and opponents deep. No fold equity on raise OOP multiway. Pot control failed post-flop; best to fold marginal hand and preserve stack for better spots. History shows Minni aggressive post-flop when strong (e.g., Hand 3,10,12).

On the turn, the board is Kh-9c-7h-6c. Minni has led out with a half-pot bet of 150. My hand is Queen-high with no draws and no showdown value. While Minni could be betting a draw or a weak pair, I have no equity to continue and no reason to believe a bluff would be successful given the board texture and Minni's stack size. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have pocket Aces on the button with two players to act (SB Elon and BB Flash). Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call — this is the best hand preflop and I want to build the pot and isolate while still keeping worse hands in. Given Elon’s aggression and Flash’s willingness to defend, I’ll raise to a size that charges speculative hands and extracts value from broadways/medium pairs but still gets called: raise to 1000. If I face a small-to-medium 3-bet I’ll 4-bet to isolate; against a very large 3-bet I’ll likely call and proceed postflop to avoid folding the best hand. Postflop I’ll continuation-bet for value/protection and adjust to board texture and opponent action.

K4o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by any king or better in Minni's likely strong button raise range. Facing a large 10BB raise from an aggressive Minni on the button, calling 950 OOP commits nearly 20% of my stack with a hand that plays poorly post-flop and has low equity. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this deep-stacked cash game.

Minni has raised to 1000 from the button, which is a significant raise (10x the big blind). My hand, T7o, is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a player with a massive stack. Given the size of the raise and the weakness of my hand, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

ATo is a strong opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. Position is excellent, and with deep stacks, a standard raise of 3x the big blind (300) aims to steal the blinds frequently or build the pot with position when called. Opponents have been active but not overly aggressive preflop based on history. Pot is small at 150, and to-call 100 suggests standard blinds with possible limp/fold, making this a profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 5s, which is a very weak starting hand. Elon has raised from the Button to 300. 45 offsuit does not play well out of position, and the pot odds are not favorable enough to justify a call against a standard opening range. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to conserve my stack.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; I’m heads-up out of position with K♣2♠. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call (≈31% break-even equity). K2o is poorly playable and is heavily dominated by a standard BTN opening range (lots of Kx and Ax), and a light 3-bet here would be a thin bluff with terrible postflop prospects. I’ll fold and preserve a large stack for better, positionally favorable spots.

I am on the button with Q4o. While the button offers a positional advantage, Q4o is a relatively weak hand. My stack is 8450, and Elon in the Big Blind has a shorter stack (5275) while Minni is the chip leader. Given the previous action where Minni has been quite active and aggressive pre-flop, I'll choose to fold this weak hand to preserve my chips for a more favorable spot rather than entering the pot with a sub-par hand and being forced to play post-flop out of position or against high pressure.

Heads-up from the small blind with K♦3♣. In a two-handed spot the SB is effectively the button postflop, so I should be raising a wide range to take advantage of position and fold equity. K3o is weak but has reasonable heads-up equity and plays much better if I raise and see the flop in position than if I just limp/call and let the BB dictate action. The pot is 150 and calling costs 50, but a min-raise to 200 applies pressure on Elon’s blind, can win the pot outright, and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: min-raise to 200 to steal or play post-flop with position; c-bet on dry flops, pot-control or release if I miss and face strong resistance, and re-evaluate if he 3-bets (likely fold K3o to a large 3-bet).

T5o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, dominated potential, and limited playability post-flop. Facing a standard raise from the SB after BTN fold, this is likely a steal or wide range. Pot odds are 3:1 (100 to call into 300), requiring ~25% equity, but T5o realizes far less against Minni's polarized SB raising range (strong hands or bluffs). Calling puts me out of position with a speculative hand that rarely improves strongly. 3-betting is not viable due to hand weakness and reverse position post-flop. Folding preserves stack (5275) for premium spots against deep-stacked Minni (25975). Standard fold from BB.

I’m on the button with K♣5♠ in a 3-handed game — marginal hand but great position and big steal opportunity. Both opponents have much shorter stacks than me, so an open raise will pick up the blinds often and give me postflop initiative when called. I’ll raise to 300 (a standard ~3x BB here) to apply pressure; I plan to fold to a large 3‑bet but continue and c-bet straightforwardly on favorable flops if called.

AJ diamond suited is a premium hand from the small blind facing a button raise. Standard play is to 3-bet for value and isolation against a wide button range. Minni's history shows aggression but also folds to pressure (e.g., Hands 21, 31). Raising to 900 (3x the open) builds the pot while leaving good implied odds with flush draw potential. BB may fold, allowing heads-up. Folding or flat-calling risks multiway pots without initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with K d 4 c. Minni raised to 300 from the Button, and Elon 3-bet to 900 from the Small Blind. K4o is a very weak hand to play against a raise and a 3-bet, especially out of position. Elon's short stack (approx. 43BB) suggests he might be playing tighter or looking for a spot to commit, and Minni has been active. Regardless of their styles, K4o is well below the defensive range required to call a 3-bet. Folding is the only sensible play.

I opened to 300 on the button and Elon 3‑bet to 900. Pot is 1,300 and I must call 600 to continue, which requires ~31.6% equity (600 / (1300+600)). K♣5♠ is a marginal, often‑dominated hand vs a typical 3‑bet range (Ax, Kx, broadways, mid pairs) and has poor playability in a 3‑bet pot. Elon is relatively short‑stacked so his 3‑bet can be polarized and easily upgraded to a shove; I don’t want to bloat the pot with this holding. A 4‑bet bluff with K5s is unattractive (weak blockers, poor follow‑through), and calling here is a marginally +EV line only vs a very wide, weak 3‑bet. I planned preflop to fold to a large 3‑bet, so I’ll conserve my stack and fold.

Preflop on the button with Ad6d suited, a speculative hand with good playability, ace blocker, and flush potential. The pot is 150 with a to-call of 100, indicating SB likely limped (completing to 100) and BB checked, creating a limped pot with dead money. Position on button is ideal for isolating or stealing. Standard play is to raise for isolation against loose blinds, leveraging fold equity from history where preflop raises often take it down. 3-handed dynamics favor wide button ranges. Sizing to 300 (3x BB) balances value, protection, and fold equity without overcommitting my 56BB stack. If called, retain position and solid equity heads-up or multiway.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 4s. Elon, on the Button, has raised to 300. This is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Even though I have a large stack, there is no reason to defend with such a marginal hand against a button raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m in the big blind with T♦5♣ facing a BTN open to 300. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (total commit to 300), so the break‑even equity on a call is ~200/(450+200) ≈ 30.8%. Against a typical button opening range Elon is likely opening fairly wide here, so T5o’s equity is around that threshold (roughly low‑30s). I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a thin 3‑bet or a small raise — raising with T5o has poor fold equity/value and can get me in trouble if called. I’ll flat‑call to realize equity cheaply and play the flop cautiously: proceed on meaningful pair/draw, otherwise check/fold to significant aggression.

Check. I’m out of position with T5o on A‑6‑3 — no pair and only a handful of pair‑outs; villain raised preflop from BTN so his range contains a lot of Ax and strong broadways that connect with this flop. There’s little benefit to over‑bluffing OOP into one opponent who will call or raise enough hands to make a c‑bet thin; checking controls the pot, lets me realise my equity cheaply, and preserves the option to fold to significant aggression. Plan: check, fold to a large c‑bet, call a small probe (cheap) to see a turn if the price is right and I pick up a pair or improved outs.

Heads-up vs Minni on a dry Ac-6s-3d flop with two pair (Aces and sixes) using Ad-6d, which is very strong and near the nuts. Board is disconnected with no flush draws completed and limited straight draws (e.g., 45). Minni checked from BB after calling a button raise preflop, representing a wide range that I dominate much of (weak Ax, pocket pairs below 66, bluffs). In position, a value bet extracts from worse (Ax with bad kicker, 6x, sets unlikely preflop) and protects against gutshots/overcards. Pot 650, betting ~50% pot (325) balances value, thin value from floats, and denial of equity to draws. Consistent sizing with preflop aggression. Stacks allow flexibility for turns/rivers.

Call 325. I checked OOP with T♦5♣ on A‑6‑3 and Elon (BTN) fires 325 into a 975 pot. Calling 325 into a 1,300 total pot requires ~25% equity. I have ~6 immediate outs (any T or 5) — roughly a 24% chance to pair by river — so it's borderline but reasonable given BTN’s wide opening/c-bet range (many missed broadways and junk that I can still get to showdown against). Raising OOP with this hand is poor (no fold equity and bad playability if called); folding is too tight for the price.
