Plan: call to see the turn, fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve to a pair or pick up strong backdoor outs; if I pair the T or 5, shift to extracting value on later streets.

I improved to a single five on the turn (board 6s 3d Ac 5h; my hand 5c Td). Preflop Elon opened wide from the BTN and c‑bet the A‑6‑3 flop, then checked the turn — that line is often pot‑control with marginal hands or missed high cards. My pair of fives is a weak, low single pair: it beats lots of missed broadways and some weaker pairs but loses to any Ax and bigger pairs. Earlier I planned to extract value if I paired the T or 5, so I want to take a small stab for value while keeping the pot manageable OOP. A modest bet (~1/3 pot) will get called by worse hands (Kx, Qx, Jx, some small pairs/one‑pair hands and busted draws) and won’t commit me if Elon shows aggression. If he raises I’ll treat that as a strong range and fold; if he calls I can reevaluate the river. Bet 400 into 1,300 to extract thin value but preserve fold equity to larger aggression.

Turn board brings a 5h, completing the 3-4-5-6 straight for hands holding a 4 (which were mostly gutshots or weak draws on flop that unlikely floated 50% pot OOP). My Ad6d top two pair (AA66) remains very strong, crushing Minni's likely range of weaker Ax (AJ-AT, KQ etc. with Ace-high or weak kicker), A5/A3 two pairs (which lose to my higher second pair), or bluffs/draws. Sets (AA/66/33/55) possible but range disadvantaged preflop/call OOP. Her small 400 bet (~23% into 1700 pot) into a strong cbet board suggests thin value with Ax or semi-bluff, not monster (would bet bigger). IP with position and deep stacks (my 5k effective), raising extracts max value from worse, denies equity to any remaining draws (e.g., 47 for gutshot), builds pot for river value, and her history shows calling aggression. Size to 1200 (~3x her bet) balances value/protection without overbetting; if she shoves, we have equity vs range (behind sets/straights ~10-20% hands, ahead others). Folding equity high against polarized range. Superior to calling (keeps pot smaller, loses IP initiative) or flat (misses value).

Preflop BTN open, I flat in BB. Flop A‑6‑3 he C‑bets, I called to see a turn. I fired a small, 400 value probe on the 5h to target worse Kx/Qx and small pairs. His huge shove‑style raise to 1,200 on the turn polarizes to a very value‑heavy range (two‑pair combos like A5/A6/A3, sets 66/33/55, trips/AA, and strong A‑x) with relatively few bluffs. Pot is 2,900; calling 800 more requires ~21.6% equity (800/(2,900+800)). My hand (T♦5♣) is a weak, low single pair with only a handful of clean outs to improve on the river and poor playability OOP if we continue. Against the line and sizing I’m almost always behind and don’t have the equity or blockers to profitably call or re‑raise. Raising would be folly OOP with such a marginal hand. Fold and preserve stack for a better, higher‑EV spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Three offsuit. This is a standard hand to open or call with, but in a three-handed dynamic, Ace-high hands have significant value. The small blind and big blind are already in the pot. Raising is usually the preferred play to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately, especially since I've been playing relatively tight recently. A standard 3x raise to 300 should be effective.

Facing a button open to 300 while I’m in the small blind with Q7o. I’ve already posted 50 and must call 250 into a 450 pot (≈36% pot odds). In 3-handed play the button’s opening range is wide, but Q7o is easily dominated (many Qx/Ax and broadways) and has poor post‑flop playability out of position. A light 3‑bet here is too speculative — I have little fold equity and I don’t want to get isolated OOP vs deeper stacks. Preserving a healthy stack and waiting for a better spot/position is the best long‑term play. Fold.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds. Pot is 450, need to call 200 more for pot odds of about 3.25:1 (23% equity break-even). 9h3h is suited with some playability (flush potential, backdoor straight draws). Vs BTN open range (wide, ~35-40% hands), 93s has solid equity (~38-40% heads-up) and good implied odds deepstacked (66BB effective). Defending wide OOP vs BTN is standard to realize equity and deny steals. Folding too tight loses BB value long-term. No raise incentive with this hand. Call and reassess postflop.

Flop is 2d5s2h, a very dry paired board with no connection to my 93h suited. I have no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor heart flush draw (one heart on board). Preflop call was for equity realization OOP vs wide button range, but I completely missed. Flash's button opening range includes many 2x, 5x, Ax, Kx, Qx, pairs, etc., most of which crush me. OOP first to act on this board, standard is check-fold weak hands like this to avoid difficult spots postflop. No value bet, no strong draw to semi-bluff. Preserve stack for better spots; 650 pot not worth risking deepstacked with air.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board is very dry (2d-5s-2h). Elon checked to me, which is expected. Ace-high has decent showdown value here, but a continuation bet is standard to take down the pot immediately and put pressure on hands that missed the flop, such as broadway cards or small suited connectors. A bet of around half the pot should be sufficient given the lack of draws on this board.

Facing a 300 bet into a 950 pot on a dry, paired flop (2d5s2h) where I hold 9h3h with zero equity—no pair, no flush draw (only backdoor hearts), no straight draw. Preflop defense was correct for equity realization OOP vs wide button range, but I completely whiffed. Flash's button c-bet range on this board is polarized: strong value (22, 52s, sets, overpairs, Ax) or bluffs (overcards, gutshots), but my hand crushes nothing and folds to further aggression. Pot odds require ~24% equity to call, but I have <10% vs his range. No implied odds deepstacked on paired board. Check-fold preserves stack for premium spots; continuing OOP with air is -EV long-term.

I have 8♦9♦ on the button facing a single raise with the big blind behind. This is a very playable, positionally advantaged hand — good straight and flush potential and strong postflop equity against an opener’s wide-ish range. The price to see a flop is cheap (100 into a 150 pot) and stacks are deep, giving large implied odds if I hit. 3-betting is an option for balance/exploit, but I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use position to play postflop, so I will call and evaluate on the flop. Committing 100 preserves flexibility and realization of equity.

A3 offsuit is a marginal hand at best from the small blind facing a button limp. Multiway pots from out of position with poor playability and low equity against calling ranges are -EV long-term. Pot odds are attractive (50 to call into 250), but postflop playability is poor OOP, and implied odds are limited without suitedness or better kicker. Opponents like Minni defend wide, and Flash in BB likely continues. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in this long 150-hand game.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 4c. Minni, on the button, has limped in (called the 100), and Elon in the Small Blind has folded. The pot is small (250) and it costs me nothing to see the flop since I have already posted the big blind. While my hand is very weak, there is no reason to fold or raise; checking is the standard and correct play to see a free flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 4c on a board of 2d 6d Qh. The pot is 250, and Minni (the only remaining opponent) has checked. My hand is very weak, having no pairs or draws. Since Minni checked behind, there is no pressure to fold, but betting into a dry board with total air is also not ideal here. I will check to see a free turn card.

Board is 2♦6♦Q♥ and I hold 8♦9♦. I have a very strong drawing hand: nine diamonds to complete a flush (≈35% by the river) plus six non‑diamond outs to pair an 8 or 9 (these are distinct), so versus a wide calling range I have ~54% to improve to something by the river. Flash checked from the big blind, which usually indicates a wide/weak defending range. Betting is the correct, value/semi‑bluff line — it gets folds from missed hands and builds the pot when I hit. I size up to 150 into 250 (≈60% pot) to generate meaningful fold equity while still keeping the pot manageable with a draw. If called I’ll proceed to the turn and re-evaluate (press for value if I hit a diamond or pair; if I miss and face heavy aggression I’ll be willing to fold to avoid bloating the pot with a non‑nut draw).

I have 8-high with no pairs and no draws on a board featuring a Queen and a diamond flush draw. Minni limped pre-flop and is now betting 150 into a 250 pot. Since I have zero equity and no realistic way to improve or win the pot without a high-risk bluff, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

Q2 offsuit is a trash hand with poor playability, low equity against any calling range, and disconnected ranks. On the button with position, limping weak hands leads to difficult postflop spots and is exploitable. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this deep-stacked cash game. Opponent histories show aggression, but no need to get involved with this holding.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c4c. Elon folded on the button, and the pot is 150. I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, or I can raise to put pressure on Minni. However, 84 suited is quite weak. Minni has been playing aggressively and has a huge stack (24,950). Looking at previous hands, Minni frequently raises pre-flop and bets consistently. Calling 50 here is fine for pot odds, but folding is also acceptable to avoid getting into a marginal spot out of position against a massive stack. Given the low cost of completion (1:4 pot odds), I will complete the small blind and see if I hit a strong draw or two pair, but I'm prepared to fold to a raise from Minni.

I’m in the big blind with 3♦5♣ and the small blind just called. Pot is only 200 and there’s been no raise, so there’s no reason to invest more with a very weak, unsuited low hand. Raising would be a pure bluff/isolation attempt against a wide SB calling range and isn’t worth the risk. I’ll check and see the flop, planning to fold to meaningful aggression unless I pick up strong improvement.

On a board of 9h-Ks-Qc, my hand (8c-4c) has no connection and no potential draws. Since this was a limped pot and I am first to act, I will check and see what Minni does. If she bets, I will fold, as this board hits a Big Blind's range much better than mine and I have zero equity.

Flop is K♠ Q♣ 9♥ and I hold 3♦5♣ — no pair, no meaningful backdoor draws and very little equity. Pot is only 200 and SB (Flash) checked to me. In position I can try a small bluff, but against a caller-heavy SB range and with nothing to gain on later streets, that is unnecessary risk for a tiny pot. I’ll check back, preserve my stack, and plan to fold to any meaningful bet unless I pick up a clear improvement on the turn.

The turn card is the nine of clubs, pairing the nine on the board. I have 8-high with no real connection to this board, other than a very weak backdoor flush draw. Since Minni checked back on the flop, it's possible she also has very little, but the nine is a card that frequently hits a Big Blind's limping/checking range. Given that the pot is small and I have no showdown value, I will check again. If Minni bets, I will fold, as I have nearly zero equity to continue.

I have 3♦5♣ on K♠ Q♣ 9♥ 9♣. That’s air — no pair, no realistic straight or flush draw (only one club in my hand and only one card to come), and only 8 outs to improve to a pair/trips on the river (3s/5s/9s) ≈ 17% equity. The SB (Flash) called preflop and checked both flop and turn, so he’s either weak or slow-playing a strong pair; either way a bet here would be a thin bluff with very limited fold equity in a tiny pot (200) and plenty of calling hands in his range. I have position, so there’s no reason to force the issue — check and fold to meaningful aggression on the river unless I actually pair or hit trips. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

On the river, the 7 of clubs completes a flush for me (8c and 4c with the Qc, 9c, and 7c on the board). Although the board is paired with nines, the passive action on the flop and turn suggests that Minni is unlikely to have a full house or a trip nine. I have checked throughout the hand, and so has Minni. Now that I've hit a flush, I will bet for value. A bet of 150 into a 200 pot (3/4 pot) is a standard value bet size that might get called by a King, Queen, or even a Seven that stayed in.

I checked the river as planned and now face Flash’s raise of 150 into a 350 pot (pot will be 500 if I call). Break-even call frequency is 150/500 = 30%. My hand (3♦5♣) has no pair, no straight and I missed the club flush (I have only one club). Flash limp-called preflop, checked both flop and turn, then bets small on the river after the third club fell — that line is much more consistent with thin value (Kx, Qx), a slow-played nine, or a completed club flush than with a pure bluff. I don’t block meaningful club/major combo bluffs and have essentially zero showdown value, so calling would require a fairly high bluff frequency by the opponent to be profitable, which isn’t supported by his line. Raising as a bluff here is also unjustified with no blockers and no credible story. Fold and preserve the stack for better, higher-expected-value spots.

I am on the button with K2o. This is a very weak hand in most contexts, but on the button in a three-handed game, it has some potential for a steal. However, I am facing a small blind and a big blind who have been active. Looking at the stack sizes, I have 8650 chips. Folding is the safest and most standard play with a weak King and a deuce kicker, as it does not play well post-flop if called, especially given the current table dynamics.

I’m in the small blind heads-up after the button folded with J♥4♣. Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 more to call (total commit 100). J4o is a weak, marginal hand — too weak to open-raise for initiative without a specific read — but the price to see a flop is cheap (pot odds ~3:1). I’ll defend by calling to realize equity and keep the pot small; if I miss the flop I’ll generally check-fold to significant aggression, and if I connect (pair, two pair, strong draw) I’ll continue more aggressively. Raising to 200 would bloat the pot OOP with a poor hand, and folding cedes too many free pots.

Qh3s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, disconnected and dominated by better Qx or any pair. Minni limped from SB, which often indicates weakness, but my hand has low equity even against a wide limping range (~35-40% vs random). As BB, I get to realize equity for free postflop and will be in position vs SB heads-up. No need to raise and risk bloating pot OOP with trash; check to induce bluffs or hit improvement cheaply. Will fold to aggression postflop unless major improvement. Conservative play to preserve stack long-term.

I hold Jh4c on a Kc-6c-Kh flop — I’ve completely missed (no pair) and only have a weak backdoor club possibility (need both turn+river clubs). The board is paired with two kings, which heavily favours any Kx or medium pair in the BB’s preflop checking range. Betting as a bluff here is thin — a bet will often get called by worse or raise by better hands and I have almost no fold equity or showdown equity. I’ll control the pot and keep leverage by checking. Plan: if Elon checks back I’ll see the turn for free; if he bets a small probing bet (≈≤1/3 pot) I may call once to see a turn, but I’ll fold to meaningful aggression (large bets/raises) since I have very little fold or showdown equity.

Qh3s is complete air on a paired kings board with a 6c flush draw possible. No pair, no draws, queen-high loses to any king, pair, or likely holdings from Minni's limp-call range. Minni limped pre from SB (wide range) and checked flop, showing possible weakness but also trapping potential. Betting is a pure bluff into her with poor blockers and bad board texture for fold equity OOP. Checking keeps pot small, realizes any thin equity on later streets if she bluffs, and preserves stack. Will fold to most turns/rivers unless massive improvement (unlikely ~10% outs). Positionally heads-up, conservative play maximizes EV long-term in 150-hand game.

I hold J♥4♣ on K♣-6♣-K♥-A♦ — I have no pair and no real draw (the turn A♦ kills the backdoor club possibility; I cannot make a flush with one card left). Pot is 200 and I’m OOP facing Elon who checked the flop and preflop; his checked range can include Kx, A-x, medium pairs or passive holdings that will call a probe-bet. Betting here would be a thin bluff with very little fold equity and big downside if he raises or calls. Best play is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression, see the river for free and re-evaluate if he shows weakness.

Qh3s remains complete air on this Kc6c-Kh-Ad turn board: no pair, no draws, queen-high crushed by the paired kings, any ace, or Minni's likely limp-check range (pairs, Ax, Kx, draws that missed). Minni's SB limp pre and double check-flop/turn suggests weakness or trap, but betting OOP as a bluff has poor fold equity on this dry-ish but scary (paired + ace) board with position disadvantage. Checking preserves my 6300 stack in a marathon 150-hand game (hand 47), realizes any thin equity showdown or vs her future bluffs cheaply, and avoids bloating pot with 0 equity hand. Will fold to river bet unless miracle, prioritizing long-term EV over spew.

I hold J♥4♣ on K♣-6♣-K♥-A♦-Q♣. I have missed completely — no pair, no straight, and I still do not have a five-card club flush (only three clubs on board + my one club = 4 clubs). The river was checked by Elon, showing either weakness or a slow-play, but I have almost zero showdown equity vs the range that checks this board (Kx, Ax, Qx, two pair, etc.). Pot is small (200) and there is no meaningful fold equity from a river bluff — a bet will often get called by worse or raised by better hands. Best play is pot control: check and take it to showdown; if Elon bets, I will fold to significant aggression since I have no hand to continue with. Preserve chips for better spots.

Qh3s on river board Kc6cKhAdQc now pairs the Qc for kings and queens (KKQQx), a very strong two-pair hand likely best vs Minni's SB limp-check-check-check range (weak Ax, mid pairs, Kx without Q, missed club/straight draws). Unlikely beaten by QQ full house or KK sets given limp pre and passive play. Pot 200 small, my 6300 stack; thin value bet induces calls from her wide range (AKo/AQs, 77-JJ, KQo/KJo, missed draws) while risking little. Half-pot sizing extracts max EV without folding out too much. Preserves stack upside in long game.

I have J♥4♣ on Kc-6c-Kh-Ad-Qc — no pair, no straight and no completed flush. Elon checked flop and turn and now fires a small 100 into a 300 pot (≈1/3 pot). Calling 100 to win 400 requires ~25% equity. His line (checking down then a small river bet) is weighted heavily toward value: Kx (trips), A♣/A♦/A♠ combos that make two pair with the board, Qx (now two pair), and other made hands; only a small fraction of his river bets will be pure bluffs (missed club draws or air). My showdown equity versus that value-heavy range is essentially zero, so I’d need Elon to bluff >25% of the time to justify a call — not a reasonable assumption. Raising OOP as a bluff here has almost no fold equity and is negative EV. I’ll fold and conserve chips for better, defendable spots.

I’m on the button with K♥3♥ in a 3-handed pot. K3s is a marginal hand but has suitedness and positional advantage, so I want to take the initiative and try to pick up the blinds or isolate one opponent. A standard open to 300 (table norm) provides solid fold equity versus the SB/BB and keeps the pot manageable if called. If I get called I'll play cautiously postflop—C-bet good flops and fold to heavy resistance when missed. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate and lean toward folding K3s unless the 3-bet is small or I have a clear read to continue.

Q2 offsuit is a very weak hand from the small blind facing a raise from the button. Minni's button raise is likely wide, but Q2o has poor playability out of position, low equity against calling ranges, and no good blockers for a 3-bet bluff. Pot odds are about 1.8:1, but the hand doesn't justify calling or raising. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this long 150-hand game.

I am in the Big Blind with K6o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While K6o is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a button raise, especially with a 3:1 price on the call (200 more to win a pot of 450). I have a healthy stack and want to see a flop rather than folding pre-flop to a standard wide opening range from the button.

On a flop of J-8-6, I have bottom pair with a King kicker. This is a board that likely connects better with Minni's button opening range (containing many J-x, 8-x, and straight draws) than my blind-defending range. However, bottom pair is worth a check to see how Minni acts. I will check and evaluate based on the bet sizing. If Minni bets small, I can call to see if I improve on the turn or if the board stays relatively safe. If Minni bets large, I will likely fold as 6-x is very vulnerable on this board.

I opened preflop and have position with K♥3♥ on a relatively dry flop 6♣ 8♠ J♥. I have an overcard (K) plus backdoor hearts, and as the preflop aggressor I retain significant fold equity against the BB’s calling range, which contains lots of missed hands (small pairs, suited connectors, Axs, Kx). A standard continuation bet will often take the pot now or charge draws and poor showdown hands. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (~325) to maximize fold equity while not overcommitting with only K-high. If I get called I’ll check the turn and re-evaluate — look for a king or heart to continue for value, otherwise control the pot. If I face a large check-raise on the flop I’ll fold most of the time because K3 has weak showdown value.

I have bottom pair (sixes) with a King kicker on a 6c-8s-Jh board. Minni's bet of 325 into a 650 pot (half-pot) is a standard continuation bet from a button opener. My hand is strong enough to call one street to see if the turn improves my hand or if Minni slows down, especially given the 3:1 pot odds. Raising here would likely only be called by better hands (J-x, 8-x, or overpairs), and folding would be too tight against a wide button range.

The turn brings a Queen of hearts, which is an overcard to the board and significantly improves the range of a button raiser like Minni (who can easily have Q-x hands). My bottom pair of sixes with a King kicker has decreased in relative value. I will check to see how Minni responds. If she bets significantly on this street, I will likely fold as my hand is now very weak against two overcards and various straight draws.

I hold K♥3♥ on 6♣ 8♠ J♥ Q♥. I have no made pair but I have two hearts in hand and two hearts on board (a four-heart combo) — one card from a very strong flush — plus a King overcard. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop; Flash called and then checked the turn, showing weakness or pot control. This is a good spot to apply pressure as a semi-bluff: bet to fold out weak pairs and missed hands, charge draws, and build a pot I can still fold to heavy resistance. I’ll make a half‑pot bet (650 chips) to get fold equity but avoid overcommitting; if I face a sizable check‑raise I’ll release, if I’m called I’ll re-evaluate on the river (I have roughly 12 outs/raw ~26% to improve).

I have bottom pair (sixes) on a board that now contains two overcards (Jack and Queen). Minni, opening from the button, has a range that very strongly connects with this board, including many Queen-x and Jack-x combinations, as well as straight draws like T-9 or 9-7. My hand has very little equity to improve to the winner (only five outs to trips or two-pair, which might still be second-best) and practically no showdown value against a second barrel. Calling the half-pot bet of 650 is not justified given the board texture and the likelihood of a third barrel on the river.

2c Th is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low high card, and minimal playability. Even on the button with position in a likely limped pot (SB limp, BB check), calling 100 into a pot of ~250 offers decent odds but the hand makes too many weak hands postflop, gets dominated on tens, and has reverse implied odds. Raising to steal (min 200 or ~300 standard) risks calls from Minni's huge stack or Flash, who have shown loose tendencies, and this hand blocks little of value while performing poorly when called. Folding preserves chips for better spots in 3-handed play over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight of hearts, a solid starting hand in a three-handed game. The pot is currently 150 (my 50 and the Big Blind's 100), and I need to call 50 more. However, King-high suited is strong enough to play more aggressively from the Small Blind to put pressure on the Big Blind or build a pot with a hand that has good post-flop potential. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB and I’m in the BB with K♦8♣. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (total commit 300) — I’m getting ~33% pot equity. Versus a small-blind open I expect a fairly wide range, and K8o has reasonable showdown and implied odds with 77bb effective stacks. I don’t want to 3-bet light out of position; calling keeps weaker hands in and lets me see a flop to evaluate. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously OOP (fold to heavy action on A-high/dominating boards, barrel or value bet when I connect).

I am the pre-flop aggressor with King-Eight of hearts. The flop of 3-5-7 is quite low and potentially hits a Big Blind's calling range, but as the aggressor, I should still represent strength with a continuation bet. I have two overcards which give me some equity if called. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to take down the pot now or see how Minni reacts.

Flash c-bet 300 into a 900 pot on 3♠-5♠-7♣. I have K♦8♣ — no pair and no spade, but calling 300 into 900 gives ~25% pot odds (300/(900+300)=25%). vs a SB open the preflop range is pretty wide so I expect a fair number of missed hands and overcards; K8 has some showdown value and can be used to float OOP and try to take the pot away on a later street. Raising OOP with this hand is spewy and folding here surrenders too much equity given the price. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to large turn aggression unless I pair or pick up a clear bluffing line (or villain checks and I can apply pressure).

The turn card is the 6d, making the board 3-5-7-6. This is a highly connected board where any 4 now has a straight (3-4-5-6-7). My hand, Kh 8h, currently has King-high but has picked up an open-ended straight draw (needs a 4 or 9 to complete a straight). However, this board is much better for Minni's calling range from the Big Blind, as she is more likely to have 4s, 5s, 6s, or 7s than I am as the pre-flop raiser. Betting again here is risky because I could easily be raised or called by a better hand or a completed straight. I will check to control the pot size and hopefully see a free river card to realize my equity.

Board on the turn is 3♠-5♠-7♣-6♦ and I hold K♦8♣ — no pair, no spade and essentially K-high. Flash (SB) checked after c-betting the flop, which looks like weakness (missed spade draws, weak pairs, or pot-control with marginal made hands). With 1200 in the pot I have an opportunity to take it away now with a credible bluff. I’ll make a half-pot stab (600): big enough to fold out missed draws and many weak pairs, small enough to preserve stack and fold to a raise. Plan if action changes: fold to significant raise (I have essentially no equity), if called I proceed cautiously on the river — check/fold unless I pair or the runout gives a clear double-barrel bluffing spot. Bet 600 to apply pressure and pick up the pot now.

The turn card 6d creates a highly connected board (3-5-6-7). My hand, Kh 8h, currently has King-high and has picked up an open-ended straight draw (needs a 4 or 9). Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, which suggests she has a piece of this low board. After I checked the turn, she bet 600 into a 1200 pot. To call, I need 25% equity (600/2400). With 8 outs to a straight, my chance of hitting on the river is approximately 16%, which is below the direct pot odds. Furthermore, on this board, even hitting a straight might not guarantee the best hand if she holds a higher straight component (like an 8-9 for a nine-high straight or a 4-8). Given the board's texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range and my draw is not to the absolute nuts, I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having positional advantage, the hand has virtually no value and does not justify even a minimum raise or call. Given the stack depths and previous aggressive play from opponents like Minni, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I’m in the small blind with KsJs heads-up vs the big blind (Elon, ~63bb). Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call. KJs is a strong, playable heads-up hand and I prefer to take the initiative rather than limp or just call. Raising to 300 (about 3x the BB) accomplishes three things: it isolates Elon, gains fold equity against his wide defending range, and builds value from worse kings/jack-hands while keeping dominated hands from seeing a cheap flop. I’ll re-evaluate if he 3-bets, but standard open-raise to 300 is the +EV play here.

T2o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing a raise from the SB (Minni, who is very aggressive and has a massive stack), the pot odds of calling 200 into 400 (25% equity needed) are not favorable given my hand's equity against her likely wide but still stronger iso-raising range. No position postflop, and my stack is 6350 (63BB), so no need to defend this speculative hand. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

Three-handed and I'm on the button with Q♣2♦ — a weak holding but position is huge. Against only the SB and BB I should open a wide stealing range rather than limp. A minimum raise to 200 applies pressure, maximizes fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if I face resistance. If either blind 3-bets big I’ll let it go — Q2o plays poorly out of position and against a strong 3‑bet. If I'm called, I'll use position to continuation-bet favorable flops (queen or low, dry boards) and control pot size on dangerous textures.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 200 (small raise, pot 350, to call 150). My hand 5s7d is offsuit, gapped, low cards with minimal playability OOP. Poor implied odds, dominated by better hands in Minni's likely wide button range. Standard fold to preserve stack; no value in calling or 3-betting this trash hand. BB acts behind but doesn't change the math. Focus on better spots to build stack over 150 hands.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd 5d. Minni, who is currently the chip leader and has been quite active, raised to 200 from the button. The pot is 350 and it only costs me 100 more to call. Given my suited queen and the pot odds (3.5:1), this is a reasonable hand to defend the big blind against a button open. I want to see a flop and see if I can connect with a flush draw or a pair of queens.

I have flopped a flush draw with the Queen of diamonds. Standard play in the big blind against the pre-flop aggressor is to check and see how they react. I want to see the turn card as cheaply as possible, or potentially check-call to realize my equity. Minni is aggressive, so a check is the most strategic starting move on this flop.

Flop is 2♠-6♦-8♦ and I hold Q♣2♦ — a bottom pair (deuces) with a weak kicker but in position. Flash (BB) checked, and this board has obvious diamond draw equity and many overcards in his calling range. I want to charge flush draws and get value from worse pairs/overcards while keeping the pot controllable, so I’ll make a half‑pot c-bet. A bet of ~225 protects my hand and folds out some equity, but leaves room to fold to a large check‑raise; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (pot‑control vs diamonds/overcards, value bet thin on safe cards).

I have flopped a flush draw with an overcard (Queen) on a 2s 6d 8d board. Minni has bet 225 into a 450 pot, giving me 3:1 odds. My equity with the flush draw is roughly 35% by the river and about 19% for the turn. Given the pot odds and the potential for implied odds if I hit my flush against an aggressive chip leader like Minni, calling is the most profitable play here. It allows me to realize my equity without over-inflating the pot prematurely with a semi-bluff raise.

On the turn, the 7s significantly improves my hand's equity. I now have both a flush draw (9 outs) and an open-ended straight draw (8 outs). Accounting for the overlap (the 4d and 9d), I have 15 clean outs to a very strong hand. Being out of position against the aggressive chip leader, Minni, the most strategic play is to check. This allows me to see her action. Given my massive draw, I will certainly be continuing if she bets, but checking first maintains pot control and keeps my range protected.

Check. I have bottom pair (2♦) with a decent kicker (Q♣) but it’s a weak showdown hand heads‑up. I bet the flop to charge diamond draws and get value from worse, and Flash’s call there keeps a lot in his range: diamond draws, overcards, some floats and medium pairs (6s/8s/7s). The turn 7♠ is a relatively blank — it doesn’t complete the diamond draw and doesn’t significantly improve my holding. Against a caller who just checked the turn, pot‑control is the correct line: I avoid bloating a 900 chip pot with a marginal pair, keep rivers cheap to see if I improve or induce a bluff, and retain the option to call small bets or fold to a face‑up raise. If Flash bets small I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the river; if he checks back we go to showdown.

The board has paired 8s on the river (2s 6d 8d 7s 8s), and I have missed my flush and straight draws, leaving me with only Queen high. Minni checked back on the turn, which suggests she may have a marginal hand or a missed draw herself. However, given her massive stack and active playstyle, bluffing into her is risky, especially since any pair or even King high beats me. Since I have no showdown value, I will check. If she bets, I will likely fold, but checking allows for the possibility of a free showdown if she also missed.

I hold Q♣2♦ on 2♠-6♦-8♦-7♠-8♠ — that gives me two pair, eights and twos. The river paired the board and added a third spade, so my hand is reasonably strong vs missed draws and floats but very vulnerable to: any 8 (trips or full houses with a backdoor pair), any 6 (eights+6 two‑pair that beats me), or any two‑spade combo that makes a spade flush. Flash’s line (BB: call pf, call my c‑bet on the flop, check turn, check river) looks passive and is consistent with missed diamond draws, underpairs or overcards/flatting hands rather than a thin value shove. I could bet small to extract from some worse hands, but a value bet is thin and opens me up to a check‑raise or a call from better hands; fold equity is minimal. With a medium pot (900) and a deep stack, pot control is the prudent EV‑maximizing line — avoid bloating the pot with a marginally vulnerable two‑pair and take the showdown. I check to go to showdown (and would call a small river bet but respect a large check‑raise).

Preflop on the button with KJo, a strong hand in position against blinds. Pot is 150, blinds posted (SB 50, BB 100), to call 100. Standard play is to raise for value/isolation/steal. Raising to 3x BB (300 total) is standard sizing. KJ has good playability postflop in position, blockers to strong hands. Opponents have folded to button raises in history. Deep stacks but positional advantage key. No fold or limp needed.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack of Clubs and Eight of Diamonds. The button (Elon) has opened for a 3x raise to 300. This hand is a bit weak to be calling out of position against a button raise, especially with a deep-stacked aggressive player in the Big Blind yet to act. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I’m in the big blind with 7h2h — essentially a junk hand, but heads-up vs a Button open to 300 (pot 450). To call I need to put in 200 more, making my total committed 300. Pot odds on a call: 200 to win 650 → ~23.5% equity required. Elon has been opening ~3bb frequently from the button, so his range is likely wide. Despite being out of position and having a very weak hand, the suited 7-2 has some backdoor equity and reasonable equity vs a wide opening range (roughly in the ~30% area), so a defend by calling is a small +EV play. A reraise with this holding would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and limited fold equity against a 60bb effective stack, and folding would be too tight heads-up given the cheap price. I’ll call to see a flop, then play cautiously OOP — often checking or folding to heavy aggression on dry boards and only continuing with clear equity or good bluffing spots.

Flop is 4c 9h 3h and I’m OOP with 7h2h — I have a strong four-flush (two hearts in hand + two hearts on board). I have 9 outs to a heart (≈19% to hit on the turn, ≈35% to hit by the river). Pot is 650 and there’s no bet yet, so betting OOP as a semi-bluff is an option, but checking preserves stack, lets Elon c-bet with a wide range, and gives me cheap odds to see the turn. Against a wide button opening range I expect many hands that I can realize equity against (overcards, one-pair hands, single hearts). Plan: check and call reasonable small continuation bets to realize my flush equity; fold to heavy raises that offer poor pot odds or that indicate a very strong hand. If Elon checks back I get to see a free turn to continue. Checking keeps options and controls the pot size — I’ll check and reevaluate on the turn.

Flop of 4c 9h 3h misses my KJ completely: no pair, no draw, just overcards. However, as the preflop raiser in position against a single BB caller who checks, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. The board has a heart flush draw but is otherwise low and uncoordinated, capping strong made hands. Minni's calling range is wide (loose history of calling raises), including Ax, suited connectors, pairs below 9, etc., many of which she might fold to pressure. A small c-bet ~1/3 pot (225) applies fold equity, denies equity to draws/improvers, and builds pot if she has a weak 9 or Ax. If called, I have position to continue or fold turn. Checking risks giving free cards to her draws/overcards. Deep stacks favor aggressive play from position. Long-term EV positive.

Call 225. I have 7h2h on 4c 9h 3h — a 9-card heart draw (≈19% to hit on the turn, ≈35% by the river). The pot is 875 and calling 225 requires ~20.5% equity (225/(875+225)), so my draw + implied odds vs a wide BTN opening range is +EV. Elon’s bet is small, indicating a wide c-bet range; raising OOP with no pair would be a thin semi-bluff with poor blockers and unnecessary commitment against a competent opponent. Folding is too tight given the cheap price. Plan: call to realize equity, re-evaluate on the turn — if I hit a heart I’ll extract value; if I miss and face heavy turn/river aggression I’ll fold unless offered correct pot odds or a clear bluffing line.

I just completed a five-card heart flush (7h2h on 4c 9h 3h 10h). Pot is 1,100 and there's currently no bet to me. Elon has been opening wide from the button and c-bet the flop very small, so his range contains lots of one-pair hands (9x, Tx, 4x), weaker hearts and some broadways — many of which will pay a reasonable bet. Checking now surrenders value and lets him see a free river; I should bet to extract value and protect against giving free cards. I choose a value/protection sizing of ~2/3 pot (700). This size is large enough to get called by worse pairs and thin hearts but not so large that only the very small number of hands that beat me (primarily A♥) will continue. Plan: if he calls I re-evaluate the river for additional value; if he raises small I will likely continue (he’s polarized to strong hearts or bluffs); if he jams a very large amount I’ll re-assess against the range (folding only to clear polarization to better nut hearts or extremely unlikely full houses), but in general I prefer to extract now rather than check back.

Facing a turn bet of 700 into 1800 after Minni checked flop and called my small c-bet on a dry 4c9h3h board. Turn Th completes many heart flush draws, adds straight draw possibilities (e.g., JQ, 5-6 for lower straights), and pairs tens. My KJo is king-high with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), and minimal equity against her range. Her range for check-calling flop includes weak pairs, Ax, draws; but leading turn after improvement card suggests value (Tx, 9x, two-pair, sets) or strong semi-bluff (missed heart turning strong draw). Pot odds are 700 to win 2500 (~28% needed), but equity is far lower (~10-15% tops vs polarized range). History shows Minni calls loosely but donks turn aggressively with strength (e.g., Hands 3,10,48). Position allows control, but no hand to realize equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots; long-term EV positive to fold trash here.

I am on the button with 7c 3c. This is a very weak suited hand. While playing in a 3-handed game allows for a wider opening range, 73s is generally not profitable to open, especially with Minni (the chip leader) in the small blind who has shown considerable aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

We are heads-up (only the big blind left) and hold 3♦4♥ — a weak but playable hand in HU where open-raising ranges are very wide. With a deep stack (27k) and only one opponent, I want to take the initiative and use position post-flop. A standard 2xBB open to 200 (total committed) gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. If Elon 3-bets big or shoves, I can fold and preserve my large stack; if he calls I can play post-flop with position and c-bet or pot-control depending on the board. Therefore I raise to the minimum open of 200 to steal the blind and seize initiative.

Preflop in the big blind, facing a raise to 200 from Minni in the small blind after Flash folds. My hand 2s9s is weak suited gappers with low playability. Against Minni's likely wide raising range from SB, my equity is poor (~30-35% at best), and I'll be out of position postflop, making it hard to realize equity even with suited potential for flush or straight draws. Pot odds are 3:1 (call 100 to win 300), but the implied odds are insufficient given the hand strength and my shorter stack (5675). Minni has been aggressive in prior hands, often raising light from early positions, but folding trash here preserves stack for better spots in a long 150-hand game. Standard fold.

I have pocket eights on the button with deep stack (27,375) facing only the blinds and 100 to call. Standard, profitable play is to open-raise from the button to 3x the big blind to isolate, build a pot with a medium pair, and take the initiative with position. A raise to 300 is above the minimum (200), pressures blind defenders' wide ranges, and keeps the pot manageable versus the mid stacks (Elon 5,625, Flash 6,850). Plan: if called I'll c-bet favorable flops; if met with a large 3-bet I'll re-evaluate (fold to very large 3-bets, call/squeeze depending on sizing and reads).

6c3s is an extremely weak offsuit hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Facing a standard raise to 300 from the aggressive Minni on the button, who frequently raises wide based on hand history. As SB OOP with a short-ish stack relative to pot commitment, folding preserves chips for better spots. No fold equity or implied odds justify calling or 3-betting.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me roughly 2.25:1 odds. T9o is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind against a Button open, especially considering Minni's frequent activity. Although I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds justify a call to see a flop.

I am out of position in a heads-up pot against the pre-flop aggressor. I have missed the flop entirely with my Ten-Nine offsuit, as the board is King-high with no straight or flush draws for my specific cards. Standard play here is to check to the aggressor (Minni) and see how she reacts. I have very little equity and will likely fold to any significant bet, but checking is the mandatory first step.

Flop: 3d Kh 4h. I hold 8s8d — middle pair but there's a K on board. I raised preflop and have position, and the BB’s calling range is wide (suited connectors, broadways, small pairs, some Kx). This texture is relatively dry and misses a lot of that range, so a continuation bet will get value from worse pairs and fold out pure air/draws while keeping the pot controllable. I don’t want to build a huge pot with a vulnerable pair, so I’ll c-bet a small-to-medium size to extract and apply pressure. Bet 250 (~35–40% pot). Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn (control pot vs. passive calls, fold to significant aggression since many check-raises here denote Kx or nutted hands). If checked to me, take the initiative.

I have completely missed the flop with Ten-Nine offsuit on a King-high board. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet. Given that I have no pair and no meaningful draws, there is no reason to continue in this hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

AQo is a premium hand from the button in a 3-handed pot. With position on both blinds, raising is standard to take initiative, build the pot, and potentially steal the blinds or play post-flop advantageously. Opponents have shown tendency to call raises but also fold often. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is appropriate size given history of similar opens.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 9s, a very weak hand. Elon, on the Button, has raised to 300. Playing such a weak hand out of position against a button raise is generally a losing strategy. It's best to fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a button open to 300 from Elon while I’m in the big blind with 8h4s. This is a weak, unsuited, poorly connected hand that will be largely dominated by a typical BTN raising range and plays terribly out of position. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call, which requires about 31% equity to break even; 8-4o’s equity vs a standard open is well below that. A light 3-bet bluff could be considered only against an extremely wide/fit-or-fold opponent, but Elon has shown regular preflop aggression and has a stack size that can call/reshape the pot. Fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am on the button with 8s 7d. In a 3-handed game, this is a borderline opening hand. Given Minni's aggressive history and large stack, and Elon's relatively short stack (56BB), opening with a weak offsuit connector like 87o risks getting 3-bet or called out of position. I prefer to fold here and wait for a more premium hand to get involved with.

I have K♦J♦ in the small blind heads-up vs the big blind (Elon). This is a strong, playable hand with suit and good blocking value; I want to take the initiative and isolate the single opponent rather than just calling out of position. Elon has ~56BB so he will defend fairly wide, but a standard 3x-ish raise gets fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if he calls. I will raise to 300 total chips (commit 300) — this is well above the min-raise, gives a healthy chance to take the pot preflop, and leaves plenty of stack to pressure postflop. Postflop plan: c-bet on dry/K/J-type flops for value and protection, be cautious on connected/flushy boards (where OOP is costly), and fold to heavy 3-bet/commitment without clear equity.

87 suited clubs is a strong speculative hand in the big blind facing a small blind raise to 300. Excellent pot odds: calling 200 into a pot of 400 gives about 3:1 direct odds, plus strong implied odds due to suited connector potential for straights, flushes, and two-pair. Post-flop, I have position heads-up against Minni. Minni plays aggressively, but this spot is standard for a call rather than a 3-bet or fold. Raising light risks too much stack (effective ~5700) with a hand that plays better multi-street. Folding wastes the odds. Plan to play post-flop aggressively if I hit.

I flopped top set (Kd on Kc Ks Ad) — an extremely strong hand against Elon's wide call preflop. His calling range includes many Kx, Ax and medium pairs; very few hands (AA, AK combos that contain both an A and K, or rare quads/full houses) actually beat me. This is a clear value-bet spot to charge worse Kx and single-A hands and to protect against the small number of turn-runner combos. Pot is 600; I want a sizing that gets thin value but doesn’t price out calling hands, so I’ll bet ~2/3 pot. If Elon raises very large I’ll re-evaluate (large flop raise often denotes full house/AA and I’ll proceed cautiously), but if he calls I’ll keep extracting on later streets.

Facing a flop bet of 400 into 1000 pot on Kc Ks Ad board with 8c7c suited, giving me a strong club flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity to improve). Excellent pot odds to call (400 into 1400 for 3.5:1), but OOP calling keeps me passive and allows her to realize equity cheaply on turn. Raising takes advantage of fold equity on this paired kings + ace board, which hits strong ranges hard (possible sets, two-pair, top pair good kicker). Minni's SB open + flop bet is wide, including bluffs/A-high, so she folds often to raise. Size to 1200 (3x her bet) balances value from worse made hands/draws and pressure. If called, pot builds for flush; stacks allow (5475 behind into ~2400 pot). Aggressive line consistent with pre-flop plan to play post-flop strongly when hitting.

I flopped top set of Kings (Kd Jd on Kc Ks Ad) — an incredibly strong hand; only thin combinations beat me (mainly AA and AK, roughly 3+2 combos). Elon called preflop with a wide-ish range, so his flop raise to 1,200 can be both value and bluffs, but the portion of his range that actually dominates me is very small. Raising now to the min (2,000) would: (a) fold out many worse hands I want to extract value from, and (b) commit me to a bigger pot where the narrow value hands that beat me can shove and create a difficult decision. Calling keeps weaker Kx/Ax and bluffs in, controls the pot out of position, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn based on his action and the card. I will therefore call the raise (total 1,200 on the flop — I need to add 800 more) and plan to extract more on safe turns or fold to extreme, polarized action that clearly represents AA/AK.

I have a very strong hand (top set of Kings) on Kc-Ks-Ad 5d. Very few hands beat me (mainly AA and AK); most of Elon's flop-raising/calling range is behind. With pot ≈3000 I want to extract value while denying a free river and keeping worse A/K hands and bluffs in. A ~2/3‑pot turn bet (2000) is large enough to charge Ax and weaker Kx, price out pure bluffs/draws, and still induce calls from the hands I beat. If he jams/raises big I’ll re-evaluate but calling is often correct vs his narrow value range.

Facing a large turn donk bet of 2000 into 5000 pot on a KcKsAd5d board with 8c7c, giving me a strong club flush draw (10 outs, ~22% to hit river directly, but higher effective equity with implied odds). Minni's line (SB open, flop donk-call vs my raise, now massive turn donk) is polarized: strong value like Kd x (trips), AK/AQ (two-pair/top pair), or sets/full houses unlikely but possible; or draws/bluffs. My flop raise represented strength, and folding now wastes that investment and equity. Calling OOP is poor: commits 2000 (47% pot) leaving 2275 (~half pot), vulnerable to river shove if miss, and poor playability. Raising all-in to 4275 maximizes fold equity against her worse draws (e.g., Ad x, diamond draws post-5d), gets value from trapped hands like AK/AQ, and if called, pot-committed anyway with live draws. Stacks ideal for shove (~0.85 SPR post-call). Minni aggressive but folds to pressure in history (e.g., Hand 10, 34); this applies max pressure. +EV vs her SB wide range.

I have K♦J♦ on K♣ K♠ A♦ 5♦ — top set of Kings. Elon's line (calling preflop, raising the flop, then shoving the turn over my 2,000) is strong and polarized, but the combinations that currently beat me are narrow (mainly pocket Aces and AK combos that contain both A+K). I also block some of those combos by holding K♦. The pot is 9,275 and it costs me 2,275 more (total commitment 4,275) — I only need ~19–20% equity to make a +EV call. My set has substantially more equity than that against a realistic shove range (Kx, single-Ax, some bluffs/semi‑bluffs), so folding this is too tight. Elon appears to be all‑in, so raising isn't meaningful. I will call to realize equity and extract value from worse Kx/Ax and bluffs.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve posted the 50 SB) with 5♥4♠ — a marginal but playable hand in position. The pot is 150 and it costs me only 50 to see the flop (putting 100 total in). Raising to 200 is an option to try to steal, but against a shorter stack (Flash ~6.5k) a raise may induce a shove and I don’t want to commit a large amount with 54o. Calling gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds and lets me use position to realize equity or apply pressure on favorable flops. Plan: complete to see the flop, play post‑flop based on texture (c‑bet small on wet boards when appropriate, check/realize equity or bluff later if folds to aggression).

I have been dealt pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My opponent, Minni, has just completed the small blind. Given her massive stack and previous aggressive plays, I want to build a pot immediately and extract value. A standard raise from the big blind over a limp is around 3-4x the big blind, but given the dynamic, a raise to 400 is a solid way to start building the pot and define her range.

Call 400. I’m on the button with 5♥4♠ — a marginal but playable hand in position. Facing a raise to 400 I must put in 300 into a 500 pot (≈37.5% break‑even equity). 5‑4o doesn’t have enough raw equity to justify a light 3‑bet (which could induce a shove) nor is folding ideal given position and implied odds. Calling preserves my positional advantage to realize equity, bluff selectively, and extract value on favorable flops. Post‑flop plan: c‑bet small on favorable/dry flops when it credibly represents a hand, otherwise check and control the pot or bluff later if the texture and reads allow.

I have an overpair with pocket Kings on a relatively dry flop of 9-3-2. This is a very strong position. I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential spade draws or overcards, although overcards are unlikely given I hold two Kings. A bet of 400 (half the pot) is a standard continuation bet to extract value from 9x hands, middle pairs, or flush draws.

Call. Pot is 1,200 and Flash has bet 400 — calling 400 requires ~25% equity (400/(1,200+400)). With 5♥4♠ on 9♠-3♣-2♠ I have a two‑card straight draw (A or 6 = 8 clean outs, ~31% to hit by river), plus additional weak pair outs and a tiny backdoor spade possibility. Flash’s small c-bet size suggests a wide range, so this hand has enough equity to continue in position. Raising here risks a shove from a shorter-stacked opponent and bloats the pot with a marginal holding, so I’ll call, keep the pot controlled, and re-evaluate on the turn: barrel/raise only if I improve or see clear weakness, fold to heavy aggression if I miss and the opponent jams.

I have an overpair with pocket Kings on a board that hasn't changed much with the 7h. I am first to act and should continue to bet for value against 9x hands, smaller pocket pairs, and spade draws. A bet of 800 (half the pot) is a standard value bet that keeps the pot growing while offering a price that draws might still call.

Call 800.

Pot is 2,400 so calling 800 requires ~25% equity (800 / (2,400+800)). With 5♥4♠ on 9♠-3♣-2♠-7♥ I have 8 clean straight outs (A or 6) — 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit on the river — plus 6 raw pair-outs (fives and fours) that add some showdown equity but are not “clean” winners versus a value-heavy range. Flash’s line (preflop open, flop c-bet, turn follow-up) is polarized heads‑up — it contains both value and bluffs — and I have positional advantage to see the river. Folding is too conservative given the pot odds and my position; raising as a semi‑bluff risks getting jammed or bloating the pot with just a draw against a shorter stack.

Plan: call and keep the pot controlled. If I hit the straight on the river I’ll look to extract value; if I pair or miss I’ll evaluate river action and fold to heavy aggression (or attempt a targeted bluff only if the river and stack dynamics make it believable).

I have pocket Kings on a board of 9-3-2-7-2. The river deuce is generally a safe card, as it is unlikely Minni is calling a 4x raise pre-flop and two streets of betting with a 2 in her hand, unless it's exactly A2s or something similar. My overpair remains very strong value. Minni has called every street so far, suggesting she has a piece of the board, likely a 9 or a middle pocket pair. I will bet 1600 (half the pot) to extract maximum value from these hands while still being able to fold if she were to shove over the top, though a fold would be difficult given the pot size.

Board: 9♠-3♣-2♠-7♥-2♦. My hand 5♥4♠ has missed completely — no pair, no backdoor. Pot is 4,800 and Flash bet 1,600; calling requires ~25% equity (1,600 / (4,800+1,600) ≈ 25%). I only beat pure bluffs (missed spade draws/air). Flash’s line (preflop open, flop c-bet, turn barrel, river bet) is textbook value-heavy in heads-up play and the river pairing the board (second deuce) actually makes more value combos (2x, 9x, 7x, trips/boats) credible. I don’t put him bluffing at the needed >25% frequency, and I have essentially no clean outs. Raising as a bluff is very thin — he still has 3,400 behind and is likely to call or jam with many hands that beat me, so that’s a high-variance, low-expected-value play. Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.
I am on the button with A4o in a heads-up situation against Minni. A4o is a strong hand to open with from the button. I'll raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot.

Heads-up vs the button’s open to 300. My hand 9♦2♦ is very weak but suited. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) is cheap given the pot (good pot odds ~3:1) and heads-up opening ranges are wide — I can realize equity and use positional/adaptive post‑flop play. 3‑betting with this junk is pure bluff and unnecessary; folding is too tight for the price. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously — fold to heavy aggression if I miss, or continue/commit only with meaningful improvement (pair, strong draw, or two‑pair+).

Flop: 9♠ Q♠ A♥, my hand 9♦2♦ — I have a middling made hand (pair of nines) but I'm out of position and the board has two overcards (A, Q) that beat me. Flash opened wide preflop, so he has many bluffs and a lot of Ax/Qx combos that are ahead, but I also have decent showdown value versus missed broadways and pure bluffs. Out of position with a one-pair hand I want to control the pot and gather information rather than build a big pot myself. Checking keeps options open: I can call a small-to-medium continuation bet for value, fold to heavy aggression, and re-evaluate on the turn. If Flash checks back I can take a free card/lead on later streets. Therefore I check to control pot size and see how he reacts.

I have top pair with an Ace on a flop of 9s-Qs-Ah. While my kicker (4) is weak, I should bet for value and to protect against potential spade draws or straight draws (like J-10). Minni checked to me, so I will continuation bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain initiative and see where I stand.

Heads‑up OOP on A♥ Q♠ 9♠ with 9♦2♦: I have a single pair (nines) with a very weak kicker but solid showdown value against a wide button opening range. Flash’s bet is small (300 into ~900), which indicates a wide c‑bet range containing many missed broadways and pure bluffs as well as Ax/Qx combos that beat me. Pot math: the pot is ~1200 after his bet; calling 300 to potentially win ~1500 gives roughly 5:1 pot odds (need ≈17% equity) — well within what my pair achieves versus his bluff/missed portions of the range. Raising OOP with a marginal one‑pair and bad kicker is a losing line here and folding is too tight for the price. Plan: call to control pot size, keep bluffs and worse hands in, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to big turn aggression if I don’t improve, call small turn bets, and extract value only when appropriate.

Check. I have bottom pair (9♦) with a very weak kicker on A♥ Q♠ 9♠ J♥ and I’m out of position vs a wide, aggressive opener. There’s no turn bet yet, so checking controls the pot and keeps his bluffs and weaker hands in the range. Leading here OOP is thin value and just exposes me to raises from A/Q/J hands that currently dominate me. If Flash bets small (e.g. ~300 into the 1,200 pot) I’ll call — the pot odds (~5:1) and my pair justify a call versus a wide c-bet range. If he fires a large bet or raises, I’ll fold — my kicker is too weak and I’m unlikely to be ahead. If he checks back, I realize showdown value cheaply and can decide on the river. Plan: check, call small turn bets, fold to big aggression, re-evaluate on the river.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Ace-Four). The turn card is a Jack of Hearts, which is very coordinated with the rest of the board (9s Qs Ah). This card completes several potential straights (like K-T) and improves many of Minni's likely calling range from the flop (like Q-J, J-9, or J-T). Given the texture, betting again is risky as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands and could easily be facing a check-raise from a superior hand or a big draw. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate the river.

I have 9♦2♦ on 9♠ Q♠ A♥ J♥ 9♣ — rivered trips (three nines). In heads‑up play this is a very strong hand; only full houses/quads beat me (combinations like A9/Q9/J9/99 or AA/QQ/JJ). Flash’s line (button open, small flop c‑bet, then check on the turn) is a wide, somewhat passive line that contains many A/Q/J combos and bluffs that are currently behind my trips. The pot is 1,200 and his effective stack is 7,600, so betting for value is correct: I want to extract calls from top‑pair/two‑pair/Ax/Qx hands that checked back the turn. I’ll size up for value but not overcommit — a pot‑size bet pressures worse hands to call and keeps the raise‑to‑jam range polarized to the rare full houses/quads. If he makes a large raise it will almost always indicate a full house/quads and I will relinquish the pot. Action: bet 1,200 to maximize value from worse hands while keeping the raise frequency that beats me small.

On a board of 9s-Qs-Ah-Jh-9c, I hold Ac-4d, giving me two pair: Aces and Nines with a Queen kicker (from the board). Minni's lead of 1200 into the 1200 pot on the river is a polarizing move. While the 9c on the river is a scare card that could have given her trips (if she held a 9) or a full house (if she held Q9 or J9), it also counterfeits any lower two-pair hands she might have had, such as Q-J. Most of her Ace-x hands will result in a split pot with me, as we both would use the board's Queen as the fifth card, unless she holds A-K. Given that I have top pair on a board where many hands tie and only a few specific hands (a 9, KT, or AK) beat me, I believe my hand is strong enough to call a pot-sized bet in a heads-up situation, especially considering her potential to bluff with missed spade draws or straight draws.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve posted the 50 SB) with J♥7♠ — marginal but perfectly raiseable in HU. Button should be aggressive and open a wide range to take the initiative, steal the BB, and play position postflop. The pot is 150 and I can call 50 or raise; I’ll raise to 200 (total commit) — a standard 2xBB sizing that builds a pot when I have initiative but keeps it manageable. If Flash folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I have position and can c-bet most dry flops or proceed cautiously on coordinated boards. If he 3-bets large I’ll largely fold this marginal hand and re-evaluate based on sizing.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine suited, a strong hand in a heads-up scenario. Minni, on the button, has raised to 200. Given the strength of my hand and the need to build a pot or take the initiative, I will 3-bet to 600 to see where I stand and capitalize on my equity.

I opened the button wide to 200 and Flash responded with a 3‑bet to 600. The pot is 800 and calling 400 would make the pot 1,200, so I’d need roughly 33% equity to justify a call. J♥7♠ is a marginal, often‑dominated hand vs a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, broadways, AK), and even if I pair a Jack my kicker problems and reverse implied odds are real. Facing a standard 3‑bet size and a shorter effective stack, this isn’t a spot to get sticky — fold and wait for a better spot in position.

In a heads-up situation, the Small Blind (Button) has position post-flop and should play a wide range. However, Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o) is one of the weakest hands in the deck. While a raise might sometimes take the pot immediately, Minni has a massive chip lead and has shown aggression in previous hands. Folding this hand is the most disciplined play to avoid being outplayed with a marginal hand.

Heads-up on the button with A♣4♣ is a clear open-raise: suited ace with position is worth building the pot and taking the initiative. The pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to see a cheap flop, but a standard 2.5–3bb open will apply pressure and get value from worse aces/pairs and weak broadways. I’ll raise to 300 (3bb) rather than the bare minimum 200 to avoid giving the big blind good odds to float cheaply. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often fold to a big 3-bet; defend/call smaller 3-bets), but if he calls I can use position to c-bet many flops and extract value from dominated hands.

I hold KJo in the big blind against a standard button raise from Minni. KJo is a strong hand in a heads-up scenario and is well ahead of a typical button opening range. While I am out of position, calling is a solid play to keep the pot manageable and see a flop, rather than 3-betting and potentially facing a 4-bet from the chip leader.

I have King-High on an Ace-high board with two hearts. I'm out of position and the flop doesn't favor my hand. I will check to see how the opponent reacts. If they bet, I'll likely fold unless the bet is very small, as my hand lacks showdown value and has limited drawing potential here.

Flop: 6h Ad 9h — I have top pair (A♣4♣) with a weak kicker on a two-heart board. I opened preflop and Villain (BB) called, so his range is wide (pairs, broadways, some aces, suited connectors and heart draws). He checks, so a value/protection c-bet is standard: it extracts value from worse aces, 9x/6x and gets called by heart draws while charging equity. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (300) — big enough to deny free cards to heart draws and get value, but small enough to keep worse hands and draws in. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I control pot on turn unless it improves him (heart or a card completing two-pair/sets) — if he check-raises large I’ll strongly consider that he’s very strong and will re-evaluate (likely fold to a big repushy), but I will call modest resistance to continue with top pair.

I have King-high on an Ace-high flop with a flush draw present. Minni, who is the chip leader and has been aggressive, has made a half-pot continuation bet. I do not have a pair, a flush draw, or any significant straight draw. Calling out of position with just King-high is a losing play in the long run, as I have very little equity and limited ways to improve to the best hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fours. In a heads-up situation against Minni, who has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively, raising is the standard play to take control of the hand and define the range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take the blinds down immediately, or play a controlled pot if called.

I have 4♦2♠ — a very weak offsuit hand. Flash raised to 300 from the button (SB). The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total committed), so calling requires ~33% equity (200 to win a 600 pot). Against a typical wide SB open in heads-up my hand has some showdown chances but is often dominated. I don't want to 3-bet as a bluff with such a limp hand — no useful blockers and I’ll be out of position post-flop, plus a raise could induce a shove or a call that leaves me in a tough spot. Folding is too tight heads-up. So I’ll call to see a flop and re-evaluate: I’ll fold to significant aggression if I miss, but continue if I pair or pick up meaningful draws.

I have 4♦2♠ and paired my deuce on a K♥ J♣ 2♦ flop — a very weak, low pair with a poor kicker. Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so his range includes lots of Kx/Jx, broadways and air. The flop is fairly dry and betting here will often get called by better hands (K/J/Tx) while folding out only pure air; I don’t want to build a big pot with a marginal hand out of position. I’ll check to control the pot and allow opponent to bluff or barrel; I’ll call small-to-medium C-bets for showdown value but fold to large, polarizing raises or jams. If Flash checks back I’ll reevaluate on the turn and look for cheap showdown or a small value bet on a safe card.

I hold a pocket pair of fours on a flop of K-J-2. This board is quite unfavorable for my low pair, as there are two overcards (King and Jack). Minni has checked to me. While I could bet to represent a King or a Jack, Minni's calling range often includes these cards, and she has a massive chip lead which allows her to float or check-raise. Checking back keeps the pot small, allows me to see a turn for free, and avoids getting blown off my equity if she decides to raise. I will check to maintain pot control.

Pot is 600 and I hold 4♦2♠ — a very weak pair of deuces with a terrible kicker on a K♥ J♣ Q♥ board. Flash opened wide preflop and checked the flop, and he checked the turn as well. Against his wide button range this turn card improved lots of hands (Kx/Jx/Qx, broadways) so I’m very often behind. Betting out OOP with such a thin value hand would bloat the pot and get called by better hands or shoved on by polar hands; I also have poor blockers to make a successful bluff. I want to control the pot and keep the showdown price low, allowing him to bluff away some equity. I’ll check here and plan to call small-to-medium river bets for showdown but fold to large, polarizing bets or shoves.

I hold a pocket pair of fours on a board of King-Jack-Deuce-Queen. This is a very coordinated board that hits a significant portion of a standard defending range. Minni has checked for a second time, which could indicate weakness, but it could also be a trap given how many pairs and straight draws are possible. With only a low pair and no real draw to improve, my hand has almost no showdown value, but betting into this board is risky as Minni is unlikely to fold any piece of the board or a Ten. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and keeps the pot small, adhering to a pot-control strategy with a marginal-at-best hand.

The river (2♦ K♥ J♣ Q♥ K♦) gives me 4♦2♠ — my best five is two pair, Kings and Twos. Unfortunately the board is paired with two Kings, so any K in Flash’s range is trips and beats me; Qx/Jx (or many broadways he opened with) make KQ/KJ two pair which also beat my K2. Flash raised wide preflop but checked flop and turn, a line that can be either a slow-played strong hand or a missed/medium holding; betting now has very little fold equity against the hands that beat me and will only get called by better or fold out pure air. With a vulnerable two-pair and the pot small relative to stacks, the lowest-variance +EV play is to check and go to showdown (and only call a small river bluff).

The board has developed into K-K-Q-J-2. My pocket pair of fours is now a very weak hand, as my best five-card combination is K-K-Q-J-4. Almost any card in Minni's range that is higher than a four (5, 6, 7, 8, 9, T, or any A, J, Q, K) will beat me. Minni has checked for the third time, but since she has a massive chip lead, she may be willing to call a small bluff with any Ace-high or better. Since I have almost no showdown value and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against her stack, I will check and concede the pot if she has a better hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB). 4h7h is suited and has good postflop playability and blocker/flushing potential — in heads-up I should open a very wide range. Folding is far too tight and just calling 50 is passive (gives the BB great odds to see a flop and surrenders initiative). I’ll make the minimum legal raise to 200 (total) to apply pressure, keep my range wide, and preserve pot control. That sizing buys fold equity against weaker BB holdings while still allowing me to play postflop in position if called; with Flash’s shorter stack (≈6450) I can exploit folds and postflop edges. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, use position to realize equity or apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the button, has made a minimum raise to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds. Q9o is a strong enough hand to defend against a button open, especially at these odds. I will call to see a flop.

I have flopped top pair with a 9 kicker on a very dry board (Q-7-2 rainbow). While my hand is strong, I am out of position against a very active and deep-stacked opponent. Checking is the standard play here to control the pot and see how Minni reacts. My hand is a very strong candidate for check-calling to induce bluffs or get value from second-best hands.

I have 7♥4♥ on Q♥ 7♠ 2♣ — second pair with a backdoor heart and position. I opened preflop, Flash called with a wide BB calling range, and he checked the flop, showing either weakness or pot-control. This is a very good spot to c‑bet: a half‑pot bet extracts value from worse pairs, many Kx/Ax and draws, denies free cards, and folds lots of pure air. I’ll bet a standard half‑pot to get value and protect my hand while keeping the pot controllable; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll give credit to Qx/sets and proceed cautiously, if called I’ll reevaluate on the turn.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on an extremely dry board. Minni's 200 bet into 400 is standard. While my hand is strong, raising here likely only gets called by better queens (AQ, KQ, JQ) or folds out her bluffs. By calling, I keep her range wide and can re-evaluate on the turn, while also controlling the pot size out of position.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on an extremely dry board. After check-calling on the flop to control the pot, the eight of hearts on the turn is a relatively safe card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. I will continue with my pot-control strategy by checking again. This allows me to keep Minni's bluffing range in play and avoids building a large pot out of position with a medium-strength top pair. If Minni bets, I plan to call and re-evaluate on the river.

I have 7♥4♥: second pair (sevens) with two hearts in hand and two hearts on board (one heart away from a flush). I was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop; Flash called and then checked the turn, which looks like pot‑control or weakness/draws. I want to charge heart draws and get value from worse pairs (deuces, small pairs, weak K/Ax) while keeping the pot controllable against likely Qx hands that beat me. The pot is 800, so a half‑pot turn bet (≈400) is the right balance: protects my hand, folds out pure air, and keeps many worse hands and draws in. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll give credit to stronger made hands and proceed cautiously (fold to polarized aggression); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a relatively safe board. Minni's turn bet is only one-third of the pot, which is a sizing that could represent a wide range, including weaker queens, middle pairs, or draws. Raising would likely only force out hands I beat and get me into trouble against stronger queens (AQ, KQ, QJ). By calling, I maintain pot control while keeping her range wide and allowing her to continue bluffing on the river.

The river has delivered an Ace, which is a significant scare card for my top pair of Queens. Minni has been betting consistently on the flop and turn. This Ace connects well with many hands in a button-opening range (like AQ, AJ, AT, or AK). By checking, I am continuing my pot-control strategy. If Minni has an Ace or a better Queen, she will likely bet for value. If she has a hand I beat, like a lower pocket pair or a missed draw, she will likely check back. Leading out would only serve to get me called by better hands and force out the bluffs I am hoping to catch. I will check and decide whether to call based on the size of her bet, if she makes one.

I have 7♥4♥ on Q♥ 7♠ 2♣ 8♥ A♦ — second pair (sevens) with no flush (two hearts in hand + two on board). I was the aggressor (c‑bet flop, bet turn) and Flash called both streets, then checked the river. Given his willingness to call flop and turn, his calling range contains a lot of Qx, 7x, medium pairs and some Ax — many of which beat my pair of sevens. The river A♦ further props up parts of his range (Ax) and makes a thin river bluff less likely to get through. Pot is 1600: a small river bet (e.g. 400) only requires ~20% equity to call, so it will be called by many hands that beat me and will fold mostly air — a poor value/bluff spot versus a sticky caller. With little fold equity and limited value, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-return line is to check and go to showdown.

I am on the Button (SB) with J3s. This is a playable hand in a heads-up or short-handed situation. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop, or at least define the range of the Big Blind. Raising is generally better than just completing the blind to maintain pressure, especially since I have been relatively aggressive in previous hands.

Facing a button (Flash) open to 300 from the small blind, the pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (i.e. 300 total) to call — about 3:1 pot odds, so I need ~25% equity to justify a call. Ten-deuce offsuit is a weak hand, but heads-up the BB should defend a wide range and the price to see a flop is reasonable. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff to the minimum (500) with this hand because the raise is small, will get called a lot, and leaves me OOP postflop with a dominated hand against a competent opponent; I’d rather avoid bloating the pot out of position.
