Plan: call to see the flop, and be prepared to fold to significant continuation aggression unless I pair (T or 2), make a strong draw, or otherwise improve.

I called preflop to see a flop and my original plan was to fold to heavy C‑bet unless I paired or picked up a strong draw. I paired the board (2d with the 2h) but it’s a very weak pair on an A‑high flop and I’m out of position versus an SB who opened wide. There are lots of A‑x and A3/A2 combos that dominate me, and the two diamonds on board give them backdoor/flush possibilities too. Betting here would bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand and get called or raised by many better hands — so I will check and control the pot. Plan: check and call reasonable small/medium continuation bets (I’ll call standard C‑bets to see a turn because I have showdown value and a backdoor diamond), but fold to large bets or check‑raises unless I improve to two‑pair/trips or pick up a strong draw on the turn.

I have middle pair (threes) on an Ace-high board after being the pre-flop aggressor. While my pair has some showdown value, a continuation bet is effective here to represent an Ace and put pressure on Minni's range, which might have missed or consists of lower pairs/draws. I will bet 300 to maintain the initiative.

I have a weak pair (2s) with a T kicker and one diamond on an A‑3‑2 (Ad,3d,2h) flop. Flash is the SB who opened wide and has bet a relatively small c‑bet (300). The pot odds are roughly 3:1 (pot 900, to call 300), so a call is justified: I have showdown value against bluffs/weak Axs and feasible ways to improve (trip 2s, a T gives me two pair, and there’s a remote runner‑runner diamond backdoor). I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a bluff 3‑bet; calling keeps his wide range in and lets me realize equity. Plan: call now to see the turn, fold to a large raise or over‑aggression if I don’t improve, call small/medium turn bets for showdown value, and only get aggressive if I make two‑pair/trips or pick up a real draw.

I hold bottom pair (2♦) with a Ten kicker and I’m out of position versus a wide SB opener who c‑bet the flop. The 5♥ on the turn doesn’t help me — it largely favors Ax and better hands in his range and only gives me a very small chance to improve on the river. With stacks as they are and the pot already 1,200, I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal made hand. I’ll control the pot size and realize my showdown value: check and see what Flash does. Plan: call a small turn bet (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot) because his range has many bluffs and I get good pot odds, but fold to a large bet/raise since I’m rarely ahead and have poor improvement odds. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and only get aggressive if I improve or get a clear read to bluff.

On the turn, the 5h adds potential straights (any 4) to the board. Having checked to me, I have a pair of threes which has some showdown value but is unlikely to get called by worse if I bet again. Since Minni called the flop bet on an Ace-high board, her range likely contains an Ace, a draw, or a medium pocket pair. Checking back allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially see a cheap showdown or improve on the river.

Check. I have bottom pair (2♦) with a Ten kicker on Ad–3d–2h–5h–Ks. Flash opened wide preflop, c-bet the flop and then checked the turn, which signals a lot of missed Ax, single-pair bluffs, and marginal holdings in his range. My hand has modest showdown value vs bluffs/missed hands but is dominated by any A‑x and now loses to Kx that hit the river. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand, often get called by better hands (A/K pairs) or fold only the very weakest hands — it’s a poor thin‑value spot. Checking controls the pot and lets me realize equity; if Flash bets small (≈≤1/3 pot, ~≤400) I’ll call for good pot odds since many turn lines look weak, but I’ll fold to large river bets (>~1/2 pot) because I’m unlikely to be ahead. For now I check to a) avoid bloating the pot OOP and b) induce a bluff or get to showdown cheaply.

I have a pair of threes on a board with an Ace and a King. While I was the pre-flop aggressor and bet the flop, checking the turn showed some weakness. However, Minni has checked the river as well. My pair of threes has some showdown value against missed draws (like diamond draws or gutshots that didn't materialize), and it's unlikely I'll get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call if I bet. Checking back is the most prudent play to reach a cheap showdown.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) A♦T♠ is a strong, value-heavy hand with good postflop playability in position. Facing a single big blind and only 50 to call, folding or just calling is too passive — I want the initiative and to isolate the BB. A raise to 300 (a standard ~3x BB sizing seen at this table) gives me fold equity, builds the pot when I’m likely ahead of his calling range, and keeps postflop decisions straightforward with position and a hand that plays well vs a wide range. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate based on size and his tendency, but default is to raise here for value and pressure.

In this heads-up situation, I'm holding Td 3s in the Big Blind. Minni, who is in the Small Blind/Button position, has raised to 3x (300 total). While T3o is not the worst hand, it is definitely in the bottom tier of starting hands and doesn't play well post-flop, especially out of position against a player with a significant chip lead. Given that I need to be selective to preserve my stack for better spots, folding here is the most prudent play.

I am on the button with 8s 6d, a marginal hand. The pot is currently 150 and it costs me 50 to call or 200 to make a standard raise to 300. Given that Minni has a massive chip lead and has been playing aggressively, I should avoid getting into a large pot with a weak hand like 8-6 offsuit. However, in a heads-up or shorthanded situation, folding pre-flop for just 50 more chips is often too tight. That said, looking at the previous hands, Minni is quite active. I will choose to fold this hand to maintain my stack and wait for a better opening against a much larger stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with pocket 9s is a strong, standard open-raising spot. Flash (BB) has ~7,700 (≈77BB effective) so there’s plenty of postflop playability but I want to build the pot and isolate his wide defending range. A standard 3x BB open (raise to 300) extracts value from worse hands, gains fold equity vs too-wide defenses, and denies free cards that could outdraw my pair. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet favorable, control the pot on coordinated/overcard boards and re-evaluate vs aggression; vs a small 3-bet I can call and play postflop for value/stack, but I’ll fold or pot-control vs large 3-bets/4-bets depending on sizing. Raise to 300 keeps the initiative and maximizes long-term EV.

In a heads-up situation, I'm holding T5 offsuit in the big blind. Minni has a significant stack advantage and has made a standard 3x open to 300. T5o is a very weak hand with poor playability, even when getting 3-to-1 odds to call. Given my position and the hand's strength, I will fold and wait for a better spot to challenge the chip leader.

In a heads-up situation as the small blind, Queen-Seven suited is a standard opening hand. Minni has a massive chip lead, so I want to play cautiously but maintain basic aggressiveness. Raising to 300 is a standard open from the button to put pressure on the big blind and control the pot size pre-flop.

Facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from the button. I'm in the big blind with Tc9d — a playable, connected hand heads-up with good equity and postflop playability. I’ve already posted 100, so calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives me roughly 33% pot odds (200 into a 600-pot after my call). With deep stacks (my 322bb vs his ~74bb) I get decent implied odds and can realize equity on many flops. I prefer to defend wide heads-up rather than 3-bet this marginal hand out of position; 3-betting would bloat pots OOP and isn’t necessary without a specific read. Plan: call preflop, then proceed based on flop texture — check/control on dry boards, continue aggressively when I hit or pick up strong draws.

Heads-up out of position with Tc9d on Kc-8h-5c. I have no pair and only very weak backdoors (need both turn and river clubs or runner-runner 6/7 for a straight), so there’s no reason to build a pot OOP. Flash’s open-raising range contains a lot of Kx and broadways that connect with this board, so he’ll continuation-bet frequently. I’ll check to control the pot, call small-to-medium cbets to realize equity, fold to large overbets, and re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up a pair or a real draw.

On the flop of Kc-8h-5c, I have Queen-high. This board generally favors the pre-flop aggressor as I have all the strongest King-X combinations in my range. Minni checked, which is standard. A continuation bet here can often take the pot down immediately by putting pressure on her mid-range or drawing hands. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to represent a King or a strong draw while keeping the price reasonable if she decides to play back.

Heads-up out of position with Tc9d on Kc‑8h‑5c: I defended preflop intentionally and prefer to keep the pot manageable on a dry board. Flash’s c‑bet is small (300 into 900 ≈ 1/3 pot), which is a very wide continuation frequency heads‑up. Calling 300 gives me 300/(900+300)=25% pot odds. My chance to hit a pair (six outs: three tens + three nines) by the river is ~24% and the runner‑runner club possibilities add a few percent of extra equity — roughly ~28% total vs a random c‑betting range. That slightly exceeds my pot‑odds, and there are decent implied odds if I pair up against a wide c‑bet range. 3‑betting OOP with this hand is unnecessary (will bloat pots with poor fold equity); folding is too tight given the odds. Plan: call the small c‑bet, keep the pot controlled, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to a big turn raise unless I pick up a pair or a real draw, otherwise proceed for value/bluff as appropriate.

I defended preflop with Tc9d and called the small 1/3-pot c‑bet on Kc‑8h‑5c because I had a club flush draw. The turn (Kh) bricks my draw — I currently have no pair and only six outs (three tens + three nines), ~13% to improve on the river. Flash checked the turn; that line is consistent with both missed hands and slow‑playing Kx (which now becomes trips), so a large portion of his range that checks here will still call or raise a turn bet. Betting as a bluff is thin: I have poor fold equity, I don’t block many of his value combos, and risking chips with ~13% equity on the river is suboptimal. Pot is 1,200 and there’s no bet to call. I’ll keep the pot small and check behind to see the river for a cheap showdown (and fold to most sizeable river bets), preserving chips for better spots.

On the turn, a second King (Kh) appears, pairing the board. This card is generally better for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, but Minni has called a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet, indicating she likely has some piece of the board or a draw (like a club draw or a straight draw). Checking here is a prudent move; it allows me to realize my equity for free, prevents building a large pot with only Queen-high, and might induce a bluff on the river or allow me to get to a cheap showdown if I improve. If she checks again on the river, I can decide whether to bluff or check down based on the final card.

Final board 5c-8h-Kc-Kh-6s; my hand Tc9d has missed — no pair, no real showdown equity. Flash opened pre, made a small c-bet on the flop, then checked the turn and checked the river. That line can be either a wide missed c-betting range or some slow-played/medium Kx — but I don’t beat enough of his checking range and I don’t block his value hands. Betting as a bluff here has very little fold equity heads-up and opens me to a costly raise; calling a raise or continuing OOP with T-high is negative EV. Pot is 1,200 and there is no bet to call, so the lowest-risk, highest-expected-value line is to check and take the free showdown. I’ll check and fold to any river raise (consistent with my earlier turn plan).

On the river, the board is 5c-8h-Kc-Kh-6s. I have Queen-high. Minni has checked to me for the third time this hand (flop, turn, and river). While her check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw, her subsequent checks on the turn and river indicate weakness or a marginal made hand like an 8 or a 5. However, since I have no showdown value with Queen-high, the only way to win is to bluff. Given Minni's large stack and her tendency to call down occasionally, a bluff here is risky. I will check it back and take the loss to her likely pair or Ace-high, preserving my stack for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with Js4c — a marginal hand but perfectly fine to include in a wide HU opening range. I want to seize initiative and play the flop in position, and Flash (BB, ~82BB) is likely to fold a good portion of his blind-only range to pressure. I’ll open to a standard 3x (to 300 total) — big enough to generate fold equity and define ranges, but small relative to my deep stack so I keep postflop flexibility. If he calls I’ll C-bet or proceed based on texture; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold Js4c and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind holding 10d 8c, which is a decent but speculative hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600) and the fact that we are heads-up, this is a standard defend. I will call to see a flop and evaluate the hand strength then.

I have completely missed this flop with Ten-high. In a heads-up situation where I am out of position (acting first), checking is the most prudent play to see how the opponent reacts. I have two overcards, but they are not strong enough to lead out with a bet on this low board.

I have Js4c on 3s-4d-7s — middle pair (fours) with a decent jack-kicker and a backdoor spade. Villain in the BB called preflop, so his range is wide and contains many missed broadways and small pairs. As the preflop aggressor and being in position, I want to extract value from worse one-pair hands and charge any turn/river backdoor draws while maintaining fold equity. I’ll c-bet ~2/3 pot (400 into 600). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises big I’ll likely release since my hand is vulnerable to sets/2-pair and I don’t want to build a bloated pot out of position against a strong range.

I have completely missed the flop with Ten-high. The board of 3-4-7 is low and somewhat coordinated. Minni has placed a 400 bet into a 600 pot, which is a significant sizing (2/3 pot). Since I have no pair, no draw, and no backdoor equity to speak of, continuing would be a mistake. Floating out of position with air against a player who has a massive stack advantage and initiative is not a winning strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ace-Nine suited on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong starting hand. My standard opening raise size has been 300 throughout the session, and I will continue with that to build the pot and maintain aggression.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with K♠7♦ — marginal but definitely worth defending heads-up against a wide opening range. Calling 200 (total 300 committed) gives correct pot odds to see a flop (I’m calling 200 to win ~600 post-call) and keeps the pot manageable OOP. 3‑betting with K7o has little fold equity and isn’t a strong value hand, so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. Postflop plan: proceed straightforwardly — value/top-pair if I hit, otherwise check/fold to significant aggression and look to realize equity on favorable boards.

I have top pair K with a weak 7 kicker on a fairly dry Qc-6h-Kh flop. Versus a wide button open in heads-up, his range contains lots of worse Ks, Qx, 6x and occasional air/bluffs. Out of position I want to extract value and charge any backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable. Betting ~1/2 pot (300 into 600) gets value from worse Kx and many Q/6 pairs and denies free cards that could outdraw me. If called I’ll reassess the turn and continue for value on safe cards; if I face a large check-raise that polarizes to sets/better kings I’ll strongly consider folding.

I have Ace-Nine suited on a board of Qc-6h-Kh. I do not have a piece of this board, but I do have one overcard (the Ace) and a backdoor straight draw. Minni's lead of 300 into a 600 pot (half-pot) is a common 'donk bet' that can represent a weak King, a Queen, or even a straight draw like JT. While I don't have a made hand, calling is reasonable to see a turn card, as I still have some showdown value and potential to improve. However, against a persistent opponent, folding is also acceptable. Given the pot odds (300 to win 1200), I will call once to see if the turn improves my equity or if Minni slows down.

Check. I have top pair (K♠7♦) but a very weak kicker and I’m out of position. I c‑bet the flop and got called; the turn (8♥) brings a third heart and is a fairly scary card — it completes flushes for any two‑heart combos and helps some turn draws. Villain’s flop‑call range contains lots of worse Kx, Qx, 6x and draws, but also some two‑heart combos and sets that now play strongly. Betting again risks bloating the pot and putting me in tough spots versus hands that currently beat me or that will shove on later streets. I’ll exercise pot control: check the turn, plan to call modest-sized bets for value (against Qx/6x/weaker Ks) but fold to large check‑raises or big polarizing aggression.

Minni's check on the turn after leading the flop (donk bet) often indicates a marginal hand that wants to see a cheap showdown or a total air-ball that is giving up. My Ace-high has some showdown value in a heads-up pot, and since I have no made hand or strong draw, checking back is the most prudent play. This allows me to see the river for free and potentially improve to top pair or win at showdown if the board remains unchanged and Minni checks again.

I have top pair (K♠7♦) on Qc‑6h‑Kh‑8h‑2s. Flop I bet and was called; turn I pot‑controlled by checking because the 8♥ completed lots of heart draws. River 2♠ is a relatively harmless card (doesn't pair the board or add more hearts) and many hands in villain's calling range (weaker Kx, Qx, mid pairs, missed draws) will call a small value bet. Villain checked the turn which often indicates pot control with mediocre hands rather than a polar strong slowplay. With an effective stack of 7.4k against a 1.2k pot I want thin value while preserving fold equity vs strong hands — so I bet small (≈1/3 pot) to get called by worse and fold to a large raise. If villain check‑raises big I’ll assume a flush/two‑pair/set and fold.

On the river, the board is Qc-6h-Kh-8h-2s. Minni led the flop, checked the turn, and has now fired a 1/3 pot bet of 400 into 1200 on the river. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 400 to win 1600), my Ace-high is extremely weak on this texture. Minni's line often represents a marginal made hand like a Queen or a Six that checked the turn for control and is now betting for thin value or to prevent me from checking back a better hand. Since I have no pair and there are very few missed draws that I actually beat (most bluffs would have better than A9 or would have been played differently), I will fold.

Heads-up on the button with T♥3♥ — a marginal hand but perfectly playable in position. I want to take the initiative, pressure the big blind’s wide defending range, and leverage my positional advantage post‑flop. A standard heads‑up open should be fairly wide; T3s has backdoor flush/straight potential and can reasonably continue on many flops. I’ll open-raise to 300 (3x BB / total commitment 300) — big enough to fold out many very weak defenses and to discourage trivial 3-bets, but still keeps postflop playability against calls. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold or proceed cautiously (T3s is dominated vs a strong 3-bet range); if he calls I’ll use position to continuation-bet favorable textures and control the pot on unhelpful boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 2s, which is a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Given the wide range she's been opening and my current stack relative to hers, I could occasionally defend, but 8-high offsuit is simply too weak to profitably call a 3x raise out of position, especially against an opponent who has a significant chip lead and has shown aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Qs6d. This is a marginal hand, but in a heads-up situation, it's often worth a raise to apply pressure or at least a call to see a flop. However, looking at the history, Minni has been quite active and aggressive recently. Given the wide range of hands played in a heads-up format, I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot early and test Minni's response.

I’m in the big blind (100 posted) facing a standard heads-up SB open to 300. I have Kc3h — a marginal but playable heads‑up holding. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 to call (making my total commitment 300). Calling gives me 200 risk to win ~600 (pot after my call), so I need ~33% equity — reasonable versus a wide SB opening range. With deep effective stacks (opponent ~7k) I have implied odds to realize top‑pair hands and can play post‑flop. A tiny reraise to 500 would be non‑credible and can put me in awkward OOP spots; folding is too tight heads‑up. Plan: call, see the flop, play cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression with nothing. Therefore: call to 300.

Flop: 8h 4h 3s. I hold Kc-3h — bottom pair (pair of 3s) with a K kicker. Pot is 600 and I’m out of position versus the SB opener who will act after me. This is a marginal but decent heads‑up holding: ahead of many unpaired overcards in his wide opening range but behind most made pairs/sets. The board is fairly dry (two hearts but only a backdoor flush possibility for me), so there’s no strong draw to worry about. Given I’m OOP with a vulnerable pair I want pot control and to let him bluff; leading out builds a pot I don’t want and can get me raised off my hand. Plan: check and respond — call a standard small c‑bet to realize equity, fold to a large raise, and re‑evaluate on the turn if I improve or face further aggression.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop of 8h-4h-3s is relatively disconnected, though it does have a heart draw. With Queen-high, I have two overcards to the board. Since Minni checked, I will lead out with a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to exert pressure and attempt to take the pot down immediately, representing a hand that connected with the board.

I’m OOP with Kc3h on 8h-4h-3s — bottom pair. Pot is 900 and it costs me 300 to call, so I’m getting ~25% pot odds. Heads‑up Flash opened and is c‑betting a wide range, so my pair is ahead of many missed overcards and some bluffs but behind any made 8s/4s/pairs or sets. Raising OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and commit me to difficult decisions; folding is too tight given the pot odds and heads‑up dynamics. I’ll call to control the pot, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve (trip 3s or better) or get a clear read/value.

I have bottom pair (3s) with a decent K kicker on 8h–4h–3s–Qc. The Q is a scary overcard that hits a large portion of Flash’s continuing range after a preflop open and flop c‑bet. I’m out of position and there’s no bet to me on the turn, so betting would bloat the pot and commit with a marginal hand that’s often behind Qx or better. Check to control the pot and induce bluffs or get showdown cheaply. If Flash barrels a small‑to‑medium bet I’ll call to realize equity against a wide range; fold to heavy aggression or a large raise. If checked through, only bet the river for value if I improve (trips/two pair) or consider a targeted bluff if the river is a safe card and villain shows weakness.

On the turn, the Queen of Clubs gives me top pair. Minni has checked twice now (flop and turn). My flop continuation bet was called, suggesting Minni has some piece of the board (like an 8 or a small pair) or a draw (like hearts). Since I now have the lead with top pair, I should bet for value to extract chips from those weaker hands and protect against draws. A bet of 600, which is half the pot, is a standard value bet in this situation.

I have Kc-3h — bottom pair (pair of 3s) with a decent K kicker. Flash’s line (preflop raise, flop c‑bet, turn barrel) is wide heads‑up but the turn bet is only 600 into a 1200 pot (now 1800), so calling 600 to win 1800 gives me ~25% pot‑odds. That’s enough against a wide continuing range that includes many bluffs/overcards. Raising OOP with a marginal pair would bloat the pot and commit me to tough decisions; folding is too tight given the odds and heads‑up dynamics. I’ll call to see the river and re‑evaluate: fold to a large, polarizing river shove or overbet, call small/medium river bets, and only get aggressive if I improve to trips/two‑pair or find a clear read to bluff.

Board: 8h 4h 3s Qc 7c. I hold Kc3h — bottom pair with a decent kicker. Villain opened preflop, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn, a line that contains both value (Qx, 8x, 7x, sets) and a fair number of bluffs in a heads‑up spot. I’m out of position with limited fold equity and little likelihood of getting called by worse on a river bet large enough to be profitable; conversely, many hands that beat me will continue. The river 7c doesn’t complete obvious backdoor draws that were live earlier, so my pair retains real showdown value vs missed overcards. Pot control is the priority: check and force the opponent to show intent. If Flash bets a small/medium amount I’ll call for pot odds and because my hand beats many of his bluffs/missed overcards; I’ll fold to a large polarizing shove. Action: check to get to showdown cheaply and avoid bloating the pot OOP.

I have top pair with a Queen, but my kicker (6) is very weak. I've been the aggressor throughout the hand, and Minni has check-called on every street. While I likely have the best hand, if I bet and get called, I'm often up against a better Queen (like Q9, QT, or QJ) or a slow-played monster. If I check, I can win the pot at showdown against missed draws or smaller pairs like an 8. Given the kicker and the fact that Minni has been passive, checking behind is the safest way to realize my equity and take the pot.

Heads-up on the button with K♠8♥ — a playable, top-card hand that performs well in position. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, but I’d prefer to take the initiative and apply pressure to the shorter-stacked BB (Flash, 8,400). A min-raise to 200 (total) is the right size: it preserves fold equity, keeps my range wide, and buys me position postflop. If Flash 3-bets large or shoves, I’ll re-evaluate (I can fold to extreme aggression with no strong draw/hand), but if I get called I plan to continuation-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or fold when pot control is necessary. Overall: open-raise to seize initiative and exploit heads-up dynamics.

I am in the big blind holding Q3 offsuit. Minni has raised from the button/small blind to 200 total (meaning it's 100 more for me to call). While Q3 is a weak hand, the pot odds are 2:1 and we are playing heads-up. However, out of position against a player with a significant chip lead, I should play tighter. In previous hands, Minni has been quite active and aggressive. Q3o is near the bottom of a defending range, so I will fold this and wait for a better spot to contest a pot.

In a heads-up situation as the small blind, T7o is a marginal but playable hand. I am against Minni, who has a significantly larger stack. Given the pot odds and the need to maintain some level of aggression or at least see flops with mediocre hands to avoid being exploited by folds, I will complete the bet to see a flop.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♥5♣. Pot is 200 and opponent (Flash, SB) has ~8,300 behind. A5o is a strong heads-up holding (ace-blocker, playable postflop), and I don't want to play a marginal ace out of position passively. I raise to the minimum (200 total) to take the initiative, gain fold equity against the SB's limp/call range, and keep the pot controllable given the effective stack sizes. If called I'll continuation-bet on many dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/face-card heavy boards; if I face a large reraise I'll fold and preserve my deep stack. Raise to 200 total.

I am in the Small Blind with T7o. Minni has raised to 200. I have already contributed 100 as the small blind. The pot is currently 300, and it costs me 100 more to call. Getting 3-to-1 pot odds, T7o is a reasonable hand to defend against a raise from the Big Blind in a heads-up scenario, especially as I am already partially committed. I will call to see the flop.

I raised preflop to take the initiative, but the flop (Jh 6c 5d) contains a face-card over my pair and is reasonably favorable to Flash’s calling range (Jx, pocket pairs, some 6x/5x, broadways). I have second pair (5s) with the A kicker, out of position, so this is a marginal holding to build a big pot with. Checking controls the pot size OOP, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me call a small c-bet to realize equity while folding to a large raise. I’ll check and re-evaluate based on Flash’s action (call modest bets, fold to big raises).

The flop of J65 doesn't hit my hand (T7o) at all. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. While a bet might occasionally take the pot down, my hand has zero showdown value and very little equity if called. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card and potentially improve to a pair or pick up a straight draw. Given Minni's large stack and my marginal holding, I prefer to keep the pot small and realize whatever equity I have for free.

The turn (As) pairs my Ah, giving me two pair — Aces and Fives. Heads-up this is a very strong holding but still vulnerable to sets (JJ, 66, 55) that could have slow-played the flop. Flash called my preflop raise and both of us checked the flop, so his range contains Jx, mid pairs, 5x/6x and broadways. I want to extract value and charge any backdoor/rare draws while keeping worse A’s/Jx and paired 6/5 hands in the pot, so I’ll bet for value and protection. A bet of 300 (≈3/4 pot) is large enough to deny clean odds to many draws and to get value from worse hands, but small enough to avoid over-committing if I face a big reraise. If he raises moderately I’ll strongly consider calling; if he jams an enormous overbet I’ll re-evaluate given the possibility of sets.

On the turn, an Ace of Spades (As) has appeared. Minni, who checked the flop after being the pre-flop aggressor, has now led out for 300 into a pot of 400. My hand (Tc7s) has absolutely no equity and does not even have a backdoor draw. The Ace is a very good card for the pre-flop raiser's range, and Minni's lead suggests they either hit the Ace or are representing it successfully. There is no reason to float or bluff here given the board texture and my lack of outs.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with pocket nines (9♣9♦). This is a clear open-raise spot: I want initiative and value against a wide BB calling range while protecting my pair from overcards. The big blind has 8,100 (≈81bb) and I have a deep stack, so I don’t need to overcommit preflop. I’ll make a standard 3x-ish raise to 300 total — big enough to extract value and deny correct odds to many speculative hands, but not so large that I bloat the pot unnecessarily against a potentially aggressive opponent. Plan: continue with a c-bet on favorable flops, re-evaluate vs sizable 3-bets or all-ins.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has raised to 300 from the small blind/button. Given the strength of my hand, I should 3-bet to build the pot and potentially take the lead. A raise to 1000 is a standard sizing here to put pressure on the opponent and extract value.

Heads-up on the button with pocket nines. I opened to 300 and Flash 3-bet to 1000, making the pot ~1300 and leaving me a call of 700 (1000 total contributed). With 9♣9♦ vs a likely wide-ish 3-bet range heads-up, the lowest-variance, highest-EV line is to call and play the flop — I realize my equity, keep worse hands and bluffs in his range, and maintain pot control. A small 4-bet (min to 1700) won’t fold enough of his range to be worth the extra risk and a large 4-bet or shove creates unnecessary variance against a shorter-stacked opponent. Folding is too weak here. I’ll call to 1000 total (700 more) and plan to evaluation the flop: extract value on safe low-card/dry boards, control the pot or fold to heavy action on overcard-heavy boards, and re-evaluate if he jams.

I have Ace-King offsuit with the Ace of hearts on a flop of 5h Td 7h, giving me the nut flush draw and two overcards. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage and should continue with a bet here to maintain aggression and realize my equity. A bet of roughly 40% of the pot (800 chips) is a standard sizing to apply pressure on the opponent's range while keeping the pot manageable if I miss on the turn.

I have a medium pair (9♣9♦) on a fairly dry flop (5♥ T♦ 7♥) after calling Flash’s 3-bet preflop. Flash’s flop stab is small (800 into a ~2800 pot) — a sizing consistent with a wide, probe-heavy or turn-barrel range rather than an ice-cold monster. Calling the 800 (bringing my total committed to 1,800) is the lowest-variance, highest-EV play: I keep worse pairs and bluffs in, preserve pot control against overpairs/top pairs, and realize equity without bloating the pot against a likely stronger segment of his 3-bet range. I’ll reassess on the turn — proceed cautiously if hearts or Tens pair the board, and fold to heavy turn/river overbets; consider extracting more value if he checks or continues small.

The board has become extremely coordinated with the arrival of the 8d, following a 5-7-10 flop. The turn card connects many of the hands Minni would reasonably call with (6-7, 7-8, 8-9, 9-10, or even 6-9). While I still hold the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and King), my equity against a made hand or a two-pair/straight is around 25-30%. Since I am out of position and the board has become significantly scarier for my Ace-high range, checking is the most prudent move. This allows me to see how Minni reacts and potentially realize my equity by seeing a free river or calling a reasonable bet, rather than bloating the pot with a bet that is unlikely to force better hands to fold.

Heads-up in a 3-bet pot with 9♣9♦ on 5♥ T♦ 7♥ 8♦. Flash 3-bet preflop and made a small-ish flop stab (800 into ~2k); his check on the turn looks like pot-control or weakness rather than a monster. I hold the only pair of 9s (both 9s are in my hand), so most straight combos that include a 9 are impossible — the only realistic straights are very narrow (e.g. 4+6). There are still hands that beat me (TT, sets, or a turned 8/5 trips), but many of Flash’s 3-bet/c-bet hands are worse (smaller pairs, broadway bluffs, heart draws) and will fold to or call a value/protection bet. I want to both extract thin value and charge draws while keeping fold equity, so I’ll bet about half the pot. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river; if he check-raises big (polarizing to monsters), I will respect that and fold.

I currently hold Ace-King offsuit with the Ace of hearts, giving me the nut flush draw on a 5h Td 7h 8d board. Minni has bet 1800 into a pot of 3600. The pot odds are exactly 3:1, meaning I need 25% equity to call. With 9 outs to the nut flush, I have approximately an 18% chance of hitting on the river. When including my two overcards (Ace and King) as potential outs, my equity increases significantly, though they may not be clean if Minni has two pair or a straight. However, the implied odds are very high; if I hit my flush on the river, I am likely to get paid off by Minni's range which likely includes many straights, sets, or two-pair hands given the board texture. Calling is the mathematically sound choice here.

On the river, the board is 5h-Td-7h-8d-9s. I have Ace-King high (Ah-Kd), having missed both my nut flush draw and my overcards. The board is now extremely coordinated, with a four-card straight (7-8-9-10) visible. Any Jack or Six makes a straight. Given that Minni called my 3-bet pre-flop, called my flop bet, and bet the turn when I checked, she likely has a made hand. My Ace-high has virtually no showdown value, but leading out with a bluff on this board is highly risky given how well it connects with a standard calling range. My best course of action is to check and prepare to fold to a bet, as I cannot profitably represent the straight and Minni is unlikely to fold a pair or better in this spot.

I rivered trips (9♣9♦ on 5♥ T♦ 7♥ 8♦ 9♠). Very strong hand heads-up, but vulnerable to straights (any 6 in Flash’s hand makes 6-7-8-9-10) and pocket tens (TT makes a set that beats my trips). Flash’s line (3-bet preflop, small flop stab, check-call my turn bet) is consistent with a mix of medium pairs, turn draws, and floaty broadway hands. He checked the river, so many worse hands will now check back — checking behind surrenders thin value. I want to extract value from worse pairs/sets (7s, 8s, 5s, Tx pocket pairs that aren’t tens, Kx/Qx floaters) while not bloating the pot against the relatively small subset of hands that beat me. A small-to-medium value bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by many worse hands and keeps fold equity vs bluffs, but doesn’t overcommit me if Flash check-raises strongly (which would typically represent a hand that actually beats me). Betting 2400 into 7200 is the right balance. If he folds, we scoop; if he calls we’re often good; if he check-raises large (near all-in) I’ll give heavy respect and likely fold because that line is usually straights or TT.

The board has a four-card straight (7-8-9-10) and I have completely missed my draws (flush and overcards). My Ace-King high has no showdown value against any pair, and Minni's consistent aggression (calling a 3-bet, calling a flop bet, betting the turn and river) suggests a strong made hand, likely a straight or at least two pair. Even at 4:1 pot odds, a call here is not profitable given my hand's lack of strength.

I am holding Pocket Kings, which is a premium hand. My goal is to extract maximum value from Minni, especially given her large stack and willingness to play many hands. I will put in a standard open raise from the button to 300 to build the pot early.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB in a heads-up spot. I’m in the BB with Tc6d — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (total 300) which gives ~2:1 pot odds to see a flop. Against a wide button opening range this hand has workable equity and decent playability to hit a ten or a straight/paired board. 3-betting here as a bluff is unnecessary and OOP postflop would make it harder to realize equity; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed cautiously OOP — fold to large aggression if I miss, and value-bet/raise if I hit a strong hand or a clear draw.

I called preflop with Tc6d and missed the flop entirely (K♠ 4♥ J♣). Villain opened from the SB, so their range is wide and they’ll c-bet a lot, but I have no pair, no real draw (only a very remote backdoor Broadway possibility) and I’m out of position. With a 600-chip pot and huge effective SPR for me but a shallowish effective stack for him, I don’t want to build a big pot OOP with air. Optimal, low-variance line is to check and control the pot: allow bluffs to show themselves, keep weaker hands in, and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan is to fold to significant aggression unless I pick up equity or get offered good pot odds to continue; call small probes selectively if the price and board texture justify it.

I have flopped top set of Kings (Kh-Kc on Ks-4h-Jc). This is an incredibly strong hand. Minni has checked to me. While I want to build the pot, this board is relatively dry, and I don't want to force a fold too early. However, since the pot is already 600, I will place a continuation bet to get value from Jacks, straight draws (like QT), or any King she might be holding. A half-pot bet of 300 should keep her interested while building value.

Board K♠-4♥-J♣, pot 900, villain bets 300. I’m OOP with Tc6d — no pair, no meaningful draw. Calling 300 to win 1200 requires ~25% equity. I have 6 clean immediate outs (3 tens + 3 sixes) → P(hit by river ≈ 24.1%), plus a very small runner-runner straight chance (~1–1.5%) which only barely reaches the break-even threshold in raw terms. Crucially, a large portion of the villain’s c-bet range will be Kx/Jx/overcards where my future pair (especially a 6) will still be behind, so my realized equity is meaningfully lower than the raw number. Raising as a bluff OOP with no fold equity and a weak hand is too risky. Given the poor realized equity and the cheap size of the bet offering only borderline pot odds, folding and waiting for a better spot is the lowest-variance, +EV line here.

Heads-up on the button with K♣7♠ — in heads-up play this is a hand worth playing aggressively. I want initiative and position postflop, and Flash has a relatively small stack compared to mine so I can apply pressure. A standard 2BB open (raise to 200 total) builds the pot, picks up fold equity against his wide defending range, and keeps the sizing reasonable if he 3-bets. If he shows heavy resistance (big 3-bet or shove) I can fold and preserve my deep stack; if he calls I’ll play position and c-bet favorable flops. Calling is too passive and lets him see a cheap flop with a wide range, so I raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh Js. Minni, who is the chip leader and quite aggressive, has raised to 200 from the Button. QJ-offsuit is a strong hand in a heads-up or short-handed context and is a standard defending hand against a 2x raise. Given my stack size of 48 big blinds, I want to see a flop and realize my equity without overcommitting pre-flop to a potentially larger 3-bet pot against a much larger stack.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker on a dry board (J-8-3 rainbow). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot against the Button raiser. Standard play from the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. If Minni bets, I will comfortably call; if she checks back, I can look to lead for value on the turn.

I opened pre-flop and have position; Flash checked the flop. My Kc7s is just a single overcard to 8♠ J♦ 3♥ and I don’t have a real draw (only one spade in hand so a 5-card flush is impossible by the river). Against Flash’s wide BB calling range this is a textbook spot to continuation-bet to take the pot down and capitalize on fold equity. I’ll use a sensible half-pot size to pressure weak hands (Qx, Tx, small pairs, missed connectors) while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate; if he check-raises large or jams, K-high is usually a fold. Betting 200 more (total commit = 400) balances fold equity and pot control.

I have top pair with a strong Queen kicker on a dry rainbow board. Minni's pot-sized bet of 400 into 400 is aggressive, but folding is not an option given the strength of my hand in a heads-up pot. Calling allows me to keep her bluffs in her range and control the size of the pot, whereas raising might only get action from hands that beat me (like AJ, KJ, or sets).

On the turn, I have improved to top two pair (Queens and Jacks). The board is relatively safe, although a spade flush draw has appeared. Since I am out of position against a very aggressive opponent who previously bet the pot on the flop, checking is the most effective play to encourage her to continue bluffing or to value bet with a wider range of hands like AJ or KJ. I intend to extract value on this street if she bets.

Heads‑up on the button with K♣7♠ vs a caller from the BB. Flop c-bet was called, and the turn Q♠ significantly helps Flash’s continuing range (Qx, Jx, 8x, pocket pairs). I only have K‑high with no realistic improvement — a river flush is impossible from my single spade and the two spades on board, and I have no meaningful straight outs. Flash checked the turn, which can be pot‑control or a trap; a turn bet here will get called by the hands that beat me and can put me in a tough spot vs a check‑raise or shove. With position and a small pot (1200) relative to stacks, the prudent line is to control the pot and realize equity: check and see the river for free. On the river I’ll re‑evaluate: if Flash bets small I can call with K‑high sometimes, but I’ll fold to large bets or shove‑sized aggression since I’m almost always behind.

On the river, the board has paired twice, and I have successfully made a full house (Jacks full of Queens). Considering the action, the only hand that realistically beats me is pocket Queens (making Queens full of Jacks), which is highly improbable given that Minni checked the turn. I have the effective nuts in this situation. I am leading with a value bet of 900 into the 1200 pot, aiming to get called by any Jack, any Queen, or a lower full house like 8s full or 3s full that Minni might be holding.

Pot was 1,200 before the river. Flash bets 900 into that pot — I must call 900 to win 2,100 total, so I need ~42.9% equity to break even. My hand Kc7s has no pair and essentially zero showdown equity against a reasonable value-heavy river betting range. Flash called my preflop raise and my flop c-bet, then checked the turn — that line is consistent with hands like Jx, 8x, or medium pairs that called down; the river Jack now turns any Jx into trips (huge value), and Jx is a very plausible and common holding here. While some bluffs (missed Q/8 draws, random air) exist, the frequency is unlikely to be high enough to profitably call 900. Raising as a bluff is unattractive — it risks much more against a short-stacked opponent who can shove and is likely to have enough calling/shoving combos with Jx or better. I preserve a huge stack and fold this marginal K‑high versus a strong-looking line and sizing.

I am on the Button in a heads-up situation with 85o. While 85o is a playable hand in some heads-up strategies, Minni has a significant stack advantage and has been playing quite aggressively. With a stack of approximately 54 big blinds, I prefer to fold my weakest hands and wait for more favorable spots to engage, rather than building a pot out of position with a marginal holding.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. T♦2♣ is weak but acceptable in position — it has some showdown value and can realise equity in many spots. Flash is the big blind with a much smaller effective stack (~53bb), so a small standard open raises fold equity and gives me initiative. I’ll raise to the min-open (200 total) to keep my range wide and the pot controllable. Plan: if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet on favourable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated textures; if he 3-bets large or shoves I’ll fold this holding most of the time since it doesn’t fare well vs heavy preflop aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. Minni, on the Button, has made a small raise to 200 (a min-raise). While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, Jack-Four offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Given Minni's significant stack advantage and aggressive playstyle, I choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity to defend my blind.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium starting hand in this format. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take advantage of my position and hand strength.

I'm in the big blind with T♥3♦ facing a button open to 300. Heads-up my defending range should be wide and this hand, while marginal, has enough playability to see a flop given the pot odds: I'm being asked to call 200 into a 400 pot (I need roughly ~33% equity). Flash has been opening frequently, so calling keeps his bluffs in and lets me realize equity without bloating the pot out of position by 3-betting with a weak hand. Plan: call to see the flop (300 total committed); proceed cautiously postflop — fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, and continue only with top pair, strong draws or clear equity improvements.

Flop is Qs Qh 3h; my Th3d gives me two pair (Queens and Threes). Heads-up against a frequent button opener this is a very strong hand vs a wide opening range that contains a lot of non‑Queen hands and many worse two‑pair/one‑pair holdings. Pot is 600 — I want value and to deny free runouts, so I’ll bet for value rather than check and allow him to freely realize equity or bluff‑catch on his terms. I’ll bet ~half the pot (300) to get calls from worse pairs, weaker two‑pairs/kickers and some bluffers, while keeping the pot manageable if he shoves. If he jams/raises big I’ll be willing to call given his short-ish stack and my hand’s equity; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and extract more when appropriate.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit on a paired board of Queen-Queen-Trey. Minni has led out with a 300-chip bet into a 600-chip pot. While a 'donk bet' can sometimes indicate a Queen, it is also a common move with mid-pairs, draws, or even total air to see where they stand. With Ace-high and a strong kicker, I have enough equity to call one street and see what happens on the turn, especially since I have position for the rest of the hand.

I have two pair (Queens and Threes) on Qs 3h Qh 4s with Th3d. Flop I bet 300 and was called; Flash then checks the turn. That line is ambiguous — it can be pot‑control with a medium hand, a missed draw, or a trap with trips (any Queen or a 3). Despite the vulnerability to a Queen/3, my hand is still strong vs most of his calling range (non‑Q/3 holdings, single pairs, broadways, some draws). I want value and to deny a free river, but I don’t want to bloat the pot and get shipped on by a polarized check‑raise. A small‑to‑medium value bet is best: bet ~half the pot to get called by worse hands and keep the pot manageable against big resistance. I’ll bet 600 into the 1200 pot. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and extract more when appropriate; if he check‑raises a small amount I’ll call and re‑assess; if he jams a very large shove I’ll be cautious and fold to a highly polarized shove (likely trips) rather than auto‑calling without a read.

Flash is currently holding Ace-high (Ac Js) on a board of Qs 3h Qh 4s. Minni has led with a 'donk bet' on the flop and followed up with a half-pot bet of 600 on the turn. While the double Queen on the board makes it possible Minni has trips, this betting pattern can also represent a marginal hand (like a small pocket pair or a 3) or a pure bluff. Given Flash's stack size and the strength of Ace-Jack in a heads-up match, calling to see the river is a reasonable decision. Flash has position and some showdown value, and may improve to the best hand if an Ace or Jack appears on the river. If Minni checks the river, Flash may be able to win at showdown or potentially take the pot with a bet if the board remains favorable.

I have two pair (Queens and Threes) on Qs 3h Qh 4s 2s. Heads‑up against a frequent opener who called both my flop and turn bets, his continuing range contains a lot of worse hands (paired 3s, small/mid pocket pairs, some Kx/Ax floats) that will pay off a value bet. The river 2s does complete some backdoor spade draws (so there are combos that now beat me) and there are Qx combos that also dominate, but overall I expect more callers than bluffs. I want thin value while keeping the pot manageable in case of a raise, so I’ll bet about half the pot to get called by worse while shrinking the number of very strong hands that can profitably raise. If he makes a small raise I’ll call and re‑evaluate; a large polarized shove would look like trips/flush and I’d fold to that.

I am holding Ace-Jack high on a board of Qs-3h-Qh-4s-2s. The board is paired (Queens) and hasn't changed much since the flop. Minni has led out on every single street (donk betting), which is a line that typically represents either a Queen, a pocket pair that has become a bluff-catcher, or a very persistent bluff. While Ace-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, calling three consecutive bets with it—especially when the river didn't improve my hand—is high-risk. Minni's consistent 50% pot-sized betting suggests strength or a high-frequency bluffing strategy. However, without a specific read that Minni is over-bluffing this exact line, Ace-high is rarely good enough to call down a triple barrel on a paired board. I will preserve my remaining stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I have position after the flop, which is a big advantage. K♣2♠ is a marginal hand, but in heads-up play you open very wide. Calling 50 lets the big blind see a cheap flop and keeps them in with a wide range; a min-raise to 200 takes the initiative, applies pressure, and keeps our investment small if we face a 3-bet. Opponent has ~40BB effective (4050), so I don't want to bloop too many chips with K2o, but I do want to seize the initiative and use position postflop to c-bet or control the pot. Plan: raise to 200; fold to a large 3-bet, but if called use positional advantage to steer the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 6h. Minni, who has a massive chip lead and has been very active, raised to 200. This is a 2x raise. I need to call 100 more to see the flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While 96o is a weak hand, the price is very cheap to defend my blind and see if I can hit a favorable flop against an opponent who opens frequently.

I have middle pair (nines) on a somewhat connected board (9-7-10). I also have a gutshot straight draw to an eight (6-7-8-9-10). Since I am first to act on the flop, checking is the standard play to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) responds. This allows me to keep the pot small with a hand that has some showdown value and draw potential but is vulnerable to overcards or bigger pairs.

I was the preflop aggressor and I have position with K♥2♠ — one strong overcard to the Ten on the flop. The board 9♠-7♦-T♥ is relatively dry with no real flush possibilities and few strong two-street draws, so a continuation bet will pick up lots of unpaired hands and get value from worse kings/overcards and some pocket pairs. I’ll c-bet a constructive, half-pot size (200 into 400) to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable vs. this ~39BB effective opponent. If called I’ll re-evaluate turn texture; if I face a large raise I’ll give up unless I improve.

I have middle pair (nines) and a gutshot straight draw (needing an eight) on a board of 10-9-7. Minni, who is highly aggressive and has a significant chip lead, has placed a standard continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 400. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that I have both showdown value and a draw to a straight, this is a clear call. Raising here would likely be overplaying my hand against a chip leader who isn't afraid to push back, so calling to see a turn card is the most prudent strategy.

The Turn card (Jack of Hearts) is quite coordinated and potentially dangerous. The board is now 9-7-10-J, meaning any 8 makes a straight. My pair of nines is now third pair on the board, and while I have a gutshot to an 8, it would only give me the low end of a straight (6-7-8-9-10), which is easily beaten by anyone holding a higher straight. Minni is a very aggressive chip leader. I will check to see how she reacts to this card, likely looking to fold to any significant pressure given the vulnerability of my hand.

I’m on the button with K♥2♠ on 9♠-7♦-T♥-J♥. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop (was called), so I still have initiative. Flash checked the turn, which shows weakness or pot control (float/marginal pair/overcards) more often than a strong made hand. I have no pair, but K-high is a reasonable blocker and a decent hand vs a wide calling range. A half-pot turn barrel pressures floats and weaker pairs while keeping the pot manageable against stronger holdings. I’ll make a constructive bluff that folds out many unpaired hands and some medium pairs; if I get raised I’ll release since I have almost no showdown value. Bet half-pot (400 into 800). If called I’ll re-evaluate the river; if he raises substantially I will fold.

The turn card (Jack of Hearts) is very unfavorable for my hand. My middle pair of nines is now third pair on an extremely coordinated board (7-9-10-J). Any 8 now completes a straight, and since Minni is a very active player, her range contains many 8s, 10s, and Jacks. While I have a gutshot draw to a six-high straight (6-7-8-9-10), this would be the low end of the straight and easily beaten by anyone holding a Queen or even a higher straight using the 7-8-9-10-J structure. Given Minni's aggression and my weak relative hand strength on this specific board texture, I should fold to her 400 bet and preserve my remaining stack.

I am on the button with 5s 7c, which is a very weak starting hand. While a wide range can be played in heads-up, my stack is relatively short (37 BB) compared to Minni's. Given Minni's aggressive play in previous hands, I will choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

Heads-up on the button with A♥6♠. In this spot A6s is a strong heads-up hand (ace blocker + suited), and I want to seize initiative and use my position postflop. Pot is small (150) and the min-raise is 200, so a standard 3x BB open to 300 applies pressure, isolates the BB and gains fold equity vs weaker holdings while keeping medium-strength hands and worse aces in to extract value. Flash has a 3.6k stack (≈36 BB) so this sizing keeps postflop playability and avoids bloating the pot unnecessarily. Plan: open to 300; if called, play postflop for value and leverage position; if met with a large 3-bet I will re-evaluate based on sizing and range.

In a heads-up situation, 6c4c is a hand with decent playability and equity against a wide button-opening range. I am getting 2-to-1 odds to see a flop, and with a stack of 3,600 (36 big blinds), I can afford to defend and look for a favorable flop without committing too much of my stack pre-flop.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) on a highly coordinated board (Td-9h-7d). Given I am out of position and the board heavily favors a standard button opening range, I will check to the aggressor to see how they proceed. My hand has some potential but is currently weak.

Heads-up on T♦ 9♥ 7♦ I have A♥6♠ — ace-high with a blocker to a lot of Ax combos and a one-card (8) straight possibility using my 6. Flash checked to me after calling my 3x open, so his range is wide and contains many missed hands, small pairs and draws. A standard continuation bet here extracts value from worse hands, folds out lots of pocket pairs and weaker broadways, and keeps the initiative with position. I choose a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600): large enough to have meaningful fold equity but small enough to control the pot against draws and to keep hands I dominate from folding. Plan: if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and use position to barrel selectively; if he raises large I’ll fold my marginal ace-high.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) on a very coordinated and wet board (Td-9h-7d). The opponent's bet of 300 into a 600 pot (offering 3:1 pot odds) is a standard continuation bet. However, my hand is extremely weak; even if I hit an 8 on the turn, it creates a board where many higher straights are possible (any Jack or Queen-Jack), and the presence of two diamonds makes my non-diamond gutshot even less appealing. Given the stack sizes and the likelihood that this board connects well with a button opening range, I do not have sufficient equity or implied odds to justify a call.

I have KTs on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand and standard practice is to raise to gain initiative and potentially take down the blinds. Given the stack sizes and previous hand history, a standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a 3bb open from the button (Flash). I hold Q♥7♥ — a playable, suited queen with backdoor flush/straight potential. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more; that gives me ~3:1 (25%) pot odds, so I only need ~25% equity to make the call profitable against a wide heads-up opening range. 3-betting is an option to apply pressure, but this hand is marginal for a light 3-bet OOP and Flash is shallow (3,100) so a 3-bet could induce a shove or a call where I’d be uncomfortable committing with Q7s. I’ll defend and see a flop — call the 200 (300 total) and play the flop based on texture and opponent action.

Flop: 8♦ 9♣ 7♦ — I hold Q♥7♥: bottom pair with a decent kicker in a heads-up spot. Flash was the preflop raiser but checked the flop, which often indicates weakness or pot control with a wide raising range. The board is connected and has some straight/diamond draw possibilities (JT and 10-6 type straights, and two diamonds), so I want to charge draws and get value from worse pairs and floating hands. With Flash relatively short-stacked (~31bb) and wide preflop, a modest bet should fold a lot of overcards and free cards while still getting called by worse. I’ll make a size that protects my pair but doesn’t overcommit if I face a raise — bet ~1/2 pot. If I get a big raise/shove I’ll assume a very strong hand and re-evaluate cautiously; if called I can continue based on the turn.

On a flop of 8d-9c-7d, I have an open-ended straight draw with my Ten of Diamonds (needing a 6 or a Jack) and one overcard (King). Minni has led out for 300 into a 600 pot. With my stack size and the pot odds being 4:1, calling is a mathematically sound decision to try and realize my equity. Raising is risky on such a connected board where the opponent is showing strength, and folding is too tight given the straight draw.

I hold Q♥7♥ on 8♦-9♣-7♦-3♣. I led the flop (300) and got called; pot is 1,200 and Flash (short ~2,800) checked the turn. This is a marginal bottom-pair hand: it’s ahead of floats/overcards but behind any 9x/8x/7x combos, sets, and many river-completing draws (6/10 straights, diamond runner). Betting the turn (even a half-pot ~600) risks inducing a shove from a short-stacked opponent or committing me to a big pot where I’m often behind. With my very deep effective stack advantage and a check from the preflop raiser, pot-control is optimal — realize equity cheaply, allow bluffs to be introduced, and avoid difficult all-in decisions. I’ll check and re-evaluate on the river: if I improve to two pair/trips or get a safe river, I’ll bet for value; if I face a large river bet with no improvement, I’ll fold.

I have an open-ended straight draw (J or 6) and one overcard. Minni led the flop but has now checked the turn. This check often indicates weakness or a desire to reach showdown cheaply with a marginal hand. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking back allows me to take a free card and realize my equity. If I hit my straight on the river, I can then value bet. If I miss, I haven't lost any additional chips. Given my stack size relative to the pot, taking the free card is the most prudent strategy here.

I checked the turn to control the pot and didn’t improve on the river — I still have only bottom pair (7s) with a Q kicker. Villain called my flop bet but then checked turn, which represents a wide, relatively passive range that includes hands that beat me (9x/8x, straights from 5-6, sets) as well as worse pairs/missed draws (3x/4x, some floaters). Pot is 1,200 and Villain is short (~2,800). A thin value bet here would risk inducing a shove or folding out worse hands for little value. Pot-control is optimal: I’ll check and take the showdown rather than create a marginal river spot where I could be forced off by aggression.

On the river, the board is 8d-9c-7d-3c-4h. I have King-high (Ks Td), having missed my open-ended straight draw from the flop. Minni led out on the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically indicates a marginal hand (like a weak pair) or a missed draw. While a bluff might occasionally force a fold from a weak pair, Minni's large stack and the board texture (which connects well with a Big Blind's defending range) make a bluff risky. Since I have some very marginal showdown value against other missed draws (like QJ or JT), and I want to preserve my remaining stack of 2500, I will check behind and concede the pot if beaten by a pair.

Open a wide heads-up range from the button / SB. 3♣6♣ is playable heads-up and has postflop playability; raising takes the initiative and often wins the 150 pot outright. A minimum raise to 200 (total) is size-efficient: it applies pressure to Flash’s 27bb stack while keeping the pot manageable if called. If Flash folds we collect the pot; if he calls we keep position and can continuation-bet favorable flops or fold to strong 3-bets/shoves. Avoid risking a large portion of my deep stack with a marginal hand preflop.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Minni has made a minimum raise to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 odds. While T7o is a mediocre hand, it is sufficient to defend the Big Blind against a min-raise in a heads-up situation. I will call and see a flop.

I have flopped top pair with a Ten. While my kicker is weak, this is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. I will start by checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they continue betting, allowing me to control the pot size or potentially check-call to protect my hand against bluffs and draws.

I opened preflop and have position. Flop 8♠ T♣ 4♦ is dry and misses a large portion of a BB calling range. I hold 3♣6♣ — no pair and only weak backdoor equity — so I don't want to inflate the pot but I do want to seize the initiative and fold out unmade hands. A half‑pot continuation bet (200 into 400) applies pressure, preserves stack depth against Flash's ~26bb, and keeps lines simple: win the pot now or see a cheap turn while folding to strong check‑raises. If called I'll proceed cautiously; if check‑raised or jammed I can fold given my very weak equity.

I have flopped top pair with Ten-Seven on a board of 8-T-4. This is a strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni's 1/3 pot continuation bet is standard. I will call to see the turn, keeping the pot manageable given my weak kicker and staying in to catch potential bluffs or lower pairs.

I have top pair with a Ten. The turn card (3d) is a blank that doesn't significantly alter the board texture, although it does introduce a backdoor diamond draw. I will continue with my strategy of checking to the aggressor to control the pot size and act as a bluff catcher, as my kicker (7) is relatively weak. Checking here allows Minni to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands.

I have a very weak made hand on the turn — pair of 3s (3♣) on 8♠ T♣ 4♦ 3♦. Pot is 800 and Flash is short (≈24bb, 2400). He called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn, which usually indicates either a medium/strong made hand (T‑x, 8‑x, sets) or a flop float that missed the turn. I have almost no fold equity versus his calling range, and a turn bet will either get called by better hands or induce a shove that I should fold to with such a small pair. Pot‑control is the best line: check and realize equity for free. If Flash bets a small amount I’ll consider a call depending on sizing (fold to a large shove); if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river for a thin value bet or check to showdown.

I have a Ten for second pair on the river after the Jack appeared. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a powerhouse, possibly a weaker Ten, a middle pair, or a missed draw. Betting now is unlikely to get called by worse hands, so I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from a missed draw. If Minni bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot.

Check. I have bottom pair (3♣) with a weak 6 kicker on 8♠ T♣ 4♦ 3♦ J♥. Pot is 800 and Flash is short (~2400). He called my flop c‑bet then checked the turn and checked back the river — a passive line that often contains T‑x, 8‑x, 4‑x, missed floats, or medium pairs that beat me. I have almost no fold equity and little value to extract; a river bet will either get called by better or induce a shove I should fold to. Best line is to preserve stack, take showdown for my marginal hand, and only call a small bet if he leads out. Checking minimizes risk and is the highest EV line here.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s4d, which is a weak hand. We are heads-up against Minni, who has a massive stack and has been playing aggressively. Given my relatively short stack (31.5 big blinds) and the low equity of this hand, there is no reason to put more money into the pot or attempt a wide steal from the button here. Folding is the standard and safest play.

Heads-up on the button with 9♣8♦ offsuit — a playable, positionally advantaged hand. Calling 50 is too passive and gives the BB good odds to see a flop; I want to seize initiative, build the pot with a wide range, and gain fold equity. Flash is only ~30bb, so a standard 3xBB open is appropriate: big enough to apply pressure but small relative to my deep stack. I’ll raise to take control of the hand and leverage postflop position.

Holding Td 8h in the Big Blind, I am facing a 3x open raise from Minni (SB). T8o is a marginal hand but generally within the defending range against a Button/SB open in heads-up or short-handed play, especially given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400). I have a shorter stack compared to Minni's massive lead, but I have enough to play a post-flop game. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on board texture.

On a flop of 8-3-3, I have top pair (eights) with a decent kicker (Ten). Since I am in the Big Blind and was the caller pre-flop, standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This allows Minni to continue with her wide range, including bluffs and semi-bluffs. My hand is strong enough to call a bet on this street, and checking keeps the pot manageable while I evaluate Minni's aggression.

Heads-up on a dry, paired flop (8c 3d 3c). My hand 9c-8d is top pair (pair of eights) with a decent 9-kicker — a strong, value-heavy holding in this spot versus Flash’s wide cold-call range. There are few plausible strong draws on this board (some two-club combos can have a flush draw, and very rarely backdoor straights), so betting for value is the correct play rather than checking and allowing free cards. I’ll size to ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) to extract value from worse pairs, single 8 combos and many Ax/Kx/Qx hands that will call, while still leaving room to fold to a large check-raise/all-in that would strongly indicate a set/full house (33, 88, or similar). Bet 400 to build the pot and deny equity to marginal hands.

On a flop of 8c-3d-3c, I have top pair (eights) with a Ten kicker. Minni's c-bet of 400 into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet on a board where the pre-flop aggressor often has the range advantage, but my hand is too strong to fold. I will call to see the turn and keep the pot manageable, as raising might fold out her bluffs while only getting called by better hands like overpairs or trips.

On the turn, the Nine of Diamonds appears, which is an overcard to my pair of Eights. In heads-up play, as the Big Blind, I act first on every post-flop street. My pair of Eights with a Ten kicker still has significant showdown value, but it is not a hand I want to build a massive pot with given the board texture. I will check here to see how Minni reacts. Checking allows her to continue bluffing with overcards or check back with marginal hands. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the size, but for now, checking is the most solid play to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand.

I hold 9♣8♦ on 8♣–3♦–3♣–9♦: I’ve improved to two pair (9s and 8s). Heads-up after my flop c-bet (400) was called, Flash’s calling range is wide (weaker 8x, single pairs, underpairs, some clubs/diamond draw combos) but only a small portion of that range currently beats me (full houses/quads: 33, 88, 99). I also block some of those combos by holding a 9 and an 8. With two pair in position I should be extracting value rather than pot-controlling. The turn has few dangerous new draws, so a value bet that charges worse pairs and weaker 8/9 combinations is appropriate. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot to get called by worse hands and deny free rivers to equity hands — sizing to 1000 chips into the 1400 pot. If Flash check-raises big/all-in I’ll treat it as a strong full-house/quads candidate and re-evaluate (likely fold to large overbets), but given the range that called the flop this is the best line to maximize value now.

On the turn, the 9d is an overcard to my pair of Eights. Minni has fired a second barrel, this time for 1000 into a 1400 pot. This sizing is quite aggressive and polarizing. Given my relatively short stack (approximately 24.5 BBs remaining), calling this bet would effectively commit nearly half of my remaining chips to a pot where I only have a bluff-catcher. Minni has shown a history of heavy aggression and large betting across previous hands. While I have top pair from the flop, the Nine makes my hand much more vulnerable to her range (A9, K9, or even 99). Folding here preserves my stack and allows me to find a better opportunity to commit my chips, rather than potentially being forced into a pot-committed situation on the river with a marginal hand.

With a stack of only 24 big blinds and a very weak hand like Queen-Deuce offsuit, it is best to avoid engaging with a chip leader who has shown significant aggression. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand to commit my remaining chips.

Heads-up on the button (also the small blind). 9♣2♠ is garbage, but in heads-up position and with a large stack advantage it's +EV to open-raise wide to seize the initiative and apply pressure to a short-stacked BB. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) — big enough to get folds from weaker holdings and not so large that I risk too much vs a shove. Plan: if he 3-bets big or shoves, fold this hand; if he calls, proceed cautiously post-flop using position to continuation-bet when favorable but fold to strong resistance without improvement.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (7h 5s). Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call, my stack is relatively short (23 BB), and playing a weak, non-connected, non-suited hand out of position against a massive stack is likely to lead to losing more chips in the post-flop streets. It's better to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 7d 4h, which is a very weak hand. The pot is currently 150 and it costs me 50 to call, but playing this hand out of position against a massive stack like Minni with such weak holdings is not profitable in the long run. Folding is the safest play to conserve my remaining chips.

Heads-up on the button with J♥3♣ is a marginal holding, but in heads-up play the button should be opening wide to take advantage of position and steal the blinds. The pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 to call; instead I’ll raise to the minimum allowed (200 total) to take the initiative, apply pressure to Flash’s wide BB defending range, and keep the pot manageable. The 200 size (I add 150 more on top of my posted 50) gives good fold equity against weaker holdings while leaving room to fold to a large shove if Flash jams. If he calls I have position to play post‑flop; if he 3-bets I can re-evaluate against his range and stack size.

In Hand #91, I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Seven of Hearts (T7o). The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 200, which means it is 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300. My stack is relatively short (21.5 Big Blinds) compared to Minni's massive stack. While T7o is not a strong hand, getting 3:1 pot odds in a heads-up situation makes it a marginal call to see a flop. However, given that Minni has been quite aggressive and my stack is low, I should look for better spots to commit chips, but for only 100 more, I will defend the blind this time to see if I can hit the board.

In Hand #91, I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Seven of Hearts. The flop is Eight of Hearts, Six of Spades, and Ace of Diamonds. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Nine). Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and, with an Ace on the board, it is likely this board hits her range better than mine. I will check to see how she proceeds.

I missed the flop entirely with J♥3♣ on A♦8♥6♠ — no pair and no realistic draw. In heads‑up play Flash’s defending range to my preflop raise will include many A‑x hands and pairs that connect with this board, so a c‑bet as a pure bluff is thin and will often be called or check‑raised. I want to control the pot and use my positional advantage to gather more information on the turn (I can still bluff on a favorable card or fold to strong aggression). Checking keeps the pot small (400) and preserves my deep stack against a shortish opponent who can shove; I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

In Hand #91, I have improved to second pair (Tens) on the turn. The board is 8h-6s-Ad-Th. Pre-flop, Minni (the button) raised and I called. On the flop, both of us checked. Since Minni checked back the flop, it is less likely she has a strong Ace, although she could be slow-playing. With a medium-strength hand like second pair, checking is a solid option to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from an aggressive player like Minni. If she bets, I have enough equity to call at least one street. I will check to see her reaction.

I missed both the flop and turn with J♥3♣ on A♦‑8♥‑6♠‑T♥ — no pair and no realistic draw. Flush is impossible (with one heart in my hand and two on board I can only reach four hearts by the river), and my only clean outs are the remaining three J’s and three 3’s (≈6 outs, ~12–13% to hit). The pot is only 400 and Flash still has 2,050 behind; a turn probe‑bet is thin — many hands in his defending range (Ax, Tx, middle pairs, pocket pairs) will call a small bet and a larger bet just creates tough shove/fold decisions. I prefer to keep the pot small and see the river: if I pair on the river I can extract value, if it bricks I can evaluate a targeted bluff only if the card credibly improves my story or he shows weakness. Checking preserves these options and minimizes risk with a weak holding, so I check.

In Hand #91, the board has completed as 8h-6s-Ad-Th-7d. I hold Ten of Diamonds and Seven of Hearts, giving me two pair (Tens and Sevens). The pre-flop aggressor (Minni) checked both the flop and the turn, which indicates a range consisting of either weak showdown value hands (like a weak Ace or a small pair) or complete air.
