With the river giving me two pair, I have a very strong hand relative to the action so far. I will bet for value, hoping to get called by a single pair like a Ten or a weak Ace that checked back earlier. A bet of 250 into a 400 pot is a standard sizing to entice a call from marginal hands.

River: board 8♥ 6♠ A♦ T♥ 7♦. My hand J♥3♣ — didn’t pair and there are no straights or flushes that make my hand credible. Flash called preflop and passively checked flop and turn, then makes a small river stab of 250 into a 650 pot. Calling 250 only wins me 900 total, so I need ~250/900 ≈ 27.8% equity to break even. On the river I have zero card outs — I only beat pure bluffs. There is no strong read here that Flash bluffs this line ≥28% of the time; his line is at least as consistent with thin value (A‑x, T‑x, or small pairs) or a blocking/value probe. A raise as a bluff is thin as well — many hands in his range will call a raise and it’s too high-variance to risk now. With a large effective stack and plenty of hands left in the session, preserving chips for better spots is correct. Fold.

In a heads-up situation from the Small Blind/Button, Ace-Deuce offsuit is a standard hand to open or call with. Since the pot is small and I have a relatively short stack compared to Minni's massive stack, I will raise to take control of the hand and apply pressure. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Heads-up with Q♣J♦ — a solid, playable hand vs a button open. Flash opened to 300 (3bb); I’m in the big blind so to call I need +200 (total committed 300). Calling here gives ~2:1 pot odds (need ~33% equity), and QJo has decent equity vs a standard button opening range. I prefer to call rather than make a thin 3-bet because I’m out of position and the effective stack is only ~21bb: a larger 3-bet either commits stacks or turns the hand into a shove/fold situation where QJo is vulnerable. Calling keeps weaker hands in, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me realize equity or pick spots on the flop. Plan: evaluate flop — continue with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression when missed.

Flop: A♠ K♠ 4♦; I hold Qc Jd and completely missed. No spade or real straight draw — only 6 outs to pair (≈24% to hit by river) and pairing Q/J will often still be second-best versus an Ace or King. Flash opened preflop so his c-bet range includes lots of A/K and strong broadways. With nothing and out of position I want pot control and information: check and see what he does. If he makes a small c-bet I can call to realize equity; I will fold to significant aggression and only continue strongly if I improve or pick up a favorable turn to bluff-raise against his short stack.

I have top pair with an Ace of Clubs. The board shows Ace of Spades, King of Spades, and Four of Diamonds. While my kicker is weak (2), top pair on this board is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni checked to me, which often signifies weakness or a trap. I want to bet for value and to protect my hand against potential spade draws. A bet of 300 (half pot) is standard and helps define where I am in the hand.

Heads-up on A♠ K♠ 4♦ with Q♣J♦ — I completely missed and have no spade backdoor. Flash (the preflop raiser) made a small c‑bet of 300 into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot), giving me ~4:1 pot odds to call. Against a button open his flop range is wide and includes many bluffs and Kx/Ax hands; my hand has enough showdown equity to justify a call and keep the pot under control out of position. I don’t want to raise — I have poor blockers, no draw, and a raise could turn into an ill-advised commit versus his short stack. Plan: call 300 to see the turn, fold to significant raise/shove unless I pair or pick up a strong draw.

Turn: A♠ K♠ 4♦ 2♠. I have Qc Jd — completely missed, no spade, no backdoor. Pot 1,200, opponent (Flash) effective ~1,850. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop, so his range is heavy with A/K and broadways plus some bluffs. The 2♠ slightly changes texture (three spades on board) but I have no spade and the only way villain has a flush is holding two spades (relatively uncommon). I only have ~6 outs to pair by the river (~13%), and pairing will often still be second-best to A/K. With no showdown value or meaningful draw, I don’t want to build the pot out of position or turn this into a costly bluff. Best line is to check and fold to any significant bet — realize equity cheaply and re-evaluate on the river if I pair or pick up actionable information. Therefore: check.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Deuces), but the Deuce of Spades also puts a possible flush on the board. Since I do not hold a spade in my hand, I am vulnerable to any single spade Minni might have. With a relatively short stack of 1250 remaining and a pot of 1200, checking here is a prudent pot-control move. It allows me to see the river for free and decide how to proceed based on the final card and Minni's subsequent action, rather than potentially committing my entire stack against a completed flush.

Board: A♠ K♠ 4♦ 2♠ T♠. I hold Q♣ J♦ — no spade, no pair, zero river outs. Flash opened and c‑bet the flop, then both checked turn; the river spade is scary because with four spades on board any single spade in his hand now makes a flush. Rough estimate: a large fraction of his preflop/c‑betting range contains at least one spade (I’d put it in the 30–40% area), plus many Ax/Kx and broadway combos that won’t fold to aggression. I have almost no showdown value and I don’t block spades, so bluffing here requires fold equity I likely don’t have (for example a 400 bet needs >25% folds to break even, and I expect less given his line). With a healthy overall stack and many hands left, preserving chips is best. Plan: check and concede to a river bet (fold to meaningful action); check behind only if he shows weakness. Action: check.

The board has four spades on it, meaning any single spade in Minni's hand makes a flush. I have two pair (Aces and Deuces) but no spade. Minni checked the river, which could be a trap with a flush or simply a sign of a weak hand like a King or a lower pair that can't call a bet. Given that I have showdown value but lose to every single spade, betting is extremely risky as it only gets called by better hands. Checking back is the most profitable and safe play to realize my showdown value.

Heads-up on the button with 3♣2♥ — a very weak hand but huge positional advantage and a massive stack edge (38,100 vs 1,750). In heads-up play I should open very wide from the button; a small min-raise applies pressure to the short-stacked BB and often wins the pot outright or isolates to a favourable postflop spot. I’ll raise to the minimum (200 total) to take the initiative. If flashed 3-bets or shoves I’ll evaluate based on sizing — an all-in from him is a relatively small portion of my stack so I can call in marginal spots, but postflop I’ll c-bet favorable flops and abandon the hand on heavy resistance given my weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 400), this is a standard defend. My stack is relatively short (17.5 BB), so I need to be cautious post-flop, but Jack-Nine has enough playability to see a flop against a wide button opening range.

On the flop of Ac 8s 9c, I have middle pair (nines). Minni has checked back. While my hand has some value, it is vulnerable to many turn cards (any Club, Ten, Queen, King, or Ace). However, by checking, I am following a standard pot-control line with a medium-strength hand. I will check to see how the turn develops and evaluate if Minni bets.

I opened very wide from the button preflop and Flash (very short-stacked ~1,650) just called. Flop A♣ 8♠ 9♣ with my 3♣2♥ is essentially air — I have no made hand and only runner-runner possibilities; my single club plus two clubs on board is not a one-card flush draw. Heads-up I should continuation-bet a high frequency to take advantage of my initiative and the caller’s wide, often missed range. Pot is 400, so a ~1/3 pot c-bet (150) applies pressure while keeping risk controlled against a short stack — if Flash shoves I’ll fold this junk; if he calls I can still fold to heavy turn action or continue only on favorable runouts. Bet 150 to steal the pot or define his holdings.

I have middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. Minni has made a small bet of 150 into a 400-chip pot. Given the pot odds and the likelihood that Minni could be betting a wide range of hands from the button, my hand is too strong to fold. I will call the bet to see a turn card and re-evaluate the strength of my hand based on the next card and Minni's subsequent action.

I have middle pair (Nines) on a board with an Ace. On the turn (4s), the board texture hasn't changed much. My goal is to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown. I will check to see Minni's reaction; if she checks back, I likely have the best hand. If she bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking is the most standard play with a medium-strength hand.

Heads-up vs a very short-stacked Flash (≈1,500). I have virtually no showdown value with 3♣2♥ (only a wheel draw to a 5 and lonely pair/straight outs), but I have significant fold equity as the aggressor and a huge stack advantage. Flash checked the turn after calling my flop c-bet, which often indicates a weak/medium holding that can fold to continued pressure. I want to apply pressure to take the 700-chip pot now while keeping my risk small if he jams. I’ll make a modest turn double-barrel — bet 300 (≈40% pot). This size represents a strong hand, keeps the pot controllable, and still forces many worse hands to fold. If Flash shoves big (all-in), I’ll respect the heavy action and fold this junk; if he calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the river (or check behind to realize equity if it’s safe).

I have middle pair (Nines) on an Ace-high board. Minni's betting pattern (small bets on both the flop and turn) suggests she could have a weak Ace, a draw, or is simply continuation betting from the button. Given the pot odds (300 to win a total pot of 1300), I am getting a good price to call and see a river. My hand still has showdown value against many of her potential bluffs or semi-bluffs. I will call to evaluate the river action.

On the river, the board has paired the Ace, giving me two pair (Aces and Nines). I have been check-calling the flop and turn with middle pair. My hand has decent showdown value, but it is unlikely to be called by many worse hands if I bet. By checking, I allow Minni to bluff with missed draws (like clubs or straight draws) or check back weaker made hands. If she bets, I will evaluate based on sizing, but given the pot odds and my stack size, I am likely looking for a showdown.

I opened ultra-wide and applied pressure (200 pre, 150 flop, 300 turn). Flash called both streets, showing a calling-down tendency with a short stack (~1,200). The river pairs the board with a second Ace (Ac...Ad) and I hold absolute air (3♣2♥) — no pair, no real river equity. Flash checked the river; if he held an Ace he'd be incentivized to shove or raise for value rather than just check, so his check is more consistent with medium pairs, missed draws or pot-control. Betting here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity against a player who’s already demonstrated willingness to call, and any meaningful bet simply creates a shove decision I must fold to. Against this opponent and with this hand, the +EV play is to check behind, concede the pot if he shows down, and preserve my large stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 6s. This is a very weak hand. Given that I have a significantly smaller stack (2,450) compared to Minni (37,400), I should avoid playing marginal or weak hands out of position. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play.

Heads-up on the button with 2♥7♦ — essentially the weakest starting hand. Pot is 150 and I must call 50 to see a flop; min-raise is to 200. Flash (BB) has a short (~23BB) but aggressive stack and has shown frequent preflop raises — a small raise here often gets met by a shove or a sticky defense. Calling 50 to play postflop with 2-7o is a losing proposition long-term (very poor equity and little fold equity), and raising to try to steal risks getting isolated into an all‑in with a dominated holding. Preserve my deep stack and avoid unnecessary variance — fold and wait for a better spot to exploit the short stack or play position with real equity.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong starting hand. I have a stack of 2450 (24.5 BB), and a standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind.

Call 300. In heads-up vs a 3x open the BTN’s range is very wide; 9♣5♦ has reasonable equity and the pot odds are attractive (I must call 200 more to win ~600, ~33% break‑even). I’m out of position postflop so I won’t overcommit with a marginal hand, but calling keeps the pot contested against a looser opener and lets me realize equity or fold to heavy action if I miss. I’ll proceed cautiously on the flop—look for a pair or strong draw to continue, otherwise check/fold to significant pressure.

I have 9♣5♦ on Q♦-3♥-7♦: no pair, no real draw (only one diamond → only remote backdoor possibilities). Out of position versus a wide 3x opener, I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. Check to control the pot and concede to a standard continuation bet — I’ll fold to meaningful pressure and only proceed if Flash gives me a free card or posts an extremely small probe that’s clearly a bluff. This preserves my stack and lets me wait for a better spot in heads‑up play.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit and was the pre-flop aggressor. The flop of Qd-3h-7d is relatively dry. While the Queen likely favors the big blind's defending range, I should continue with a standard continuation bet of 50% pot to represent a pair or a strong draw and to take down the pot immediately if Minni missed. Given my relatively short stack of 22 BB, I want to maintain pressure but stay mindful of pot control.

Fold. On Q♦‑3♥‑7♦ with 9♣5♦ I have no pair and essentially no realistic draw (only a runner‑runner diamond flush). Flash’s half‑pot c‑bet of 300 into the 900 pot gives me 25% pot‑odds (300/(900+300)=25%). My chance to pair by the river (6 outs: any 9 or 5) is only about 24.1%, and the runner‑runner flush chance is ≈4.2% — overall equity is at or below the break‑even threshold against a standard BTN c‑bet range that includes many Qx, diamonds and value hands. I’m out of position and will face tough decisions on later streets if I call. With a healthy 37,200 stack and many hands left, folding this marginal holding is the +EV play rather than investing more chips or attempting an OOP bluff‑raise.

Heads-up on the button with 8h9h — a strong, playable suited connector with positional advantage. I should not limp; I want to seize initiative, get fold equity against a relatively short 27BB BB, and build a pot I can play in position. Min raise is 200, so I open to 3x the BB (300 total) — big enough to apply pressure and discourage light defenders, but not so large as to overcommit if Flash 3-bets. If Flash folds I scoop the 150 pot. If he calls I’ll play postflop in position with a hand that flops well. If he 3-bets or shoves I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his range (call versus a very wide shove; fold to a polarized, strong 3-bet). Overall raising to 300 maximizes value and fold equity in this heads-up spot.

I am holding Ace-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind. In a heads-up situation, this is a strong starting hand. The opponent, Minni, has been active and raising frequently from the button. While I could 3-bet here, my stack is relatively short at 27 big blinds. Calling allows me to see a flop and keep the pot manageable, although I will be playing out of position for the rest of the hand.

On this flop, I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw. The board is relatively dry (2c-6d-Jc). Since I am the Big Blind and was the caller pre-flop, I will check to the aggressor, Minni, to see how she reacts to this texture before deciding whether to continue.

I raised preflop and have position; flop Jc-2c-6d misses my 8h9h but as the preflop aggressor in heads-up I can leverage initiative. Flash in the BB has a short ~25BB effective stack and will defend with a wide range, but many holdings (overcards, small pairs, some clubs) will fold to a respectable c-bet. I want to extract folds and keep the pot manageable if called. Bet sizing: ~50% pot is standard here — enough fold equity to win the pot outright but small enough that I can comfortably fold to a large shove with this marginal hand. Plan: bet 300 to take it down now; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with improvement or strong reads; if he check-raises/shoves I will fold this hand.

I have Ace-high (Ace-Eight) on a relatively dry board of Jack-Six-Deuce. While Minni's continuation bet could represent a Jack or a pocket pair, her range from the button is wide and includes many hands that I currently beat or have decent equity against. Given the pot odds (calling 300 into a 900 pot) and the fact that Ace-high often has showdown value in heads-up play, I will call this bet to see how the turn develops.

On the turn 5h, the board remains relatively safe, although there are some straight draws now possible (like 43). As the caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My Ace-high still has some showdown value against her wide button range, but I want to see if she continues her aggression before committing more of my remaining 22 big blinds to the pot.

Check. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop, but the 5♥ on the turn is a blank for my 8♥9♥ — I have no pair and only a 7→straight (4 outs) to improve on the river (~4/46 ≈ 8.7%). The pot is 1,200 and Flash’s effective stack is ~2,200, so a turn bet large enough to fold out his weaker holdings also risks a shove or call from hands (Jx, small/mid pairs) that I’m crushed against. Calling or folding to a shove with only ~9% equity is long-run losing; betting small to try to fold him here is thin because he’ll often call or shove. Checking controls the pot, realizes equity for cheap, and keeps river bluffing / value options available — if he shows weakness on the river I can bluff or take a free card to hit my 7; if he fires a large bet I can fold without committing more chips.

On the river, the board is 2c-6d-Jc-5h-Qh. My hand is Ace-high (Ace-Eight). Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a strong holding, such as a high Jack or a pocket pair. The Queen on the river is an overcard, which could be a scare card, but it's more likely she has a weak pair or another Ace-high hand. Ace-high has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot after the turn goes check-check. Betting wouldn't likely get better hands to fold or worse hands to call, so checking to reach showdown or evaluate her reaction is the most prudent play.

I c-bet the flop and got called, then checked the turn. Flash’s turn+river checks indicate weakness — a range with many small pairs, missed club draws and some Jx. The Q♥ on the river is a perfect scare card: I can credibly represent Qx (or a turned/rivered two-pair line) after my earlier actions. I’ll apply pressure with a ~2/3-pot river bluff to fold out the weaker part of his calling range while not risking too large a portion of my huge stack if he decides to jam. Assuming my showdown equity vs his calling range is ~20%, a ~66% pot bet needs roughly a ~33% fold rate to be +EV; given his passive line I expect at least that. If he raises/shoves I’ll fold — I have very little showdown equity — but if he folds I win the 1,200 pot outright. Betting 800 balances fold equity and risk here.

I am currently holding Ace-high (Ace-Eight). On the river, the board is Jack-Six-Deuce-Five-Queen. Minni checked the turn, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a check-back with air. However, her 2/3 pot bet (800 into 1200) on the river Queen is polarized. While she could be bluffing after I showed weakness by checking twice, the Queen is a very likely card in her button opening range (like KQ, QJ, or AQ). Given my relatively short stack of 2200, calling 800 with only Ace-high is a high-risk move that could leave me with very few chips. Based on Minni's previous aggressive play and the likelihood that she has at least a pair, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Q5o. This is a weak starting hand. Minni has a significantly larger stack and has been playing aggressively. Given the stack disadvantage and the weak hand, folding is the most prudent strategy to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with T5s (10♦5♦). This is a playable, suited, position-first hand so I should be aggressive rather than limp. Flash is effectively short (~20BB) so a standard heads-up open-size will apply pressure and fold out a lot of his wide BB defense while keeping the pot manageable if he shoves. I’ll raise to 3BB (300 total) to seize the initiative and play postflop in position; if he 3-bets all-in I’ll fold marginal holdings like this, but if he calls I have decent playability with flush/straight potential and position advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh6s. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. My stack is relatively short at 20.5 BBs. While the pot odds to call are 2:1, Q6o is a weak hand to play out of position against a big stack who has shown significant aggression. I prefer to wait for a stronger hand or a better spot to commit my remaining chips rather than defending with a marginal hand that often leads to difficult post-flop situations.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play or from the small blind. Although my stack is relatively short (20 BB) compared to Minni's massive stack, I should still play aggressively to build my chips back up. I will raise to the standard 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately or see a flop with the initiative.

Flash opened to 300 from the button and I’m in the BB with A♠7♥. Heads-up A7o is a strong hand against a wide SB opening range. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (300 total) — that’s 200 to win 600, so I need ~33% equity, which A7o comfortably has vs a typical opener. I’m out of position postflop, so I prefer to keep the pot controllable and see a flop rather than make a small 3-bet (to 500) that gains little and could induce a shove from his short stack. Plan: call, evaluate the flop and use my stack/hand to apply pressure on favorable boards.

Flop: A♣8♦3♥? (board A♥ 8♦ 3♥) — I have A♠7♥: top pair with a mediocre kicker, out of position. Flash is short (≈17.5bb) and opened wide from the button, so his range contains a lot of Ax, broadways and pocket pairs — some of which (A8, A3, 88, 33 and A with a better kicker) already beat me, but many worse A-x and middle pairs remain. I called preflop to keep the pot controllable; I want to stick to that plan on this texture. Betting OOP risks getting raised/3-bet jammed by a short stack and bloating the pot when I’m vulnerable to many better A-x combos. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets him bluff-c-bet worse hands, and allows me to call small c-bets and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: check now, call reasonable flop bets for value, and re-evaluate (raise/call/fold) depending on sizing and turn card.

I have middle pair (8s) with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. Checking back after Minni checks is a standard pot-control move. It allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot small with a hand that has showdown value but is likely behind any Ace in her range. By checking, I also avoid being forced to fold to a check-raise while keeping her bluffs in for later streets.

I have top pair A with a weak kicker (A♠7♥) on A‑8‑3‑9 rainbow. Heads‑up vs a short, wide opener (~17.5bb) who checked the flop, the goal is to extract value from worse A‑x and small pairs while keeping the pot controlled. Pot is 600 — a half‑pot turn bet of 300 charges his worse Ax and single pairs, denies a free river, and makes any shove by him more expensive to profitably bluff. If he jams his 1750 all‑in over my 300, the extra I’d need to call is 1450 and the final pot would be ~4100, so I’d need ~35% equity to call — a realistic threshold for calling some shoves but I’ll fold to clearly polarized shove ranges (two pair/sets) and re‑evaluate if he simply calls. Betting 300 strikes a balance between extracting value and avoiding bloating the pot with a marginal kicker out of position.

I have second pair (8s) with a decent kicker on an Ace-high board. After checking back the flop to keep the pot small and maintain my hand's value, Minni has led out for half the pot on the turn. In heads-up play, second pair is too strong to fold to a single probe bet, especially since my check on the flop might have induced a bluff. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (like an Ace), so calling is the best way to keep the pot manageable and reach a showdown or evaluate the river.

Board: 8♦ 3♥ A♥ 9♠ Q♠. I hold A♠7♥ — top pair with a weak kicker. Flash opened wide from the button and is short (≈1450), checked the flop and called my modest turn bet, which puts him on a fairly wide calling range of Ax, second/third pairs (88, 99, 77, etc.) and some showdown-ish broadways. Some hands clearly beat me (A9, A8, A3, AQ, sets), but a large portion of his range is worse A-x and single pairs that will call a value bet.

Goal on the river: extract value from worse A-x and medium pairs without bloating the pot against the hands that dominate me. Checking hands the showdown for free and misses value from many worse Aces/pairs; a small-to-medium value bet accomplishes both value extraction and keeps a jam by him informative.

Sizing: Pot = 1200. I will bet ~½ pot (600). This size gets called by worse A-x and middle pairs, leaves Flash ~850 behind (so a shove over my 600 would commit him and be polarized). If he jams, the extra call required is 850 to win a ~4100 pot — about a 21% equity requirement. I’ll fold to a polarized shove that looks like two pair/sets, but call if his shove range appears very wide/bluffy. If he simply calls my 600, I expect to have good showdown equity and to collect extra value.

Action: bet 600 to extract value and keep options against a short, wide opponent.

On the river, the board is EIGHT OF DIAMONDS (8d), TREY OF HEARTS (3h), ACE OF HEARTS (Ah), NINE OF SPADES (9s), QUEEN OF SPADES (Qs). I have a pair of eights (8s Tc). Minni has led out for 600 into a pot of 1200, making the total pot 1800. My check on the flop capped my range, and the board has since developed two overcards to my pair (the 9 and the Q) in addition to the Ace already present. While my pair of eights had some value on the turn, it has now become the third or fourth best possible pair. Minni's lead on the turn followed by a half-pot bet on the river suggests a value hand like a weak Ace, a nine that she turned, or a queen she hit on the river. Given my stack size of 1450, calling 600 represents nearly 40% of my remaining chips. I am better off folding and preserving my 14.5 big blinds to find a more favorable spot rather than calling with a marginal hand that only beats a pure bluff.

Heads-up in the SB with 2♠9♦ — a very weak hand, but the price to see a flop is cheap. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 (100 total committed) which gives me ~25% break-even equity. Against a wide heads-up defense this hand has enough equity to justify a call rather than risking a bigger raise that will often induce a shove from the short-stacked BB (1350). Plan: call to see the flop and play postflop cautiously — fold to large aggression or a shove if the action and board strongly favor villain; continue only with clear equity (paired 9/2, good draws) or if I can take it down with a well-timed bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 13.5 big blinds holding 7h 5h. Minni has limped from the Small Blind. Given my relatively short stack, I want to see a flop as cheaply as possible to realize the equity of my suited connectors. Raising here would commit a significant portion of my stack and potentially force me to play a large pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking allows me to see a free flop.

On this flop of 3s-2c-Qs, I have missed completely with my 7h5h. Minni has checked to me. While I could attempt to bluff, my stack is quite short at 13.5 big blinds, and Minni has been playing aggressively. Checking back costs me nothing and allows me to see a turn card for free, potentially picking up a straight draw or a pair that could allow me to continue later.

Heads-up on a wet-ish Qs 3s 2c flop I’ve hit bottom pair with 2♠9♦ (pair of deuces) and I also carry a spade for backdoor possibilities. Pot is only 200 and Flash checked, so his range is wide — lots of bluffs, spade cards and weaker pairs. A small continuation bet will: (1) extract value from worse hands and draws, (2) deny free cards to spade/straight backdoors, and (3) give me information on his strength. I size to ~1/2 pot (100) — small enough to get called by worse and by draws, but large enough to charge equity and keep the pot controllable. Plan after this bet: fold to a large raise/shove (he’s representing a hand that dominates my pair), call a modest raise and re-evaluate on the turn depending on the card (fold if heavy aggression and overcards arrive, continue for value if checked back or I improve).

I am in the Big Blind with 13.5 big blinds, holding 7h 5h. After checking pre-flop in a limped pot, the flop is 3s-2c-Qs. Minni has bet 100 into a 200-chip pot. I have completely missed the board, possessing no pairs, no straight draws, and no significant flush draws. Given my short stack and the lack of equity, there is no reason to continue in this hand. I will fold and preserve my chips for a better opportunity.

In Hand #102, I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Th3h. My stack is very short (1300 chips), while Minni has a massive lead (38550 chips). Ten-Trey suited is a marginal hand, but given the 2:1 pot odds to call (50 more into 150) and the fact that I'm playing heads-up, I should at least see a flop to capitalize on its potential flush/straight connectivity. However, since my stack is so shallow, I need to be careful about committing too much without a strong post-flop connection. I will call the small blind to see a flop.

Heads-up vs a limp-complete from the small blind. Kc7c is playable heads-up (king blocker + suited), and Flash’s limp indicates a wide, often weak range. I want to take the initiative and either win the pot preflop or play the flop in position. A standard raise to 300 gives useful fold equity against a wide calling range, keeps the size manageable vs his 1,250 stack, and sets me up to C‑bet favorable flops or control the pot when I miss. This is an exploitative, initiative-taking play to maximize EV in a heads-up spot.

With a stack of only 12.5 BBs, I am in a precarious position. Minni has a massive chip lead and is applying pressure. Facing a 3x raise pre-flop with Ten-Trey suited, the hand is simply too weak to justify calling away nearly 20% of my remaining stack. While suited hands have some value in heads-up play, the low rank of the cards makes it likely I'm dominated or will be forced to fold on most flops. I'll fold and wait for a more premium hand to commit my chips.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) I should be opening a wide range. 6♣7♥ is a playable, connected hand that performs well post-flop in position. The pot is 150 and calling 50 surrenders initiative; a small raise to the 2xBB minimum (200 total) puts pressure on the BB, often wins the pot outright, and keeps the pot manageable if Flash defends or shoves (he only has ~11.5bb). I’ll raise to seize initiative and play the flop in position; if he jams I’ll re-evaluate facing a short-stack shove but prefer to apply pressure here.

I have King of Spades and Queen of Diamonds, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni raised to 200 from the Small Blind, and I am in the Big Blind. With a stack of only 1150 (about 11.5 Big Blinds) and such a strong hand, this is a clear spot to either call or raise. Given my short stack relative to the blinds, I want to maximize value or simply take the pot down now. A 3-bet to 600 or an all-in shove are both viable. Since I have nearly 1/3 of my stack committed if I 3-bet to 600, shoving all-in is the most effective way to realize equity and put pressure on the opponent.

I opened to 2xBB with 6♣7♥ (fine heads‑up), but Flash 3‑bet to 1,150 — a very large, polarizing size. The pot is 1,350 and I must call 950 more (calling would make the pot ~2,300), so I need ≈41% equity to break even. 6‑7 offsuit has poor equity vs a value‑heavy 3‑bet (pairs and A/K broadways) and is frequently dominated; a small 4‑bet or shove is unnecessary and exploitable with this hand. With position notwithstanding, I don’t want to bloat a pot out of position with a speculative offsuit connector against a strong sizing — fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 7s4s. This is a speculative suited hand. In a heads-up situation with a short stack of 1,400 chips (14 big blinds), I have limited room for post-flop play. Minni has a massive stack and has been aggressive. However, calling the extra 50 to see a flop with a suited connector-type hand is acceptable to see if I hit a monster, though folding is also reasonable given my stack depth. I will call the 50 to see a flop, keeping my options open for a shove or fold depending on the board.

Heads-up with the button (Flash) limping into 50 and pot already 200. I have 6♣5♣ — a strong heads-up, suited connector that plays well postflop and has good equity vs a wide limp. Flash is short (≈1350), so aggression will get folds or isolate a wide calling range. I’m out of position postflop, so I prefer to take the initiative preflop: raise to isolate and extract fold equity while keeping the pot size reasonable. A raise to 300 total (3bb) is a standard isolation size — big enough to discourage too many speculative hands and win the pot outright often, small enough to avoid bloating the pot or forcing an automatic all‑in every time. If he shoves, his range will be wide and my hand has decent equity and pot odds to call; if he calls we play a favorable heads‑up pot with initiative. Therefore: raise to 300.

I have a short stack of 1,350 chips (13.5 big blinds). Minni has raised to 300, requiring me to call an additional 200. While 7s4s has some potential, calling away 15% of my stack with a weak 7-high hand is suboptimal against a massive, aggressive stack. I will fold and wait for a more favorable hand to commit my chips.

Heads-up on the button with 7♠5♦ — a marginal hand, but against a lone, short-stacked big blind this is a clear open/pressure spot. Pot is 150 and I only have to put in 50 to see a flop, but opening to the minimum (200 total) gives me fold equity, keeps the pot small if I get called, and exploits Flash’s short stack (1250 ≈ 12.5bb). If he folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I keep position postflop and can play a cheap pot. If he shoves, after a 200 raise I’d need to call ~1050 to win ~2500 (≈42% break-even equity) — versus a short-stack shove range my hand has reasonable equity, so calling shoves is often acceptable. Raise to 200 to apply pressure while limiting risk.

I have been dealt a very strong hand for heads-up play, a pocket pair of Eights. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button. Given the stack sizes, I only have 1250 chips remaining (12.5 big blinds). This is a classic 'push or fold' situation, and with pocket eights, it is a mandatory shove. I want to maximize my double-up potential or take down the pot pre-flop. I will raise all-in.

We're heads-up; I opened to 200 and Flash shoved/3-bet to 1250. Pot is 1450 and I must put in 1050 more to call (1250 total). If I call the pot will be ~2500, so my break-even equity is 1050/2500 ≈ 42%. With Flash effectively short-stacked this is very often a wide shove (pairs, many Ax/Kx and bluffs), and 7♠5♦ has borderline but acceptable equity against a wide short-stack shove. The amount to risk (1050) is small relative to my 38,450 stack, while the EV of folding vs a wide shove is negative. Reraising is pointless here (would only extract ~100 more and create side-pot awkwardness). Call and take my chances — this is a correct exploitative call vs a short-stack shove.

I have an overpair (88) on a very dry board (3-5-5). Given that I only have 100 chips remaining and the pot is already 2500, I am fully committed to this hand. I will bet my remaining 100 chips to put myself all-in.

Flop: 3♦ 5♠ 5♥ — I have 5♦ for trips. The pot is ~2,600 and Flash has put out a tiny bet (100). Calling 100 to win ~2,700 is a no-brainer (break-even <4%). I’m far ahead of basically any reasonable all‑in/shove range here, folding would be a mistake, and raising is pointless because Flash is effectively out of chips and can’t call more. I’ll call the 100 and let my trips realize value on later streets while preserving my deep stack for future spots.
